nodes.label: Data backup overview
nodes.heading: Data backup overview
nodes.snippet:
Data backup overview

Data backup is available on request. Contact Kissflow support to have it enabled for your account.

Data backup in Kissflow safeguards your organization's data against loss and security incidents, and helps meet audit and compliance requirements. Super admins can export account data to a cloud storage destination or a downloadable link, keeping it accessible for reading, archiving, and analysis outside of Kissflow.

media.alt: Introducing Data Backup in Kissflow

nodes.label: Data backup overview
nodes.heading: Storage destinations
nodes.snippet:
When you configure a backup, you choose where the exported files go:

- Google Drive
- Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3)
- Google Cloud Storage (GCS)
- Kissflow backup — no external account needed; Kissflow emails you a downloadable link once the backup completes.

nodes.label: Data backup overview
nodes.heading: What gets backed up
nodes.snippet:
Configuring a backup involves authenticating your chosen storage destination, setting backup file preferences (like the ZIP split size and optional encryption), and selecting which categories of data to include:

- User and group data — all account user and group records.
- Transactional data — flow item details such as status, comments, and assignees, across Processes, Boards, Datasets, Forms, and Projects.
- Audit log data — roughly the last year of account- and flow-level audit activity.
- Attachments — files attached to items in processes, boards, datasets, and other apps. Attachments are packaged alongside their records inside the transactional data folder, not as a separate output — deselecting this category excludes attachment files from that folder while still including the records themselves.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Data backup configuration page showing selectable data categories

nodes.label: Data backup overview
nodes.heading: What you'll receive
nodes.snippet:
Once your backup finishes, you receive your data as JSON files, packaged into ZIP files (split and optionally encrypted based on your configuration). The package mirrors the categories you selected, with up to three top-level items:

- User and group data: a single _UserAndGroup.json_ file listing every user and group.
- Transactional data: a folder organized by module type (Process, Board, Dataset, Form, Project), then by app. Each app folder holds its records, the users who had access, comments, and attachments in their original file formats.
- Audit log data: a set of numbered files covering roughly the last year of account and flow activity.

Your data is exported for reading, archiving, and analysis. It is not a restorable database, so it can't be imported back into Kissflow.

For a full breakdown of the folder structure, what each record looks like, how to match attachments and users back to records, and how to read your field values, see Understanding your backup output.

nodes.label: Data backup overview
nodes.heading: Who can back up, and how often
nodes.snippet:
Only super admins can request, configure, and manage backups. You can only back up your data once every six months.

Once a backup starts running, it can't be interrupted. You can only cancel a backup while it's still queued.

nodes.label: Data backup overview
nodes.heading: Status and notifications
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow provides real-time monitoring for ongoing backups. A backup moves through these statuses: queued, in progress, completed, failed, or canceled.

Super admins receive email and in-app notifications as a backup progresses — when it's queued, when it starts running, and when it finishes (whether successfully or not). You can also review a complete record of past backups in Backup history.

nodes.label: Data backup overview
nodes.heading: Troubleshooting common issues
nodes.snippet:
- Backup delays can occur with large data volumes or high system load.
- Storage authentication errors occur when your cloud storage account isn't correctly authenticated. Verify the access credentials and permissions for that storage destination.
- File location and upload errors occur when the configured file location can't be found or the data can't be exported.

If you run into an issue that isn't covered here, reach out to the support team.

nodes.label: Data backup overview
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Backing up data to Google Drive
- Backing up data to Amazon S3
- Backing up data to Google Cloud Storage
- Backing up data to Kissflow
- Compliance and backup history
- Understanding your backup output

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: Understanding your backup output
nodes.snippet:
Understanding your backup output

Data backup is available on request. Contact the support team to have it enabled on your account.

Once your account backup finishes, you receive a set of JSON files and your original attachments, packaged into ZIP files (split and optionally encrypted based on your configuration). This article explains what's inside that package, how the data is organized, and how to read it.

If you haven't run a backup yet, start with Data backup overview, which covers the available storage options.

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: How the backup is packaged
nodes.snippet:
Your backup mirrors the data categories you selected during configuration. After you unzip the package, you'll see a file for user and group data, a folder each for transactional data and audit logs, plus a flow index and a readme:

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

At a glance, here's what each part is for:

| Part | What it is | Best used for |
| --- | --- | --- |
| UserAndGroup.json | Your master identity file: every user, external user, service account, role, and group in the account. | Mapping permissions and your directory structure; resolving "who did what." |
| TransactionalData-0000/ | The actual records from your flows, plus their comments and attachments. | Loading operational data into tools like Power BI, Tableau, or your own scripts. |
| AuditLogs-0000/ | A chronological log of account and flow activity over roughly the last year. | Compliance monitoring, forensic checks, and activity tracking. |

A few things to know up front:

- Your records are stored as JSON files (plain text), except attachments, which are kept as their original files (PNG, PDF, XLSX, and so on). The package also includes a couple of supporting files in other formats: a CSV flow index and a Readme.txt — both covered below.
- Most JSON files are a single array of records. The audit logs are the one exception: they use JSON Lines (one object per line). We flag this again in the audit section, because it changes how you open those files.
- The -0000 suffix is a batch number. Large accounts split their transactional data into several batch folders (TransactionalData-0000, TransactionalData-0001, and so on). Most flows sit entirely in one batch, but a very large flow's data can spread across more than one. Use the flow index to find a flow's main location; if a flow is large, also check the other batch folders for the same Flow ID. User and group data is always a single file with no batch suffix.
- The backup is a data export, not a restorable database. It's meant for reading, archiving, and analysis, not for importing back into Kissflow.

media.alt: Unzipped backup package showing the user and group data file, transactional data and audit log folders, flow index, and readme

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: How to use your backup data
nodes.snippet:
Because everything is open JSON, you can work with it in whatever tool you prefer. Some common uses:

- Audit and compliance — read the audit logs to produce a record of account activity for a review period. Group related events using BatchID.
- Reporting and analysis — load a flow's .json into a spreadsheet, BI tool, or script to analyze your records outside Kissflow.
- Archiving — keep the package as a long-term, offline copy of your account data, independent of the platform.
- Reconnecting the pieces — join records to people through the _id in UserAndGroup.json, and records to attachments through the record ID folder names under Attachments.

Worked examples

These short walkthroughs show how the IDs tie the files together.

Who deleted a group? You spot a group with _is_deleted: true in UserAndGroup.json (for example, _id: "Gr5f_Tu24tkp", the _Operations Department_ group). Look at the _modified_by block on that same entry. It names the actor, for example _id: "Us4YeHqN_4jb". Look that ID up elsewhere in UserAndGroup.json to confirm it's _Steve Rogers_, the admin who removed it.

Who's behind an audit log entry? An audit line's Object field is formatted id -- name (for example, Us7OHZJLfP2E -- Jane Doe). Take the ID before the --, look it up in UserAndGroup.json, and you get the full profile: their status, department, and type.

Who submitted a record? Open the record in the flow's .json (not the _Users.json file, which only lists who had access). Read the record's _created_by object, take its _id, and look it up in UserAndGroup.json to reveal the full identity, including their UserType.

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: Things to keep in mind
nodes.snippet:
- This export is for reading and archiving, not for importing back into Kissflow.
- If you chose Email as your storage mode, your backup arrives as a downloadable link — download it within seven days, since the link expires after that and you'd need to run a new backup to get another copy.
- Field keys may not match app labels, and there is no schema file. When a key is unclear, check the live app.
- Audit logs cover roughly the last year only.

If you encrypted your backup, save the passphrase you set — see Opening an encrypted backup below. Keep a copy of every passphrase you've used: if you later edit your backup configuration and set a new one, backups already encrypted under the old passphrase won't decrypt with the new one.

If you have any questions about your backup data, reach out to the support team.

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: Opening an encrypted backup
nodes.snippet:
If you didn't set a passphrase when configuring your backup, your files arrive as plain ZIPs — you can skip this section.

If you set a passphrase, your files arrive as encrypted ZIP files (.aes256cbc), one per module, named like TransactionalData-0000.aes256cbc. Decrypt each one before you can read it.

1. Install OpenSSL on your machine, if you don't already have it.
2. For each encrypted file, run:

__CODE_BLOCK_0

For example:

CODE_BLOCK_1__

3. Unzip the resulting .zip files to get the folders and JSON described in the rest of this article.

Your backup also includes a Readme.txt with these same steps.

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: User and group data
nodes.snippet:
File: UserAndGroup.json

A single JSON array listing every identity in your account: human users, external users, service accounts, roles, and groups. Each entry is one object, and the Kind field tells you what type it is.

Identity types (the Kind field)

| Kind | What it is | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| User | An internal human team member. | |
| ExternalUser | An outside client or vendor who signs in to your external portals. | |
| ServiceAccount | An automated integration account (API access). | IDs are prefixed SA-. |
| Group / ExternalGroup | A set of users used to route and share work (for example, "Marketing"). | |
| Role / AppRole | A permission level. AppRole is scoped to a specific app. | AppRole IDs are prefixed Ro. |

Useful fields on a user

| Field | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| _id | Internal ID for the identity (for example, Us43NlCnXQMV). |
| Kind | The identity type, as above. |
| Name | Display name. |
| Email / EmailVerified | The user's email address and whether it has been verified. |
| Status | Account status: Active, InActive, Invited, Requested Access, or Deleted. |
| UserType | Privilege level, such as Super Admin, User Admin, Billing Admin, or User. |
| UserDepartment | The user's department, where set (for example, HR, Marketing). |
| _created_at / _created_by | When and by whom the record was created. |
| _modified_at / _modified_by | Last change details. |
| _is_deleted | true if the entry has been removed. Deleted entries are still included for completeness — check this field rather than Status, which can carry other values unrelated to deletion. |
| Descendants / _descendants | For groups, the members and nested groups. |

The _id values here are the key to everything else. The same IDs appear throughout your transactional data and audit logs wherever a person or group is referenced, so this file is your directory for resolving "who did what."

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: Finding a flow: the flow index
nodes.snippet:
Your backup includes a flow index, a CSV file (for example, flows.csv) that lists every flow in your account. It's the fastest way to locate a specific flow's data, especially when your transactional data spans several batch folders.

Each row has five columns:

| Column | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| S. No | Row number. |
| Flow name | The flow's display name, as you see it in Kissflow. |
| Flow ID | The internal ID, which is also the folder name for that flow. |
| Flow type | Process, Board, Dataset, Form, or Project. This tells you the type of folder to open. |
| File location | The batch folder the flow lives in (for example, TransactionalData/TransactionalData-0001). |

To find a flow's data, look up its name in the index, then open ///. For example, a Process named "Leave Management" with Flow ID Leave_Management located in TransactionalData-0001 lives at TransactionalData-0001/Process/Leave_Management/.

Flow names can contain commas, so open the index in a spreadsheet app rather than splitting on commas if you process it with a script.

File location is a flow's primary batch, not a guarantee. A very large flow's data can be split across more than one batch folder, and such a flow may appear more than once in the index. If you don't find all of a flow's records in the listed location, check the other TransactionalData-NNNN folders for the same Flow ID.

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: Transactional data
nodes.snippet:
Folder: TransactionalData-0000/ (and -0001, -0002, and so on for large accounts)

This is the largest part of your backup. It holds the actual records (items) from your flows, along with their comments and attachments. The data is organized first by flow type, then by flow. The five flow types are Process, Board, Dataset, Form, and Project.

In Kissflow, an app is a bundle that groups several flows together. The backup does not preserve that grouping: an app's process, board, dataset, and dataform are each stored in their own flow-type folder, alongside standalone flows. There is no Apps folder. To gather everything that belongs to one app, look up its flows by name in the flow index.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Each flow folder is named by its Flow ID (the value in the flow index) and is self-contained. Open .json to read the records, _Users.json to see who had access, and the Comment and Attachments subfolders for anything attached to those records.

_Users.json lists the people who had access to the flow and their roles. It is not the list of who submitted records. To find who created or filled in a record, look at the _created_by field on the record itself, inside .json.

What's in the records file

.json is an array of records. Every record shares a common set of system fields, then adds the fields specific to that flow.

System fields you'll see on almost every record:

| Field | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| _id | Unique record ID. |
| _flow_name | The app the record belongs to. |
| _created_at / _created_by | Creation details, including the submitter. |
| _modified_at / _modified_by | Last modification details. |
| Name | The record's display name. |
| _doc_version | Internal version number tracking how many times the item changed. |
| _is_deleted | Appears (and is true) only on records that were deleted but kept in the backup for compliance. Most records won't have this field. |

The remaining fields hold your data and differ by module. Here's what to expect from each.

Process records

Process records carry the full workflow state, not just the form values. Alongside your form fields you'll find:

- ProcessInstance and ActivityInstance — nested objects describing the workflow run, current activity, and history.
- _status — the item status (for example, Completed).
- _stage, _current_step, _current_assigned_to, _last_completed_step — where the item is in the flow and who holds it.
- _request_number — the human-readable request number.
- _submitted_at, _completed_at — timeline stamps.

Board records

Board (case) records describe a card and its position on the board:

- CaseWorkflowInstance — the case's workflow object.
- _status_id / _status_name — the column the card sits in.
- _priority_name, _priority_sequence — priority.
- ItemType, _category, _is_draft — classification and draft state.

Dataset records

Datasets are the simplest. Each record is a flat row of your fields plus system fields:

- _order — the row's position in the dataset.
- Name and your own field columns.

There is no workflow data, because datasets store reference data and don't run through a workflow.

Form records

Form records are also flat rows of field values. You may notice field keys with a numeric suffix (for example, Status_1, Use_case_1), which is how repeating form fields are stored. A _visited field indicates whether the form was opened.

Project records (deprecated)

Project records resemble Process records but use project-specific workflow objects:

- ProjectFlowInstance and StepInstance — the project workflow and its steps.
- _current_step_name, _current_step_id, _entered_at — current position.
- _counter, _item_id — identifiers.

Comments

File: /Comment/Comment.json

Comments made on records are stored here, separate from the records themselves. Each comment includes:

- Content — the comment as a structured rich-text tree, with a RawContent field giving the plain-text version.
- AtMention and mentions inside Content — people tagged in the comment, with their _id and Name.
- Reactions, Status, AssignedTo, _created_by, _created_at — reactions, open/closed state, assignment, and authorship.

Comment.json is organized by record: each top-level object's _id matches the _id of the record the comments belong to, so that's how you link a comment thread back to its record. Within a comment, EntityType and EntityId point to where on the item the comment is anchored (for example, a specific activity or step), not to the record itself.

Attachments

Folder: /Attachments/

Attachments are kept as their original files with their original filenames, nested inside folders named by internal IDs so each file can be traced back to the record it came from. The layout typically looks like this:

```
Attachments/
└── <record ID>/
    └── <instance ID>/
        └── <attachment ID>/
            └── original_file.png
```


The top folder name is the record _id. To find which record an attachment belongs to, match that folder name against the _id in .json. The inner folders are internal references Kissflow uses to keep each file unique; you don't need them to locate the record.

media.alt: Flow index listing flow names and their corresponding Flow IDs

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: Audit log data
nodes.snippet:
Folder: AuditLogs-0000/

Audit logs are split across many numbered files (AuditLog_000000000000.json, AuditLog_000000000001.json, and so on). These files use JSON Lines format: one JSON object per line, not a single array. Read them line by line rather than parsing the whole file as one object.

Each line is one event:

| Field | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| AuditID | Unique event ID. |
| Timestamp | When the event happened (UTC). |
| ActedBy | Who performed the action. System-generated actions show as Flobot -- User, where Flobot is Kissflow's internal system account. |
| Event | The event type (for example, AC_DatasetBatchUpdate, AC_BackupStatusChanged). |
| Eventcategory | The broader category the event belongs to. |
| Object | The thing acted on, as id -- name. |
| IP, Platform | Where the action came from, when available. |
| BatchID | Groups related events from the same operation. |
| EventData | Event-specific details. |

Audit log backups include roughly the last year of activity at both the account and flow level. Older events are not part of the export.

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: Reading field names and values
nodes.snippet:
This is the most important thing to understand before you start working with your data.

The keys in your records are internal field IDs, not always the labels you see in the app. Most line up with the label (for example, Advance_Amount), but some won't:

- A field you never renamed may appear as Untitled_Field or Untitled_field.
- A renamed field keeps its original internal key. If you renamed "Description" after creating it, the data might still use a key like _1643035136_Description.
- Repeating fields carry numeric suffixes (Status_1).

The backup does not include a schema or data dictionary. There is no file that maps each field key to its current label, type, or form section. So if a key isn't self-explanatory, the most reliable way to identify it is to compare the record against the same item in the live app.

It adds a little friction, so it's worth knowing before the keys catch you by surprise.

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: Common value formats
nodes.snippet:
A couple of value shapes repeat everywhere, so they're worth learning once.

Dates are objects, not plain strings:

__CODE_BLOCK_0

Use dv for a clean display value, or the $date inside v when you need exact precision. They aren't identical: v.$date keeps milliseconds (...09.857Z) while dv drops them (...09Z), so use v.$date for exact-match or deduplication work. Both are in UTC; the tz and td fields describe the original timezone and can usually be ignored. If you're parsing in Power BI or SQL, point your function at the inner $date string.

People (users and groups) appear as objects:

CODE_BLOCK_1__

The _id matches an entry in UserAndGroup.json, so you can always resolve a reference back to a full user or group record.

An _id only resolves within its own file. User and group IDs live in UserAndGroup.json; record IDs live in each .json. Don't expect a record ID to appear in the user file, or vice versa.

nodes.label: Understanding your backup output
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Data backup overview
- Compliance and backup history

nodes.label: Duplicating and deleting a dataset
nodes.heading: Duplicating and deleting a dataset
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

You can easily duplicate datasets for reuse or delete them when they’re no longer needed. Here's how to manage both actions:

nodes.label: Duplicating and deleting a dataset
nodes.heading: Duplicating a dataset
nodes.snippet:
If your business requirements are in such a way that you cannot use an existing dataset as is but need a slightly modified version of it, then you can opt to create a replica of it. Duplicating a dataset creates a copy of it so that you can modify its structure without affecting its original version.

1. Click the More options button and then click Duplicate
2. The default name of the new dataset is Copy of <original dataset name>. However, you can modify it as you want.
3. Check the box if you would like to retain the same values and views for the new dataset.
4. Click Duplicate.

nodes.label: Duplicating and deleting a dataset
nodes.heading: Deleting a dataset
nodes.snippet:
1. Click the More options button and then click Delete. Kissflow checks if any flow is using the dataset you are trying to delete. Its dependencies, if any, will be shown on your screen. You can delete the dataset only after you remove the dependencies.
When deleting a dataset with granted app access, only dependencies in the production environment will be displayed. Dependencies from the development environment will be ignored, and any app flows relying on the dataset fields will not function as expected after the dataset is deleted.

2. Click Delete if you are sure there are no dependencies or have removed them all.

Deleting a dataset will permanently remove both the dataset and all its data. This action is irreversible.

nodes.label: Importing and exporting data from a dataset
nodes.heading: Importing and exporting data from a dataset
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

The CSV import feature in your dataset will help you transfer data from a CSV file or a spreadsheet with a .csv extension to your Kissflow dataset. Once imported, your CSV rows will appear as rows in your dataset, and each column will contain metadata about your data.

nodes.label: Importing and exporting data from a dataset
nodes.heading: Organizing the data in your CSV file
nodes.snippet:
The CSV Import feature allows you to import data in bulk into Kissflow. Here are some guidelines to follow when preparing a CSV file for import:

- Mandatory fields: If mandatory fields exist in your Dataset form, you must map at least one column in your CSV file with the required fields.
- Data: Within the CSV file, you can map only the following field types to your dataset: Text, Text area, Number, Rating, Date, Date-time, Currency, Yes/no, User, Single-select dropdown, Multi-select dropdown, Slider, and Checkbox.
- You cannot import data from a CSV file for the following field types: Attachment, checklist, signature, and image.
- For Yes/No fields, you can use any binary values: True/False, 1/0, Yes/No.
- Delimiter: You must only use the comma (,) as a delimiter inside the CSV file.
- Rows: The CSV file can have up to 50,000 rows, excluding the header.
- Columns: Your CSV file can have up to 100 columns. The CSV column names can be alphanumeric but should contain only the following characters: space, hyphen (-), and underscore (\_).
- Encoding: The CSV file must have a .csv extension that supports UTF-8 encoding.

> Note: To ensure consistent performance, datasets have default row and column limits. If you require an increase, please contact our support team with details about your use case. All requests to expand limits are evaluated individually, and expansions are approved at our team's discretion.

Supported date and time formats

Data Format

Example

DD-MMM-YY

02-DEC-20

DD-MMM-YYYY

02-DEC-2020

DD/MM/YY

02/12/20

DD/MM/YYYY

02/12/2020

MM/DD/YY

12/02/20

MM/DD/YYYY

12/01/2020

DD.MM.YY

02.12.20

DD.MM.YYYY

02.12.2020

DD-MM-YYYY

02-12-2020

- The supported datetime formats

Datetime Format

Example

DD.MM.YY HH:mm

- Timestamp formats

Timestamp

Example

D - Date

DD - 01, 24

M - Month

T - Constant Character (As per ISO)

T

H - 24 Hour

A - AM, PM

Z - Time Zon

Z - Z, +05:30

Once you have confirmed that your CSV file meets the above requirements, follow these steps to import the CSV data to your Kissflow Dataset:

1. Sign in to your Kissflow account. If you haven't already, create a new dataset.
2. On the top right section of the page, click Import CSV beside the Share button.
3. After the file picker opens, click Choose your files, browse your documents, and select your desired file with a .csv extension.
4. After importing the CSV file, the system checks it for file corruption and delimiter errors. If you find an issue, please correct it in the CSV file before re-uploading it.
5. Click Next to continue. Then choose the columns from your CSV file that you would like to appear in the dataset. You must map the columns in your CSV file to the fields in your dataset. For example, you can choose to map the email addresses from your CSV document into the Email field in your dataset.

> Note:
>
> - CSV header names and data fields must be of the same data type.
> - The import tool currently supports only alphanumeric column headers in CSV files. To prevent errors, please ensure your CSV headers are in English and do not contain special characters. Once the file is accepted, you will be able to map these alphanumeric headers to your specific system fields during the import configuration step.

After mapping the dataset values, you will have the choice to handle the data in your dataset:

1. You can either add new rows and update existing rows, or add only new rows while importing the data.
2. You can delete or keep the dataset rows that are not in the CSV file.
3. You can skip specific rows or cancel the import in case of errors.

After you have selected your options, click Next. Your data will begin importing.

You can close the window and continue working while waiting for all your data to be imported into your dataset. After the import is complete, you will be notified via email and push notifications.

There are 3 possible scenarios.

- All the imported data was successfully uploaded into the dataset.

- Some of the data was imported successfully, but some rows failed. You can download the failed rows, work on them, and upload the file again.

- The import was aborted. View the error logs to see what went wrong.

nodes.label: Importing and exporting data from a dataset
nodes.heading: Exporting data from a dataset
nodes.snippet:
To export a data from a dataset, follow these steps:

1. Click the Export button on the top right side of the screen next to the Filter button.
2. You can export data in two file types: CSV file and TXT file.
3. Choose the file you prefer and click Export.
4. After exporting, you will receive an email link to the exported file.

> Note:
>
> CSV exports are limited to 100,000 rows per file. If your dataset exceeds this limit, the export will be split into multiple CSV files and bundled into a ZIP folder. This ensures smoother downloads and better compatibility with tools like Excel.

> Note:
>
> When exporting datasets that include user fields, you’ll now receive an additional column with the user's email ID, alongside the existing User ID and User Name.

nodes.label: Importing and exporting data from a dataset
nodes.heading: FAQ
nodes.snippet:
Q) Can I use the Import CSV option in the dataset when auto-lookup is enabled in your dataset's lookup field?

Ans) When an auto lookup is enabled, you cannot use the import CSV option for a lookup column in Dataset. Auto lookup is designed to automatically populate the lookup column based on existing data relationships, which conflicts with manually importing data for that column.

nodes.label: Managing associated flows in a dataset
nodes.heading: Managing associated flows in a dataset
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans: ✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

The Associated flows section provides an overview of how your dataset interacts with other flows. It helps you understand dependencies, making managing and updating them easier.

To access associated flows, click Manage at the top-right corner of the dataset and select Associated flows.

nodes.label: Managing associated flows in a dataset
nodes.heading: Looked up flows
nodes.snippet:
Lookup fields allow a dataset to retrieve and display information from other flows. This section is divided into two parts:

Flows looked up to

This section shows the flows that reference this dataset to display or fetch information. For example, if a Process references vendor names or vendor IDs from this dataset, the process' name will appear here.

> Note:
>
> If a value in the master dataset is updated, any existing requests that have already used that value will remain unchanged. However, any workflows that reference the updated value will use the new data when processing new requests.

To configure the reference, click Configure, and you'll be redirected to the Security settings page.

Flows looked up from

This section displays the flows this dataset pulls data from. For example, if your Vendor name dataset retrieves address data from another dataset, the name of that flow will be listed here.

nodes.label: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.heading: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.snippet:
Audit log is a chronological record of user activities within your dataset. These activities include actions performed on flows and items by users.

Audit logs help maintain transparency and accountability by tracking changes at both the Flow-level and Item-level.

nodes.label: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.heading: Accessing the Audit Log
nodes.snippet:
To view the audit log:

- Navigate to Manage \> Audit log.

- Use the toggle to switch between Flow-level and Item-level views.

nodes.label: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.heading: What you can see in the audit log
nodes.snippet:
Each log entry provides detailed information about a specific event:

- Audit ID: A unique identifier automatically generated for every recorded event.

- Timestamp: The exact date and time the action occurred.

- Actor: The user account that performed the action.

- Action: Describes the specific activity, such as Dataset published, Items updated in batch, and so on.

- Object: The resource or entity that was affected.

- Event Category: A general label for the event type, useful for filtering. For example, Dataset administration.

- Platform: The device or operating system used during the action.

- IP Address: The network address from which the action was performed.

- Batch ID: A shared identifier for a group of related events, useful for correlating actions. Unlike the Audit ID, multiple events can share the same Batch ID.

> Note:
>
> Audit log entries may appear in the interface with a short delay, typically ranging from a few seconds to up to four hours.

For a deeper understanding of each entry, refer to the Interpreting audit logs guide.

nodes.label: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.heading: Sorting fields in logs
nodes.snippet:
To easily organize and analyze entries on the Audit log page, you can sort individual fields:

- Click the Expand icon next to a field header to sort the entries in ascending or descending order.

This helps you quickly find specific events based on timestamps, actions, actors, or other fields.

nodes.label: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.heading: Searching fields
nodes.snippet:
To find specific entries in the Audit log, you can use the built-in search functionality:

- Click the Expand icon next to a field header to reveal the filter options.

- Use the Search box to enter the term you want to look up within that field.

This helps you quickly narrow down the log to the most relevant entries.

nodes.label: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.heading: Filtering Audit log events
nodes.snippet:
You can filter audit log entries using the following fields and criteria:

Audit period

Choose a time range to view events from:

- Last 30 days – Displays events from the past 30 days.

- Last 60 days – Displays events from the past 60 days.

- Last 90 days – Displays events from the past 90 days.

- Custom date range – Select a specific date range to view events within that period.

> Note: The audit log retains data for the past 90 days only. To access records older than 90 days, please contact our customer support team.

Object

Filter by object name to narrow down logs related to a specific item. You can either select from the suggested list or enter the exact object name manually.

Event category

Use event categories to locate specific types of events efficiently. If you're unsure of the category name, try entering a flow name (e.g., _Process_ or _Board_) in the search bar—this will return relevant event categories associated with that flow type.

Actor

Filter events based on one or more users who performed the actions. Simply select the users from the list.

Action

Search or select a specific action (For example, Dataset published). Please note that you can filter by only one action at a time.

Audit ID, Batch ID, and IP address

Use these fields to trace specific events:

- Audit ID – Filter by the unique ID assigned to each recorded event.

- Batch ID – Use this to locate a group of related events.

- IP address – Filter logs originating from a specific IP address.

nodes.label: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.heading: Removing filter conditions
nodes.snippet: To remove a specific filter condition, hover over it and click the Remove button to clear it.

nodes.label: Managing audit logs in a dataset
nodes.heading: Exporting an audit log
nodes.snippet: Click the Export button to export the audit log as a file on the Audit log page. An email with the download link will be sent immediately to your registered email address from where you can download the file.

nodes.label: Managing filters
nodes.heading: Managing filters
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

A filter helps you view a confined set of data that matches certain conditions. With advanced filters, you can filter contextually on a few attributes like user and time thereby giving more depth to the filter conditions.

nodes.label: Managing filters
nodes.heading: Creating a filter
nodes.snippet:
1. Click the Filter button on the dataset's page.
2. Click Add a filter.
3. Select a field that you want to add to the filter from the Select field dropdown to define your filter conditions:
1. The operators are contextually populated based on the field you select. Choose an appropriate operator such as Equal to, Not equal to, Contains, Is empty, and so on.
2. Next, select a Field or enter the Value against which you are validating this condition.
4. Click Add a condition to add as many AND/OR conditions as you want.
5. Similarly, click \+ Add new filter to add as many filters as you want.
- After adding enough conditions and filters, choose between AND and OR conditions based on your preference. AND operator checks if all the applied filter conditions are met, and OR operator checks if at least one of the applied filter conditions is met.
6. Click Apply to filter records in your dataset.

You can use the ( - ) symbol in red beside every filter condition to remove it, and the close icon beside every filter to delete it.

nodes.label: Managing filters
nodes.heading: User-based contextual filters
nodes.snippet:
An advanced filter is about giving more context to your filter conditions, so we have introduced user-based contextual filters that will help you choose the exact attribute related to the current user when you define filter conditions involving user names or text values.

Adding a filter condition based on a user

While matching a text or user field against another field, you will see a field named Current User along with all its attributes like Name, Email Address, User Type, Status, and Designation in the right-hand side dropdown. Choose the attribute that's appropriate to your filter condition as necessary.

For example, if you wish to check if a specific department head's name is the current user's name, its filter condition would read as follows:

\[Department head\] \[Equal to\] \[Current User.Name\]

The user-based contextual fields are also available on the left-hand side Select field dropdown.

nodes.label: Managing filters
nodes.heading: Time-based contextual filters
nodes.snippet:
There are various options to filter your fields related to date and time.

Adding a filter condition based on time

While matching a date or time-based field against a value, click the ellipsis icon to view the contextual options. You can choose an appropriate one according to your requirements.

The following options are listed when a date or time field is selected:

- Now
- Next 6 hours
- Next 12 hours
- Today
- Tomorrow
- Yesterday
- This week
- Last 7 days
- Next 7 days
- Last 14 days
- Last 30 days
- This month
- This quarter
- Last quarter
- This year

You can also open the calendar after choosing a relevant value and modify the date as necessary in the Value field.

nodes.label: Managing members and permissions
nodes.heading: Managing members and permissions
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

You control who can access and manage your datasets through roles: Admin, Member, and Viewer. Each role comes with specific permissions to ensure the right level of access for every collaborator.

Here's a quick guide to what each role can do.

nodes.label: Managing members and permissions
nodes.heading: Admin
nodes.snippet:
A dataset admin has the highest privilege in a dataset and anyone who creates a dataset becomes its Administrator, that is, Admin by default.

A dataset admin can:

- Create, edit, and delete datasets and dataset views.
- Add or remove flow members in a dataset and assign relevant roles to them.
- Access the dataset history.
- Add or remove columns from a dataset.
- Modify the role of flow members to an Admin, a Member, or a Viewer as necessary without any restrictions.

> Tip
>
> Is there a way for a dataset admin to access others' datasets?
>
> Yes, if the dataset admin is also a Super Admin at the account level, they will be able to access anyone's dataset, access its share settings, make themselves an admin or any role of their choice.

nodes.label: Managing members and permissions
nodes.heading: Dataset member
nodes.snippet:
The permissions granted to a dataset member are relatively restricted when compared to an admin and they can:

- Modify the values of a dataset.
- Add or remove rows in a dataset.
- Duplicate a dataset.
- Create, edit, and delete the dataset views they own.
- Make another flow member a dataset member or a viewer only, that is, they cannot make anyone an admin or modify or delete the already existing admins in a dataset.

> Note
>
> A  Member cannot add or remove columns in a dataset.

nodes.label: Managing members and permissions
nodes.heading: Dataset viewer
nodes.snippet:
A dataset viewer has the least set of privileges. They can only:

- View a dataset.
- Export the dataset.
- Filter values in the dataset.

> Note
>
> If a flow member is only a viewer in a dataset but has Super Admin access at the account level, they will be able to access any dataset and access its Share settings.
>
> They can make themselves an Admin, Member, or a Viewer in the dataset they access via their Super Admin privilege and perform relevant actions as they wish.

Roles and permissions of a flow member in a dataset

Here is a detailed overview of permissions for these roles:

nodes.label: Managing members and permissions
nodes.heading: Sharing a dataset with the flow members
nodes.snippet:
Anyone who creates a dataset becomes its admin by default.

1. Navigate to the dataset for which you would like to add members.
2. Click the Manage button at the top right corner and click Share.
3. Click \+ Add members
4. Enter the name or email address of the flow member you would like to add.
5. Select a relevant role that would best suit them.
6. Click Add.

> Tip
>
> Only Flow Members with Super Admin access at the account level can view the Share settings page for any dataset. This means they can access and manage sharing settings for all datasets, regardless of their assigned role within each one.

nodes.label: Managing members and permissions
nodes.heading: Managing members in a dataset
nodes.snippet:
As a dataset admin, you can modify the roles of other flow members in your dataset.

To modify one's role:

1. Navigate to the dataset page and click Manage > Share.
2. Click the More options button beside a specific user's name.
3. Reassign the user to a different role or remove them from the dataset as necessary.

Learn the privileges you have in terms of reassigning users based on your current role in the dataset:

Your current role in the dataset

Make an admin

Make a member

Make a viewer

Remove

Admin

✓

✓

✓

✓

Member

X

✓

✓\*

Viewer

X

X

X

X

- (\*) - A Member can remove another Member or a Viewer only. They cannot remove the admins from the dataset.
- A Viewer can neither modify anyone's role nor remove them from a dataset.

nodes.label: Security settings and access control
nodes.heading: Security settings and access control
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

To update your dataset's security settings, open the particular dataset and click Manage > Security.

Here you can access the following settings:

nodes.label: Security settings and access control
nodes.heading: Security settings
nodes.snippet:
Dataset Admins can share datasets with other processes, boards, specific apps, and portals through the Security settings page. To share a dataset with an app, ensure that app access is granted for the dataset.

To update your dataset's security settings, click  Manage > Security > Security settings. On the right, you'll find options to configure field permissions for exposing data to the Lookup artifact in Kissflow flows.

Global permission

Global permission defines the access rule for the fields in your dataset across all flows. By default, the All fields permission is enabled for the Lookup artifact.

You can disable this permission for more restrictive control and grant permission to look up only specific fields.

1. Expand the All fields section and select Lookup specific fields.

2. In the popup, choose the fields you want to expose to your flows.

3. Click Done.

Customized permission

Customized permissions provide granular control over dataset access for specific flows. Dataset Admins can set exclusive access rules, overriding global permissions.

1. Click \+ Add a customized permission.

2. In the popup, navigate the tabs and select the flows.

3. Click Done. The selected flow will now appear on the settings page, where you can grant the flow access to all fields or specific fields.

> Note
> If no customized permission is set for a flow, it follows the global permission settings.

To remove customized permissions for a specific flow:

1. Click the emove customized permission button next to the flow.

2. Check for any lookup dependencies before confirming the deletion.

nodes.label: Security settings and access control
nodes.heading: Grant app access
nodes.snippet:
To refer to a dataset within apps and use it in the app’s flows, Dataset Admins can sync fields and configuration data to apps using this setting. By turning on the toggle button , a blank copy of the dataset, along with its fields and configurations, will be available inside apps.

> Note
> Once activated, this action is irreversible.

All datasets granted with app access permission will be available on the Explore page of the development environment.

Auto-generate dataset records

App users can auto-populate sample dataset records and simulate the app’s behavior in a realistic dev setting. Records can be auto-generated using our advanced AI, or you can choose to import data from a CSV file.

> Note:
>
> Users can only auto-generate records to preview how the shared dataset functions within the app's development environment. Creating new datasets directly within the development environment is not possible.

Restrict app access

By default, once you activate the Grant app access permission, all apps in the account will have access to the dataset. However, to restrict access to specific apps, navigate to Security settings and click Add a customized permission. Then go to the Apps tab and select the apps you wish to grant access to the dataset.

Lists in datasets

Any list associated with the shared dataset will automatically appear as a valid data source for specific field types. Unlike datasets, the original list’s data used in specific dataset fields will also sync to the dev environment for accurate testing.

> Note
>
> Though you can create new lists in the development environment, modifying the values of the original list associated with the dataset shared from the production environment is not possible.

nodes.label: Security settings and access control
nodes.heading: Access controls
nodes.snippet:
Dataset admins can allow or restrict users' permissions, which can impact their ability to print forms, download attachments, or impersonate datasets.

Download permissions

To decide who can download attachments from this dataset, you can toggle between two options: Anyone and Only Admins.

Print permissions

Setting print permissions for Anyone allows any user to print attachments from a dataset. Choosing Only admins restricts print permissions to dataset admins.

Impersonation

Using impersonation, you can act on behalf of a user or an item inside your Kissflow account. It applies to the dataset and all its associated views. Learn more about impersonating a user.

nodes.label: Account governance overview
nodes.heading: Account governance overview
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

nodes.label: Account governance overview
nodes.heading: What is account governance?
nodes.snippet: Account governance is a centralized hub, allowing Super Admins and IAM Admins to audit and manage their Kissflow accounts effortlessly. This feature is pivotal for maintaining data security and protecting sensitive information within your account. A user-friendly dashboard gives you a comprehensive view of all users, groups, workflows (including processes and boards), datasets, lists, connections, and authentication keys associated with your Kissflow account.

media.alt: Embedded video

nodes.label: Account governance overview
nodes.heading: Benefits of account governance
nodes.snippet:
- Compliance and standards: By implementing robust governance practices, you can maintain data integrity and establish a responsible and compliant operations framework.

- Data protection and risk mitigation: Account governance is crucial in safeguarding sensitive proprietary data and critical assets. By preventing risks associated with ungoverned data, you can mitigate vulnerabilities and maintain the security and integrity of your account’s data.

- Activity monitoring and abnormal detection: By tracking account activities over time, you can gain valuable insights and identify any abnormalities or suspicious behavior. This proactive approach allows for timely intervention, ensuring the security and integrity of your account.

nodes.label: Account governance overview
nodes.heading: What can you govern?
nodes.snippet:
- Users
- Groups
- Processes
- Boards
- Datasets
- Lists
- Authentication keys
- Connections

nodes.label: Account governance overview
nodes.heading: How can you access account governance?
nodes.snippet: Click your profile on the top right corner inside Kissflow and click Account governance.

nodes.label: AI control center
nodes.heading: AI control center
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Super admins can access AI settings for their Kissflow account using the AI Control Center. This feature allows administrators to enable or disable individual AI features. To access the AI Control Center, click your Profile icon > Account governance > AI control center.

nodes.label: AI control center
nodes.heading: Managing AI access
nodes.snippet:
Turn on Account-wide AI access switch to activate AI services for your account and then begin to enable or disable specific AI features across Process, Board, Dataset, Apps and portals, Integrations, and Account.

> Note: When Account-wide AI access is switched back on, the previously configured settings for individual AI features will be restored.

Data shared with AI

The Data shared with AI tag indicates that the feature uses your account or transactional data as input to provide AI-powered functionality. When a feature has this tag, relevant data from your Kissflow account may be sent to the underlying AI model (Large Language Model) to generate responses, suggestions, or insights. This tag helps administrators clearly understand which AI features rely on account data to function.

You can consider controlling  AI features listed on this page based on the following factors:

- Need of the AI feature for your account users.
- Enablement of features based on the Data shared with AI tag.
- Align usage with internal AI compliance policies.

> Note: When account-wide AI access switch is inactive, all AI-powered features across your account will be unavailable to users.

nodes.label: App governance
nodes.heading: App governance
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

App governance offers Super admins and IAM admins, a lens into how apps are used across their Kissflow account. It highlights trends in app activity, helps spot idle or high-usage apps, and surfaces potential risks like unauthorized access or external data exposure. These insights are essential for making informed decisions around app lifecycle management and data governance.

Click your profile at the top right corner inside Kissflow and click Account governance > Apps tab on the left. You can find the following sections in the overview page:

- Active apps - Apps that were most active during the selected time period. This helps you understand where most work is happening.

- Inactive apps - Unused apps in the selected time range. This lets you reassess whether these apps are still required or can be retired.

- Installed from app store

- External data - Apps connected to external systems which may involve data exchange with platforms outside Kissflow.

Clicking any live app in the overall view takes you to its detailed page with deeper insights. View the app’s status, creator, creation date, and last deployed details. Apart from these details, you have a lot of other sections where you can learn more about the app.

Users

The Users section shows everyone who is part of the app across environments. Monitoring the app membership helps you control over who can interact with the app, reducing the risk of data leaks or unapproved changes.

> Note:
>
> This is the only section available in the detailed page of a draft app.

Usage metrics

Unique users - Check the number of distinct users who accessed the app in the selected time period to understand real engagement.

Roles

This section lists the various roles defined within the app, along with the number of users assigned to each role. This helps you assess how responsibilities are distributed within the app.

Clicking a role takes you to its detailed page, where you can get more information about:

- Users - View the list of users in each role.

- Flows - See which flows users in this role can access, ensuring access stays authorized.

- Reports - Track report access for better control and compliance.

- Views - Monitor which views are available to each role.

External app roles

Identify any roles that are linked to this app from other apps in your account. These roles may bring in users who aren’t explicitly part of the app but still have access due to broader role assignments elsewhere. Monitoring these links is key to minimizing unintended data exposure and ensuring that only the right users can access sensitive information within the app.

Clicking a role takes you to its detailed page, where you can get more information about:

- Users - View the list of users in each role.

- Flows - See which flows users in this role can access, ensuring access stays authorized.

- Reports - Track report access for better control and compliance.

- Views - Monitor which views are available to each role.

Integrations

This section contains the list of integrations used within the app. It displays the status of each integration along with the connectors used, whether they link to internal Kissflow systems or external platforms. Reviewing this information helps you ensure that the app is only connected to the intended systems, reducing the risk of unauthorized data exposure.

External data objects

The External data objects section lets you see if the app connects to any third-party platforms to fetch or interact with external data. Monitoring these connections is essential for understanding where your app’s data is flowing and helps you assess any potential risks related to data security or compliance.

Clicking an external object takes you to its detailed page. This page provides a list of all the flows that retrieve and display data from that external object.

Flows created

In this section, you can get a list of all flow types created within the app - processes, boards, and data forms. This gives you a sense of how the app is structured.

Clicking a flow directs you to its detailed page, which lets you view the information below:

- Roles and activity

- Public forms (process and dataform)

- Integrations

- Sensitive fields

- Flow relationships:

- Flows looked up from/to

- Dependent and source flows (process and board)

- Logs - Download logs, print logs (process and board), and export logs.

Custom components

You can view the list of custom components built in this app, such as widgets or specialized interfaces. It’s helpful to identify unused or outdated components that can be retired to streamline the app.

Public forms

In this section, view all the process forms and data forms in this app that can be accessed from outside Kissflow. Any information in these forms is exposed externally.

Decision tables

In this section, you can view all the decision tables created in this app.

Linked apps

The Linked apps section shows you a list of apps and portals that are linked to this app and look up data from it. This section helps you:

- Understand where this app’s data is being consumed externally, supporting better data visibility.

- Identify potential data exposure, especially if sensitive information is being accessed by multiple apps.

Deployment history

This section provides a complete timeline of the app’s deployment activity, including details of when changes were deployed and who made them. Each entry logs the deployment date and time, and the user responsible for the deployment.

This deployment history is vital for:

- Maintaining a reliable audit trail of all app changes.

- Tracking accountability by identifying who made specific updates.

- Troubleshooting issues by pinpointing the exact version or deployment that introduced a change.

By monitoring the deployment history, you can ensure that all changes are intentional and authorized.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: Board governance
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ EnterpriseAs a Super Admin, or an IAM Admin, account governance feature in Kissflow gives you a good look at all the boards in your account. It helps manage data better by showing you everything about your boards, letting you control and make smart choices about how they're used and improved. This feature helps you manage your boards well to boost overall performance.You can see a list of all your boards in the _All boards_ section. In the _Idle boards_ section, you can find the boards that haven't had any activity for a chosen time period. This way, you can decide what to do with boards you don’t need anymore.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: Security and permissions
nodes.snippet:
The Externally connected section lets you identify the boards exposing sensitive data outside your account through external connectors of the integrations in your account. The sections, Unrestricted download permissions and Unrestricted print permissions, provide valuable information regarding board attachments and forms that can be accessed and printed by anyone. Reviewing these sections allows you to identify potential instances where sensitive data may be shared outside Kissflow. This enables you to take appropriate measures to protect the confidentiality and security of the data.

Selecting a board will navigate you to the detailed page of that board. Here, you can view its creator, when it was created, when it was published, and who published it.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: Admins
nodes.snippet: In the Admins section, you can view all board admins and their roles, aiding security and preventing unauthorized access. Identifying privileged individuals or groups allows you to monitor and control unauthorized actions or data breaches, ensuring board integrity and confidentiality.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: Activity
nodes.snippet: You can assess the productivity of a board by analyzing the activity within it. This includes the number of item creations on the board and any note added to those items for a selected time period. It also gives you information on the number of items updated for that period. This provides valuable insights into the board’s effectiveness and helps you identify areas for improvement to enhance productivity and streamline operations.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: Integrations
nodes.snippet: In this section, you can check out the list of integrations connected to a specific board. It shows you the statuses of those integrations and the connectors they use. This way, you can ensure the board is only connected to the integrations they're supposed to and avoid any data exposure risks.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: AI-identified sensitive data
nodes.snippet:
This section lets you know the form fields that may potentially contain sensitive data that AI identifies. With this information, you can take measures to prevent unwanted exposure of these sensitive data later on. These sensitive data are identified and classified by AI into three categories:

1\. Personal data - Any data that could lead to identifying a specific individual. For example: Name, address, contact details, etc.

2\. Confidential data - Any data demanding a high level of security and not intended for public access. This will include proprietary business information, financial data, sign in credentials, or other confidential details that would need protection.

3\. Health data - Any data concerning an individual's physical or mental health, medical history, or treatment records.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: Flows looked up from and looked up to
nodes.snippet: In the _Flows looked up from_ section, you can view a list of flows from which a board can look up data. Data from those flows are exposed to the specific board.Using the _Flows looked-up_ section, you can view a list of flows that can look up data from a specific board. Data from this board is exposed to the flows that look up this board.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: Download, print, and export logs
nodes.snippet: In this section, you can watch all the attachment downloads, form prints, and exports of a board and its reports in the last 30 days to see if any sensitive information is being exposed. It's crucial to spot any instances where sensitive data might be shared with people or systems inside and outside Kissflow and take action to prevent it.

nodes.label: Board governance
nodes.heading: Audit log
nodes.snippet: You can learn about the activities performed on a board using the audit log. This log provides a comprehensive record of all actions in the last 30 days, allowing for detailed analysis and a deeper understanding of the board. This helps track and examine various activities on the board for enhanced monitoring and security purposes.

nodes.label: Dataset governance
nodes.heading: Dataset governance
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

As a Super Admin, or an IAM Admin, account governance offers valuable insights into all the datasets currently in your Kissflow account. This feature enhances data governance by providing visibility and control, and helps you in informed decision-making regarding dataset utilization and optimization in your account.You can view a list of all the datasets in your account under the All datasets section. The Not looked up section lists all the datasets that are not looked up by any other flow or system. Based on this, you can decide which datasets are no longer needed and can act on them accordingly.

nodes.label: Dataset governance

This helps you identify any sensitive data that can be shared outside Kissflow.Selecting a dataset takes you to a detailed page where you can know who created the dataset and when. You also have other sections from which you can learn more about the dataset.

nodes.label: Dataset governance
nodes.heading: Admins
nodes.snippet: The Admins section displays all the admins in a dataset and their roles. This information allows for thorough monitoring and control of privileged access, ensuring that only authorized personnel can make administrative changes or access sensitive data in the dataset.

nodes.label: Dataset governance
nodes.heading: Integrations
nodes.snippet: In this section, you can explore the list of integrations linked to a particular dataset. It provides details about the status of each integration and the connectors used. This allows you to ensure that the dataset is only connected to the intended integrations, minimizing the potential for data exposure and associated risks.

nodes.label: Dataset governance
nodes.heading: AI-identified sensitive data
nodes.snippet: This section lets you know the form fields that may potentially contain sensitive data that AI identifies. With this information, you can take measures to prevent unwanted exposure of these sensitive data later on. These sensitive data are identified and classified by AI into three categories:1\. Personal data - Any data that could lead to identifying a specific individual. For example: Name, address, contact details, etc.2\. Confidential data - Any data demanding a high level of security and not intended for public access. This will include proprietary business information, financial data, sign in credentials, or other confidential details that would need protection.3\. Health data - Any data concerning an individual's physical or mental health, medical history, or treatment records.

nodes.label: Dataset governance
nodes.heading: Flows looked up from and looked up to
nodes.snippet: In the Flows looked up from section, you can view a list of flows from which a dataset can look up data. Data from those flows are exposed to the specific dataset.You can view a list of flows that can look up data from a specific dataset using the Flows looked up to section. Data from this dataset is exposed to the flows that look up this dataset.

nodes.label: Dataset governance
nodes.heading: Download and export logs
nodes.snippet: In the Download logs and Export logs section, you can keep an eye on all the instances of attachment downloads, and dataset exports in the last 30 days to see if any sensitive information is being exposed to people, or systems inside and outside Kissflow.

nodes.label: Dataset governance
nodes.heading: Audit log
nodes.snippet: In the Audit log section, you can gain visibility and valuable insights into activities performed in a dataset. This log provides a comprehensive record of all actions in the last 30 days with respect to time, allowing for detailed analysis and a deeper understanding of the dataset. This is helpful to track and examine various activities in the dataset for enhanced monitoring and security purposes.

nodes.label: Governing authentication keys
nodes.heading: Governing authentication keys
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Governing authentication keys is crucial for several reasons. First and foremost, it helps maintain the security and integrity of your Kissflow account and its data. As a Super Admin, or an IAM Admin, you can control who has access to your APIs and integrations by managing and governing these keys. This minimizes the risk of unauthorized access or data breaches. Proper governance of authentication keys also allows for better tracking and auditing of API.You can learn about all the authentication keys (access and API keys) in your account on a single page. Details like expiry of the keys, active time, created at and by, last accessed time, and their statuses are displayed so you can have proper visibility.

nodes.label: Governing connections
nodes.heading: Governing connections
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

As a Super Admin, or an IAM Admin, you can get information about the connections used in your account. This gives you visibility and helps you in informed decision-making regarding the optimization of connections in your account. You can view the number of integrations that use a particular connection, as well as the connection's creation time and creator.

By clicking a connection, you'll access its detailed page, where you'll find information about the creator and the date it was created.

Further insights about the connection can be obtained from the following two sections:

Admins

Connections can also be used with third-party tools, making it even more important to thoroughly monitor and control privileged access to a connection. From the Admins section, you can ensure that only authorized people can make changes or access sensitive data using the connection.

Integrations used in

This section lists the integrations that use a particular connection to transfer data between various internal and external systems. This allows you to ensure that only the intended integrations use the connection, reducing the potential for data exposure and associated risks.

nodes.label: Governing lists

nodes.snippet:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

As a Super Admin, or an IAM Admin, you can manage and optimize the usage of lists in your account, leading to enhanced data management practices and improved overall performance. The list governance page lists all the lists in your Kissflow account, along with when they were created and who created them.Selecting a list takes you to a detailed page that shows you the list creator and date of creation. You can learn more about the list from the following sections.

nodes.label: Governing lists
nodes.heading: Admins
nodes.snippet: In this section, you can see all the admins in a list and their roles. This information allows for thorough monitoring and control of privileged access, ensuring that only authorized personnel can make administrative changes or access sensitive data in the list.

nodes.label: Governing lists
nodes.heading: Flows looked up to

nodes.label: Group governance
nodes.heading: Group governance
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

As a Super Admin, or an IAM Admin, account governance provides you a clear view of all groups within your Kissflow account. It presents a list of groups, their creators, and the tally of members and subgroups they encompass. Selecting a group from the list directs you to a detailed page showcasing all users and subgroups within that group.This functionality is crucial in executing effective group governance, ensuring proper access controls are in place. It's a proactive measure to guarantee that sensitive resources within your account are accessed solely by authorized groups.

nodes.label: Portal governance
nodes.heading: Portal governance
nodes.snippet:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Portal governance offers Super admins and IAM admins, a lens into how portals are used across their Kissflow account. It highlights trends in portal activity, helps spot idle or high-usage portals, and surfaces potential risks like unauthorized access or external data exposure. These insights are essential for making informed decisions around portal lifecycle management and data governance.

Click your profile at the top right corner inside Kissflow and click Account governance > Portals tab on the left. You can find the list of portals in your account along with their details.

Clicking any portal takes you to its detailed page with deeper insights. View the portal’s status and creator details. Apart from these details, you have a lot of other sections where you can learn more about the portal.

Users

The Users section shows everyone who is part of the portal across environments. Monitoring the portal membership helps you control who can interact with the portal, reducing the risk of data leaks or unapproved changes.

> Note:
>
> This is the only section available in the detailed page of a draft portal.

Usage metrics

Unique users - Check the number of distinct users who accessed the portal in the selected time period to understand real engagement.

Roles

This section lists the various roles defined within the portal, along with the number of users assigned to each role. This helps you assess how responsibilities are distributed within the portal.

Clicking a role takes you to its detailed page, where you can get more information about:

- Users - View the list of users in each role.

- Flows - See which flows users in this role can access, ensuring access stays authorized.

- Reports - Track report access for better control and compliance.

- Views - Monitor which views are available to each role.

External roles

Identify any roles that are linked to this portal from other apps/portals in your account. These roles may bring in users who aren’t explicitly part of the portal but still have access due to broader role assignments elsewhere. Monitoring these links is key to minimizing unintended data exposure and ensuring that only the right users can access sensitive information within the portal.

Clicking a role takes you to its detailed page, where you can get more information about:

- Users - View the list of users in each role.

- Flows - See which flows users in this role can access, ensuring access stays authorized.

- Reports - Track report access for better control and compliance.

- Views - Monitor which views are available to each role.

Linked apps & portals

This section shows you a list of apps and portals that are linked to this portal and look up data from it. This section helps you:

- Understand where this portal’s data is being consumed externally, supporting better data visibility.

- Identify potential data exposure, especially if sensitive information is being accessed by multiple portals.

External data objects

The External data objects section lets you see if the portal connects to any third-party platforms to fetch or interact with external data. Monitoring these connections is essential for understanding where your portal’s data is flowing and helps you assess any potential risks related to data security or compliance.

Clicking an external object takes you to its detailed page. This page provides a list of all the flows that retrieve and display data from that external object.

Flows created

In this section, you can get a list of all flow types created within the portal - processes, boards, and dataforms. This gives you a sense of how the portal is structured.

Clicking a flow directs you to its detailed page, which lets you view the information below:

- Roles and activity

- Public forms (process and dataform)

- Integrations

- Sensitive fields

- Flow relationships

- Flows looked up from/to

- Dependent and source flows (process and board)

- Logs - Download logs, print logs (process and board), and export logs.

Custom components

You can view the list of custom components built in this portal, such as widgets or specialized interfaces. It’s helpful to identify unused or outdated components that can be retired to streamline the portal.

Decision tables

In this section, you can view all the decision tables created in this portal.

Public forms

In this section, view all the process forms and dataforms in this portal that can be accessed from outside Kissflow. Any information in these forms is exposed externally.

Integrations

This section contains the list of integrations used within the portal. It displays the status of each integration along with the connectors used, whether they link to internal Kissflow systems or external platforms. Reviewing this information helps you ensure that the portal is only connected to the intended systems, reducing the risk of unauthorized data exposure.

Deployment history

This section provides a complete timeline of the portal’s deployment activity, including details of when changes were deployed and who made them. Each entry logs the deployment date and time, and the user responsible for the deployment.

This deployment history is vital for:

- Maintaining a reliable audit trail of all portal changes.
- Tracking accountability by identifying who made specific updates.
- Troubleshooting issues by pinpointing the exact version or deployment that introduced a change.

By monitoring the deployment history, you can ensure that all changes are intentional and authorized.

Audit log

By utilizing the audit log, you can gain visibility and valuable insights into activities performed in the portal. It provides a comprehensive record of all actions with respect to time, allowing for detailed analysis and a deeper understanding of the portal. Based on this, you can track and examine various activities in the portal for enhanced monitoring and security purposes.

The Idle processes area shows you all the processes that haven't had any activity for a while based on your chosen period. This way, you can spot which processes are no longer needed and take action on them as needed.

From this, you can determine which process forms can be accessed externally, as this exposes any information contained within the forms. From the Externally connected section, you can identify the processes exposing sensitive data outside your account through external connectors of your account’s integrations. Being aware of such processes helps you manage data security and minimize the risks associated with the exposure of sensitive data outside your Kissflow account.Unrestricted download permissions and Unrestricted print permissions sections inform you about the process attachments and forms that anyone can download and print, respectively. This helps you identify the processes that can share sensitive data with people or systems outside Kissflow.You can click any process to access its detailed page to see the status of the process, its creator, and its creation date. Regular monitoring and review of the admins can help you maintain the integrity and confidentiality of the process, reducing the risk of unauthorized actions or data breaches.

This way, you can ensure the process is only connected to the integrations they're supposed to and avoid any data exposure risks.

This section lets you know the form fields that may potentially contain sensitive data that AI identifies. With this information, you can take measures to prevent unwanted exposure of these sensitive data later on. These sensitive data are identified and classified by AI into three categories:

1\. Personal data - Any data that could lead to identifying a specific individual. For example: Name, address, contact details, etc.

2\. Confidential data - Any data demanding a high level of security and not intended for public access. This will include proprietary business information, financial data, sign in credentials, or other confidential details that would need protection.

3\. Health data - Any data concerning an individual's physical or mental health, medical history, or treatment records.
