nodes.snippet: In the Flows section, you can view the flows (processes, boards, and apps) a particular user can access. This helps ensure the user is granted access only to authorized flows, aligning with proper permissions and security measures.

Correspondingly, the statuses of those integrations and the connectors used by them are also displayed. This will help ensure that the user is only accessing authorized integrations and prevent potential data exposure.

nodes.label: User governance
nodes.heading: Connections

You can also identify the users exposing sensitive data outside your account through external connectors of your account’s integrations. This makes identifying potential risks or issues easier and proactively mitigating them.

nodes.label: User governance
nodes.heading: Authentication keys
nodes.snippet: You can view a comprehensive list of all the access and API keys created by a specific user in your account. These keys are used for authentication purposes, and by reviewing the list, you can gain visibility into when they were last accessed when they will expire, and which keys are currently active. This information can help you manage your account’s security and ensure only authorized users can access your resources.

nodes.label: User governance
nodes.heading: Active sessions
nodes.snippet: Under this section, you can view a specific user's active login sessions, their last sign-in time, and the internet protocol (IP) address from which they logged in. Based on this, you can learn about any unauthorized or suspicious login sessions.

nodes.label: User governance
nodes.heading: Download, print, and export logs

nodes.label: User governance
nodes.heading: Audit log

nodes.label: Administration
nodes.heading: Administration
nodes.snippet:
Administration

Manage users, security, governance, and environments.

nodes.label: Administering portals
nodes.heading: Administering portals
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Portal admins have access to the Portal settings tab on the portal administration screen. From your portal page, click Manage > Portal administration > Portal settings. Here, you can view and customize your portal domain name, change the portal logo, apply format settings to the portal, and manage portal security.

nodes.label: Administering portals
nodes.heading: Portal settings
nodes.snippet:
Portal details

While setting up the portal, you would have given a name for your portal and a unique portal domain URL. You can change the portal name and domain at any time, but if another portal that's part of your Kissflow account uses a particular domain URL, you cannot use that URL.

If you change the domain address yourself, Kissflow will automatically redirect the old domain to the new one, and all portal users will be locked out and notified. However, your former domain will become available to register by another organization. If it is claimed by someone else, your old domain will no longer redirect.

Portal Customization

Portal logo and Mobile branding

Your organization’s logo is your default portal logo. It will appear on the sign in screen and certain product emails. You can also change it to a customized logo.

We recommend you use a rectangular logo that is 240 X 36 pixels. The logo file must be less than 250 KB and in JPG, PNG, or SVG format.

Also, for portal on mobile phones, provide app name and app icon under the mobile branding section. App name will always be your portal name. The app icon must be  512\*512 pixels in resolution, less than 500 KB in size, and in PNG format.

Format settings

As a Portal Admin, you can control your portal's default settings that impact how data is formatted and displayed.

Portal time zone: Your portal's time zone is considered when you configure a scheduled action in integrations or include a deadline in a workflow. All data will be matched to your portal's time zone. You can select a timezone using the map or directly from the dropdown. You can zoom in and out of the map to see all available time zones worldwide.

Language: Choose the primary language for your portal account. Kissflow is currently offered in English, Simplified Chinese, Italian, Korean, Japanese, Spanish, Brazilian Portuguese, French, German, Russian, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic.

Date format: You can choose a data format of your choice. This will adjust the formatting of your portal's number, date, and date-time fields. Here are the data format options provided by Kissflow:

- Local (based on your browser settings)
- US (D/MM/YYYY)
- European (MM/D/YYYY)
- ISO (YYYY-MM-DD)

Number and currency format: Local number and currency conventions are automatically discovered from the browser settings. Numbers will always automatically be formatted based on the user's local conventions by default.

An individual user's preferences can override the portal’s timezone, date and time format, and language.

Click Save when you are done.

Portal security

Manage how users sign in

Your portal is assigned a unique URL that can be used for signing in. On the sign-in page, portal users can enter the email address and password they set up during the initial sign-up or after a password reset. Kissflow also supports OAuth-based sign-in for Google and Microsoft accounts.

Manage how users sign up

Rather than individually inviting new users, you can choose to make your portal sign-up process globally accessible by sharing the custom sign-up URL. You can access Sign-up preferences under Portal administration and enable the Allow new signups option.

The URL expiry limit can be set anywhere from 1 to 7 days under the Set invite link expiry section. Anyone who visits this URL will be prompted to add their name and email address. Then, Portal Admins will be notified to approve the sign-up of the user. Choosing No approval needed will allow portal users to sign up directly without the approval of the Portal Admin. As a Portal Admin, you can manually choose the portal user from the Portal user tab and click the Activate button to provide an active license to the portal user.

If you want to disable future sign-ups via this URL for new users, disable Allow new signups and click Save.

Organization settings

As a portal admin, you can define your trusted organizations and give them access to view and assign tasks to all portal users across organizations. This customization allows you to meet your portal’s operational needs.

Choose the organizations you believe should have the visibility and authority to assign tasks to all portal users. You can select and add multiple organizations from the dropdown and click Save.

This ensures that the right entities within your portal can effectively manage and coordinate tasks.

The organization names listed in the dropdown menu here in organization settings are sourced from the organization name column in Portal administration > Portal users.

Managing organization name in portal users

The organization name is an important field in portal users, helping to categorize and manage users based on their respective organizations. This field is auto-detected but can be manually edited if needed.

Enabling cross-organization user access

This toggle switch enables all portal users to view and assign tasks to all other portal users across organizations.

> Note:
> Before enabling this option, ensure that cross-organization user access complies with your organization's policies and relevant regulations. If you do not want to provide this universal access, carefully define your trusted organizations in the organization settings section.

FAQs

1\. Can I add a new organization directly in the dropdown menu?

No, new organization names must be added by updating the organization name field for a user in portal users.

2\. What happens if I delete an organization name from a user in portal users?

The organization name will be removed for that specific user, but if that particular organization name is assigned to at least one other user, it will remain visible in the dropdown menu under organization settings.

However, if no other users have that organization name, it will no longer appear in the dropdown menu under organization settings.

3\. What is the default setting for organizational settings and cross-organization user access?

By default, organizational settings are not configured and cross-organization user access is turned off. This means portal users can only view and assign tasks to other portal users within their own organization.

4\. What happens if a user is using a public domain?

If a user is using a public domain, the organization name will be empty in portal users. However, the admin can edit this field to add an organization for the user.

nodes.label: Administering portals
nodes.heading: Portal users
nodes.snippet: Portal Admins can activate, deactivate, and delete portal users here. They can also resend verification emails to unverified users and send reset password emails when necessary. The Portal users table offers a way to modify details of multiple portal users simultaneously.

nodes.label: Administering portals
nodes.heading: Audit log
nodes.snippet: The Audit log section stores the history of all the activities related to the portal. It allows the portal admin to search and find the portal-level and item-level changes made by users in a portal.

nodes.label: Administering portals
nodes.heading: Deployment history
nodes.snippet: Deployment history allows you to track the different version changes deployed while building the portal app. If multiple users are building the same app, it will show the details of who deployed a particular version and when it was deployed. You can learn more about it here.

nodes.label: Creating and managing portals
nodes.heading: Creating and managing portals
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

nodes.label: Creating and managing portals
nodes.heading: Creating a portal from scratch
nodes.snippet:
Creating a new portal inside Kissflow is a straightforward process. As a Super Admin of your account, you can create portals.

1. Click the Create button > Portal from the left navigation bar to create a new Portal.
2. Enter a unique name for your portal. It should be less than 80 characters in length. Give your portal a custom domain address. External users will use the Portal domain URL to access your portal. You can later change this portal domain by going to portal administration.
3. Once you have created the portal, go to Manage and click Edit portal. You will be taken to the development environment that allows you to develop the portal application, which you can share with portal users.

Click here to learn more about the different types of environments in an app builder. Once a portal is created, it will be in a draft state until you make it live by deploying the developed portal application.

To learn how to build your application from scratch, read these topics next:

1. Assigning roles and permissions.
2. Creating data forms, processes, and boards.
3. Designing UI with pages.
4. Building bespoke navigation paths.
5. Creating components.
6. Integrating with other applications.
7. Managing security and settings of the application.

> A user with access to your live public portals, not your Kissflow account, is called an external user.

nodes.label: Creating and managing portals
nodes.heading: Managing portals
nodes.snippet:
After a portal is created, you can find the portal in the production environment on the Explore page in the Managed by me section. You may add yourself as the admin of the portal. Each enterprise account in Kissflow can create a maximum of 10 new portals, with each portal having a maximum of 10 external users per portal. This allows customers to experiment with building portals and test them by adding external test users. When a customer wants to expose a portal they have created as a full-fledged external portal and add real external users, they may reach out to support for pricing and other details.

Once you have created a portal, you can add other Portal Admins to manage it. Click Share and click + Add users to add any user that is part of your Kissflow account to manage this portal. The same user can be the Portal Admin for multiple portals. Portal Admins can enable, disable, edit, or archive a portal. Portal Admin also has permission to deploy new versions of an application inside a portal. Currently, to delete a portal, you have to contact Support. Inside the portal share settings, you can find the different roles created for use inside your application.

nodes.label: Creating and managing portals
nodes.heading: Enabling and disabling portals
nodes.snippet: Once you have built your application from scratch and tested it in the test environment, you can deploy it and publish it live, allowing portal users to log in and access it. Sharing the portal domain URL with the portal users will enable them to create an account and access it. Click the More options button and click Disable to disable access to the portal and make it live for portal users. You will see an enable portal option in the same place when the portal is in the disabled status.

media.alt: unnamed.png

nodes.label: Creating and managing portals
nodes.heading: Archiving and unarchiving portals
nodes.snippet:
Click the More options button and click Archive to archive the portal. Portal users will immediately lose access to the archived portal. You will see an enable portal option in the same place when the portal is in the disabled status.

To unarchive a portal navigate to Explore > Managed by me > Portals and click on the portal you want to unarchive. This takes you to the portal page from you can unarchive the portal as shown in the gif below.

media.alt: unnamed.png

nodes.label: Creating and managing portals
nodes.heading: Portal administration, Integrations and Roles
nodes.snippet:
Portal users have multiple sign in options that you can change in portal administration. Portal integrations and roles work the same in Kissflow apps.

> Important note:Please keep in mind that changes saved in your portal app while in the development environment are final, as the environment does not include an undo or recovery option.What this means:
>
> - If you delete a child flow, like a page or a dataform, it will need to be recreated manually. There is no option to restore deleted flows.
>
> - Your work in the development environment is safely isolated from production until deployment. When you’re ready, deploying the portal will move all of your new changes to production at once. Therefore, we urge you to review your changes before saving. This will help make sure all the necessary flows stay intact and reduces rework for you.
>

nodes.label: Managing portal users
nodes.heading: Managing portal users
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Portal Admins can activate, deactivate, and delete portal users. They can also resend verification emails to unverified users and send reset password emails when necessary. The User Management table offers a way to modify multiple portal users simultaneously. Click here to learn more about defining roles within an app.

> Note:
>
> After deploying an external portal into production, only external users can be added to roles within that portal.

nodes.label: Managing portal users
nodes.heading: Adding users manually
nodes.snippet:
1. Navigate to Portal administration > Portal users.

2. Click Add a user.

3. A dialogue box will appear where:
1. Both the First name and Email address are required.
2. There's an option to send or skip an email invite to the new user.
4. Click Add to finalize the user addition.

Inviting users

When you invite someone to join your portal, an invitation email is sent to the specified address. If the invitee accepts and there are available user licenses, they'll be automatically activated. Alternatively, you can manually activate them by selecting the user checkbox and clicking Activate. Activation of multiple users simultaneously is possible if adequate licenses are available. The user count is automatically updated in the Active Users tile. If there's a shortage of licenses, the invited user will remain inactive and unable to sign in to the portal.

Resend verification email

To resend a verification email, select the user checkbox next to their first name and click Resend verification email. Users who are already verified, or those who are deactivated, won't get an email. Post the operation, a summary will show details about who got the email and who didn’t, with reasons.

Resetting a password

To initiate a password reset, select the user checkbox and click Reset password. The user will get an email containing a reset link, valid for 24 hours. Until resetting the password, they can't sign in. Some users who won't get this email include:

- Unverified users.
- Inactive users.
- Users from portals where _Sign in with Kissflow_ is turned off.

nodes.label: Managing portal users
nodes.heading: Managing portal users
nodes.snippet:
To manage attributes of portal users:

1. Visit Portal administration > Portal users.
2. Click the user to make direct edits. Adjustments are saved automatically. If an email is updated, the user must verify the new address before accessing the portal again.
3. Click Resend verification email if needed.

Deactivating and activating users

To deactivate any user, select the user checkbox and click Deactivate. Once deactivated, the count under Inactive users tile updates itself. Inactive users can sign in once their account is activated again by a Portal Admin. To reactivate, select the user and click Activate. The user count is automatically updated in the Active users' tile. If you're out of licenses, contact support to buy more licenses.

A user’s license gets revoked after deactivation. However, their data is retained temporarily in our systems before eventual deletion.

Deleting users

When you delete portal users, they are removed from your account, and all their data is lost. To delete a portal user,

1. Select the portal user checkbox.
2. Click Delete users.
3. Upon deletion, all user-related data is lost, and the user count gets updated.

nodes.label: Portal audit log
nodes.heading: Portal audit log
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ EnterpriseThe audit log section stores the history of all the activities related to the portal. It allows the portal admin to search and find the portal-level and item-level changes made by users in a portal. To view your audit log, click the More options button  \> Portal administration > Audit log.

You can get the following details of events recorded as part of your portal from the audit log:

- Audit ID: This is a unique ID generated automatically by the system for every event that's recorded as part of the portal history.
- Timestamp: The exact time when a specific action happened or was performed.
- Actor: The user who performed the action.
- Action: The kind of activity or action that had taken place.
- Object: The object or the underlying resource that is being changed
- Event category: Category name for a set of events that you can use to filter down.
- Platform: The device or OS from which the event was recorded.
- IP: The IP address from which the event was recorded.
- Batch ID: This is a unique ID generated automatically by the system for a group of events that are related to each other. Unlike Audit ID, the Batch ID of these events will be the same for reference purposes.

nodes.label: Portal audit log
nodes.heading: Sorting fields
nodes.snippet:
1. On the portal page, click the More options button > Portal administration > Audit log.
2. Click the Down-arrow button   beside a field's name to sort a field in ascending or descending order.

nodes.label: Portal audit log
nodes.heading: Searching fields
nodes.snippet:
1. On the portal page, click the More options button  > Portal administration > Audit log.
2. Click the down-arrow button  beside a field filter to search for a specific entry as necessary.
3. Enter the search term in the search area that follows as necessary.

nodes.label: Portal audit log
nodes.heading: Filtering fields
nodes.snippet:
You will be able to filter the audit log events based on the following fields/criteria:

- Audit period
- Last 30 days: Get event information for the previous 30 days from the current date.
- Custom date range: Select a customized date range (within the last 90 days) to filter events.
- Object: Select one or more objects from the suggestions or enter the exact object name for which you would like to derive the log details.

- Event category: Filtering events based on event categories is one of the recommended ways to get specific events.

- Actor: You can select one or more account users to filter events based on users.

- Action: Enter a relevant action in the field's search bar or select one from the suggested list of actions as necessary. You can filter events based on only one action at a time.

- Audit ID, Batch ID, & IP: Filters are applied on these fields mostly to backtrace an event's history. Enter the exact Audit ID or Batch ID or IP address to filter events as necessary.

nodes.label: Portal audit log
nodes.heading: Removing filter conditions
nodes.snippet: Applied filter conditions will be highlighted in _blue_ as in the screenshot below and to remove a specific filter condition, click the Remove button to clear it.

nodes.label: Portal audit log
nodes.heading: Exporting an audit log
nodes.snippet:
1. On the portal page, click the More options button  \> Portal administration > Audit log.
2. Click the Export button to export the audit log as a file. An email with the download link will be sent immediately to your registered email address from where you can download the file.

nodes.label: Portal overview
nodes.heading: Portal overview
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

nodes.label: Portal overview
nodes.heading: What is a portal?
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow Apps enable you to create complex, dynamic applications using visual tools and custom scripting. Businesses and non-professional developers can quickly create and deploy applications only Kissflow users can use.

With Kissflow Portals, you can create and deploy applications you can share with users outside your Kissflow account.

Portal eliminates the need to add external users to your Kissflow account. It allows you to keep business-sensitive data within your Kissflow account while creating an application for external users. It can allow external users like vendors, customers, or partners to connect and interact with applications created using Kissflow Apps. You can create applications with a wide range of possibilities by utilizing the capabilities of Kissflow Apps and integrations with third-party applications, all while maintaining the enhanced security that comes with a Kissflow account.

> Only Enterprise accounts can create portals. You can increase your Kissflow account's soft limit of 10 portals by contacting Kissflow customer support later.

nodes.label: Portal overview
nodes.heading: Common use cases for portals
nodes.snippet:
You can use portals to build applications to cater to various use cases. Below are some practical ways to use it:

- A vendor portal that you can share with external vendors that are not a part of the company.
- A helpdesk that customers of your product can use to raise and track complaints or share feedback.
- A community portal that people can use to access and share information and engage in discussions.
- A field service management portal that you can use to assign work to field service agents and manage them.

You can also refer to the dedicated Kissflow Apps documentation to learn more about Kissflow Apps and how to use them.

nodes.label: Admin actions in a process
nodes.heading: Admin actions in a process
nodes.snippet:
Admin actions in a process

The Administration section of a process gives Process admins and Data admins a single place to view every item, edit its data, and step in when an item is stuck — by reassigning, completing, or deleting it. This article covers opening the Administration section and using each of these actions.

nodes.label: Admin actions in a process
nodes.heading: Open the Administration section
nodes.snippet:
1. Navigate to the process.
2. Click the More options button at the top right.
3. Select Administration.

This opens a list of every item in the process, showing:

- Title — the item's name, as configured in the process settings.
- Created at — when the item was first created.
- Created by — the person, or guest (if submitted via a public form), who created the item.

Click the Show/hide fields button to add other fields to the list or apply filters.

media.alt: More options icon
Show/hide fields icon

nodes.label: Admin actions in a process
nodes.heading: Search for items
nodes.snippet: Use the search bar to find items quickly. Its dropdown lists every field in the process — select one, then enter a value to list only the items whose selected field matches it.

media.alt: Search bar with a field dropdown for filtering items

nodes.label: Admin actions in a process
nodes.heading: Edit an item's fields
nodes.snippet:
You can view and edit any field on an item, or reassign it to someone else.

1. Open the item.
2. Click Update field values at the top of the item form.
3. Make your edits.
4. Click Save changes.

All of these actions are recorded in the process audit log.

media.alt: Editing field values on an item and saving the changes

nodes.label: Admin actions in a process
nodes.heading: Perform bulk actions
nodes.snippet:
From the Administration section, you can reassign, mark complete, or delete several items at once — up to 100 items in a single batch.

1. Navigate to the process and click the More options button > Administration.
2. Select the items you want to act on.

Reassign items

If an item sits at a step too long — because the assigned person is unavailable or unable to act — you can reassign it to yourself or another user with the right permissions to move it forward.

An item's initiator can also reassign it, depending on the permissions granted to them.

1. Select one or more items.
2. Click Reassign.

Reassigning an item with more than one assignee invalidates the previous assignment entirely — it doesn't just add the new assignee alongside the others. For example, if you, John, and Linda are all assigned to an item and you reassign it to Abraham, Abraham becomes the sole assignee. You, John, and Linda are all removed automatically. This is different from how reassignment works for a process member, where the reassigning member is simply replaced by the new assignee and the rest stay on the item.

Items that are already completed, rejected, or withdrawn are skipped during reassignment — only items in In progress status are reassigned. Skipped items are prefixed with a yellow exclamation mark so you can identify them.

Items inside parallel branches cannot be reassigned.

Mark items as completed

Select one or more items and click Mark as completed.

Marking an item complete advances it straight to the end of the workflow, skipping every remaining step. This is useful when an item's assignee is unavailable and you need the item closed out immediately.

Delete and restore items

Select one or more items and click Delete.

Deleted items can be restored within 30 days of deletion. After that, they're permanently deleted.

To restore a deleted item:

1. Click the Deleted items tab at the top of the process.
2. Select the items you want to restore.
3. Click Restore.

media.alt: More options icon
List of process items with checkboxes for bulk selection
Reassign action selected for one or more items
Mark as completed action for selected items
Deleted items tab with items selected and the Restore action available

nodes.label: Admin actions in a process
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Process audit log
- Member actions in a process
- Configuring actions for process steps
- Process settings

nodes.label: AI-powered solution overview
nodes.heading: AI-powered solution overview
nodes.snippet:
AI-powered solution overview

A solution overview gives you a complete, AI-generated breakdown of a process in a readable, structured format that highlights how its different components come together to solve a business problem. It helps process admins and developers understand the overall structure and logic behind a process without having to manually explore every configuration detail.

media.alt: AI-powered solution overview

nodes.label: AI-powered solution overview
nodes.heading: Generate a solution overview

media.alt: More options menu with the Solution overview action
Solution overview generating for a process

nodes.label: AI-powered solution overview
nodes.heading: Sections of the solution overview
nodes.snippet:
The generated overview is organized into five sections:

- Overview — a brief description of what the process is designed to do.
- Glossary — definitions of the terms and components used in the solution, such as connected flow, form, integration, goto, parallel branch, section, and workflow, so that business users can easily interpret the rest of the overview.
- Detailed explanation — an end-to-end walkthrough of how the process works:
- Form — lists all sections and the type of information each collects, along with required and computed fields.
- Workflow — explains each step in order, showing who it's assigned to, deadlines, editable or hidden sections, and any connected flows or integrations it triggers.
- Summary — a concise, start-to-finish walkthrough of the process, highlighting key transitions, decision points, and how data moves through the flow.
- FAQ — answers to a handful of common questions a business user might have about the process, such as who initiates it, what triggers connected flows, and how permissions are set.

nodes.label: AI-powered solution overview
nodes.heading: Associated flows and integrations
nodes.snippet:
The right panel of the solution overview lists the process's connected flows, integrations, and looked-up flows. Click any of them to open the corresponding flow or integration in a new tab.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Associated flows and integrations panel in the solution overview

nodes.label: AI-powered solution overview
nodes.heading: Keep the overview up to date
nodes.snippet:
A solution overview reflects the process design at the time it was generated. If you publish changes to the process afterward — or update an integration it uses — a banner appears at the top of the overview prompting you to refresh it.

There's no separate manual regenerate control — refresh only appears once changes are actually detected, so you always know whether the overview you're reading matches the live process.

nodes.label: AI-powered solution overview
nodes.heading: Print or save as a PDF
nodes.snippet:
You can print the solution overview or save it as a PDF. Click the Print button in the top right corner of the overview and choose your desired options from your browser's print dialog.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Print button in the solution overview

nodes.label: AI-powered solution overview
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- AI solution analyzer — catch form, workflow, and permission issues before you publish.
- Connected processes overview
- Associated flows for processes
- Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process

nodes.label: AI solution analyzer
nodes.heading: AI solution analyzer
nodes.snippet:
AI solution analyzer

Solution analyzer is an AI-powered tool for process admins and developers who build and manage a solution. It reviews your form fields, workflow steps, and permissions against the intent it infers from your process design, then flags potential issues before you take the process live.

These are AI-generated findings — always review them against your process's actual requirements before acting on them.

Solution analyzer is currently available only for no-code processes. It isn't supported inside apps or portals.

nodes.label: AI solution analyzer
nodes.heading: When to run it
nodes.snippet:
We recommend running the analyzer after you finish building your process, to check that:

- Forms have the required validations.
- Workflow steps are assigned to the right people.
- Permissions match your intent.

nodes.label: AI solution analyzer
nodes.heading: Run the analyzer
nodes.snippet:
1. Open your process.
2. Click Manage > Edit process to open the Form builder.
3. Click Analyze at the top right of the builder.

The analyzer runs automatically and displays the results.

You don't have to wait around for the analysis to finish. Close the analysis window, or click Run analysis in background, and you'll get a toast notification once it's complete.

media.alt: Solution analyzer running in the process builder

nodes.label: AI solution analyzer
nodes.heading: Review the results
nodes.snippet:
If no issues are found, you'll see a success message confirming that your form, workflow, and permissions align with your process's intent.

If issues are detected, you'll see a list of observations instead.

Each observation shows:

- Title — a short description of the issue.
- Tags — whether it relates to the workflow, permissions, or form.
- Severity — Critical or Suggestion. Critical findings point to issues more likely to break the process or expose data, such as a misassigned approval step or an overly permissive field. Suggestions are best-practice recommendations that won't break the process but are still worth reviewing.
- Locate — jumps to the exact spot in the form, workflow, or permissions where the issue was found.

At the top of the results, you'll also see the total number of observations (for example, *7 observations made*) and the timestamp of the last analysis run. Use the filters to narrow the list by severity or scope.

Locate and navigate between issues

Click Locate on an observation to jump to the exact spot where it was found. The list narrows to that single observation, with previous and next controls so you can step through the remaining issues one at a time.

Click Back to return to the full list of observations.

media.alt: Solution analyzer showing a success message with no issues found
Solution analyzer showing a list of observations
Locating an issue and navigating between observations

nodes.label: AI solution analyzer
nodes.heading: Rerun the analyzer
nodes.snippet:
You can rerun the analyzer only after you make changes to your process. The timestamp of the last run stays visible in the analyzer window until you do.

Rerunning can change the results — existing issues might resolve, new ones might appear, and some might disappear entirely. Review the current observations before you click Rerun analysis, since you may not see the same results again.

nodes.label: AI solution analyzer
nodes.heading: Examples
nodes.snippet:
Workflow issue — An approval step meant for a specific role, such as a finance director, gets assigned to Everyone by mistake. Solution analyzer flags this as a critical workflow issue and recommends reassigning the step to the correct role.

Permissions issue — A field that should stay read-only after an initial entry, such as a compensation amount, gets left editable at a later step. Solution analyzer flags this as a critical permissions issue and recommends making the field read-only again.

media.alt: Solution analyzer flagging a workflow assignment issue
Solution analyzer flagging a permissions issue

nodes.label: AI solution analyzer
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
- AI-powered solution overview
- Reviewing publish changes
- Managing process members and permissions

nodes.label: Associated flows for processes
nodes.heading: Associated flows for processes
nodes.snippet:
Associated flows for processes

The Associated flows section shows how a process depends on, and is depended on by, other flows in your account — giving you a map of relationships to check before you rename fields, change permissions, or archive the process.

nodes.label: Associated flows for processes
nodes.heading: View a process's associated flows
nodes.snippet:
1. Open the process and click the Manage button at the top right.
2. Click Associated flows.

Associated flows is available only to process admins and editors, and only while the process is Live.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

There are two types of associations, shown as tabs on this page: Looked up flows and Connected flows.

media.alt: Process Manage panel with the Associated flows tile

nodes.label: Associated flows for processes
nodes.heading: Looked up flows
nodes.snippet:
Lookup fields let a process pull and display data from other flows. The Looked up flows tab shows both directions of that relationship.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Flows looked up to

Shows which flows are looking up this process to fetch and display information.

Example: If another process references details like Purchase Order IDs or Vendor Names from the current process, that process appears under Flows looked up to.

Click Configure next to a flow in this list to open this process's security settings, where lookup permissions are managed.

The Configure action is available only under Flows looked up to. To manage what the current process looks up from other flows, open the security settings of that other flow instead.

Flows looked up from

Shows the flows that the current process is looking up.

Example: If your Purchase Request process pulls Asset Types or Budget Codes from a master dataset, that dataset appears under Flows looked up from.

media.alt: Looked up flows tab showing the Flows looked up to and Flows looked up from lists

nodes.label: Associated flows for processes
nodes.heading: Connected flows
nodes.snippet:
Connected flows require the Enterprise plan.

Connected processes let actions in one process automatically create or update an item in another process. The Connected flows tab shows both directions of that relationship.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Dependent flows

Shows the connected processes that can automatically initiate new items based on items created or updated in this process.

Example: When a Purchase Request item is approved, it can automatically create an item in a connected Purchase Order process.

Source flows

Shows the connected processes that can initiate new items inside this process.

Example: A Service Request process can trigger this process to create a hardware request.

media.alt: Connected flows tab showing the Dependent flows and Source flows lists

nodes.label: Associated flows for processes
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Connected processes overview
- Connecting processes — set up a connected step, map fields, and configure conditions
- Lookup field
- Security settings for flows

nodes.label: Connected processes overview
nodes.heading: Connected processes overview
nodes.snippet:
Connected processes overview

In Kissflow, you can connect multiple processes so that actions in one process automatically initiate related items in another. When a work item in a source process reaches a step you've configured, a dependent item is created in the connected (destination) process — carrying over the field values you've mapped between them. This lets team managers and IT admins orchestrate and keep related workflows across departments in sync, without manual handoffs.

media.alt: Connected processes overview video

nodes.label: Connected processes overview
nodes.heading: Why connect processes
nodes.snippet:
- Automate item creation — when a source item reaches a configured step, a dependent item is created in the connected process automatically, cutting out manual handoffs between teams.
- Coordinate across teams — connected processes give everyone a shared, holistic view of how work moves between departments.
- Reduce errors — automating the handoff removes the chance of a step being missed or details being miscommunicated when work passes from one team to another.

What happens to the source item once its dependent item is completed — whether it's automatically unblocked and resumed, or handled another way — depends on how the connected step is configured. The example below describes the common setup, where the source item pauses at the connected step until its dependent item completes.

nodes.label: Connected processes overview
nodes.heading: Example: connecting Purchase Request and Purchase Order
nodes.snippet:
Say your organization wants purchase orders to be created automatically once a purchase request is approved.

1. An employee submits a request in the Purchase Request process, filling in details like the software needed, quantity, and justification for the purchase.
2. The request moves through its approval stages. Once approved, it reaches a step named Ready for order.
3. Because Ready for order is configured as a connected step, a dependent item is automatically created in the Purchase Order process as soon as the source item reaches it. Field values you've mapped between the two processes — such as software name, quantity, and cost — carry over to the new item.
4. The procurement team picks up the new item in Purchase Order, reviews and finalizes the details, and sends it to the supplier.
5. Once the dependent item in Purchase Order is completed, the source item in Purchase Request resumes and moves on to its next step.

nodes.label: Connected processes overview
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Connecting processes — set up a connected step, map fields, and configure conditions.

- Admin actions in a process — find the source and dependent items linked to any item.

nodes.label: Connecting processes
nodes.heading: Connecting processes
nodes.snippet:
Connecting processes

Connecting processes lets a step in one process (the source) automatically create and link an item in another process (the connected process) — for example, automatically creating a Purchase Order item when a Purchase Request is approved. This article covers adding the connection, mapping fields between the two processes, and viewing connected items.

For a walkthrough of why and when to connect processes, see Connected processes - overview.

nodes.label: Connecting processes
nodes.heading: Connect a process
nodes.snippet:
1. Click Manage at the top right of your process home page, then click Edit process.
2. In the Workflow tab, click the Add button at the point in the workflow where you want the connection, and select Run a process.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

3. In the panel that opens on the right:
- Enter a name and, optionally, a description for the step.
- Select the process you want to connect to. Items are initiated in this process whenever the source item reaches this step.
- Map fields — map fields between the connected processes so the source item's field values are inherited by the dependent item(s). You can also apply functions or expressions while mapping a field.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

To map fields between a table in the source process and a table in the connected process, click Add a table, select the table, and add the fields you want to map.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

These field types can't be mapped: Attachment, Smart attachment, Image, Signature, Aggregation, Remote lookup, Lookup, JSON, Object, Object list, and String list.

- Set conditions (optional) for creating dependent items in the connected process. Only items that match the conditions enter the connected step.
4. Click Go live to publish the changes.

media.alt: Add step icon
Adding a Run a process step to a workflow
Field mapping panel for a connected process step
Mapping fields between tables in the source and connected processes

nodes.label: Connecting processes
nodes.heading: View connected items
nodes.snippet: In the process's Administration section, process admins can find the source and dependent items of any item from the Connected item(s) tab.

nodes.label: Connecting processes
nodes.heading: Initiate multiple dependent items from a table
nodes.snippet:
Instead of creating a single dependent item, you can initiate one dependent item per row in a source item's table — for example, one dependent item per row in a table of line items.

1. In the connected step's configuration panel, toggle on Initiate items from table entries.
2. Select the table whose rows should each initiate a dependent item.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

You can select only one table to initiate multiple dependent items.

3. Optionally, click Add a filter to set conditions — only rows that match the filter initiate a dependent item.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Once this toggle is on, the selected table's fields also become available for field mapping.

media.alt: Toggling Initiate items from table entries and selecting a table
Adding a filter to a table used for initiating dependent items

nodes.label: Document templates
nodes.heading: Document templates
nodes.snippet:
Document templates

A document template lets you automate the generation of process-related PDFs. For example, as an HR manager managing the Recruitment process, you can create a document template that generates offer letters automatically once an interview is closed.

You must be a Process Admin or a Process Developer to create a document template.

nodes.label: Document templates
nodes.heading: Creating a document template
nodes.snippet:
You can create a document template from scratch or generate a first draft with Kissflow AI.

Create from scratch

1. Navigate to the process for which you want to create a document template.
2. Click Manage > Document templates > Create template > Create from scratch.
3. Enter a name and description for your document template.
4. Click Create from scratch to open the template in the editor and build it manually.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Generate using AI

1. Navigate to the process for which you want to create a document template.
2. Click Manage > Document templates > Create template > Generate using AI.
3. Enter a document template name — for example, Employee Offer Letter or Purchase Order.
4. Select a language for the template.
5. Click Generate. Kissflow AI uses these inputs as a prompt to create the first draft, called Draft 1.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

AI regeneration to enhance drafts

Refine a draft by adding more information to it, then click Regenerate to get the changes reflected in a new draft.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

You can navigate to any existing draft and use it as the base for regenerating a new draft with the changes you want.

A draft can only be used to regenerate within one hour of its creation. For example, if draft 7 was generated on January 19 at 3:45 PM, you can use it to regenerate a new draft only before January 19, 4:45 PM. You can also create a maximum of 10 drafts per document template.

Finalizing a template draft

Once you're satisfied with a draft, open it and click Use this draft at the top right of the page. The draft is transferred to the document template editor, where you can further customize the document.

nodes.label: Document templates
nodes.heading: Editing a document template
nodes.snippet:
The template editor supports the standard formatting options you'd expect from any text editor, plus support for inserting process fields, page breaks, images, and form tables.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

This feature is built on the Jinja templating engine. If you're familiar with Jinja, you can write advanced conditions or loops to extend what your template can do — see the Jinja documentation for the full syntax.

Inserting process fields

Insert a system or process field in either of these ways:

- Type two curly braces without a space between them ({{) to see the list of fields used in the current process, then select the one you want to insert.
- Drag and drop a field from the left panel onto the template editor.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Supported fields

Only form tables and the following field types are supported inside document templates:

- Text
- Text area
- Number
- Currency
- Email
- Sequence number
- Date
- DateTime
- User
- Dropdown
- Yes/No
- Radio button
- Image
- Aggregation
- Signature
- Scanner

Unsupported fields

These field types are not supported inside document templates:

- Multi-select dropdown
- Checkbox
- Lookup
- Remote lookup
- Attachment
- Smart attachment
- Geolocation
- Checklist
- Rating
- Slider
- Rich text
- Button

Inserting page breaks

Add as many pages as you want in your document. Click the Page break button in the toolbar to insert one.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Adding a table

Insert a form table created as part of your process into your document template the same way you'd insert a field — by dragging and dropping it onto the template editor.

Dragging a form table onto the editor adds a line of code above and below it. These lines are added by Kissflow — don't remove or modify them, or the template will stop working correctly.

Enter the column header names, and use double curly braces to insert a process field as a column's value.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

You can't drag and drop a field while setting a value for a specific field in a table — use double curly braces instead.

Inserting an image

Add an image in any of these ways:

1. From your desktop — drag and drop an image file from your computer into the template editor.
2. From a web page — right-click the image and select Copy image address. In the template editor, click the Insert image icon, right-click and paste the image address, then click Done. Hover over the image to format, resize, or delete it.
3. Copy and paste — copy an image from an existing document (for example, in Microsoft Word or Google Docs) and paste it directly into the template editor.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

You can also copy and paste text content from any external source into the editor.

Inserting a header and footer

A header appears at the top of a PDF document and a footer at the bottom. Both are typically used for information that repeats on every page, such as the document name, headings, page numbers, images, or the date.

1. Click Header & Footer in the editor to open a separate editor window for the header and footer. The height of the header and footer is set by the top and bottom margins of the base canvas — change these using the Margins button. Inside the header/footer editor, you can further configure a top and bottom margin, up to a maximum of 400px. The base canvas also determines the header and footer's left and right margins, and these can't be changed from inside the editor.
2. Add content to the header and footer:
- Textual content
- Images and static tables
- Page number and page count

You can't insert a child table in the header or footer.

3. Once you've added the content you need, click Save, then click Preview to see how the document template will render.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

nodes.label: Document templates
nodes.heading: Formatting currency, number, and date fields

nodes.label: Document templates
nodes.heading: Customizing your page
nodes.snippet:
Use the Page setup option to configure the basic layout and appearance of your document.

Page orientation

Choose Portrait or Landscape for your pages.

Page color

Set the background color of your pages.

Margins

Configure your page margins:

- Auto — automatically sets all four margins (top, bottom, left, right) to 92px.
- Custom — lets you set each margin manually.

Click Apply to save your changes.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

nodes.label: Document templates
nodes.heading: Previewing a document template

nodes.label: Document templates
nodes.heading: Associating a document template with a process connector
nodes.snippet:
Automate PDF generation by connecting your process to a document template through the process connector.

1. In the connector, select the Generate a PDF document step.
2. Set up a connection with the required account.
3. Choose a process, then select the relevant process instance ID.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

4. Select the document template to map to the process.
5. Enter a title for the document, and choose whether to attach it to a field on the process form. If yes, select the attachment field to use.

You can refresh fields to see any recently added form fields. You can also map fields from the trigger step or from earlier action steps — these values are filled in automatically once the action runs.

6. Click Next.
7. Add a Kissflow Email connector step to share the generated PDF as an email attachment.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

8. Fill in the email fields, and under Attachments > Fields > Generate PDF, select the key field.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

9. Toggle the integration ON to activate it.

- English
- Italian (Italiano)
- Russian (Русский)
- German (Deutsch)

- Romanian (Română)

nodes.label: FAQ - Connected processes
nodes.heading: FAQ - Connected processes
nodes.snippet:
Why can't I see the process I want to connect to?

You can only pick from processes where you have admin access — processes you don't administer won't appear in the list.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Why can't I initiate dependent items for all the table rows in my source item at once?

Kissflow initiates up to 100 dependent items per source item, based on the number of matching table rows. If your source item's table has more than 100 rows, only 100 dependent items are created — the rest are not initiated.

I'm getting an error while creating dependent items in the connected process. What could cause this?

A couple of things to check:

- Unmet conditions — the source item, or the specific table rows you expected to trigger dependent items, may not satisfy the conditions configured for creating them.
- Assignee on the connected process' first step — if that step's assignee is set to Created by, Modified by, or Initiator rather than an actual user, it can prevent items from being created correctly. Assign a real user (for example, Stephen Strange) instead.

If neither of these explains the error you're seeing, contact Kissflow support with the item ID and a screenshot of the error.

Can I initiate dependent items from the same source item more than once?

Not while the earlier set of dependent items is still in progress or already completed. Moving the source item back to the flow-connected step doesn't automatically clear the existing connection — Kissflow blocks a second round of dependent items until the prior ones are resolved, to avoid creating duplicates.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

My source item has been stuck in the flow-connected step for a long time. How do I move it along?

You have two options:

- Complete the dependent items — once all dependent items reach the connected process' Completed step, the source item automatically advances to its next step.
- Contact the connected process' admin — if you don't have access to the connected process yourself, ask its admin to complete the dependent items on your behalf.

nodes.label: Managing process members and permissions
nodes.heading: Managing process members and permissions
nodes.snippet:
Managing process members and permissions

Every member added to a process is assigned a role that determines what they can do — from initiating items to editing the workflow itself. This article covers the available roles, what each one can do, and how to add members, change their roles, and manage their permissions.

Whoever creates a process becomes its Process Admin by default. From there, only a Process Admin can add other members and assign them a role.

nodes.label: Managing process members and permissions
nodes.heading: Roles in a process
nodes.snippet:
Process Admin

A Process Admin has the highest privilege in a process. They can perform any operation on the process, including adding, removing, or modifying the roles of other members. As a Process Admin, you can:

- Edit the form and workflow.
- Assign a role to a member while adding them to the process.
- Add Process Admins, Developers, Data Admins, Report Admins, and Members at any point in the process's lifecycle.
- Remove any member, regardless of their role.
- Grant or revoke permissions for a member.
- Share reports with members or with anyone outside the process.
- Manage process settings and perform bulk actions and view the process administration table.

Data Admin

A Data Admin can view, edit, and delete process data, and create reports.

Data Admin is available on the Enterprise plan only.

As a Data Admin, you can:

- View, edit, and delete process data.
- View all reports, open a process form from a specific report, and access the process's metrics report.
- Perform bulk actions and view the process administration table.
- Initiate items (optional — a Process Admin must grant this separately).

A Data Admin cannot add members to a process.

Report Admin

A Report Admin can create, edit, and share reports in a process, including reports created by other members. They can delete only the reports they created themselves.

Report Admin is available on the Enterprise plan only.

As a Report Admin, you can:

- View, create, edit, and share any report in the process, and delete reports you've created.
- Open a process form and its comments from a report.
- Keep access to your reports even if your role is later downgraded — unless a Process Admin explicitly revokes it.
- Initiate items (optional — a Process Admin must grant this separately).

A Report Admin cannot add members to a process.

Developer

A Developer builds and modifies a process. As a Developer, you can:

- Edit the form and workflow.
- Access and edit the process, and initiate items.
- Add initiators to the process while editing or modifying the workflow.
- Create, edit, share, and delete reports you've created (report creation is optional — a Process Admin must grant it separately).
- Keep access to your reports even if your role is later downgraded — unless a Process Admin explicitly revokes it.
- Publish the process (optional — a Process Admin must grant this separately, from the More permissions menu).

Member

A Member can access a process and initiate items. As a Member, you can:

- Initiate items in the process.
- Create reports if a Process Admin grants that permission, and access reports you've created or that were shared with you.

nodes.label: Managing process members and permissions
nodes.heading: Permissions by role
nodes.snippet:
| Permission | Process Admin | Data Admin | Report Admin | Developer | Member |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Access process | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Initiate an item | ✓ | Optional | Optional | ✓ | ✓ |
| Manage process settings | ✓ | X | X | ✓ | X |
| Add or remove members | ✓ | X | X | X | X |
| Edit process | ✓ | X | X | ✓ | X |
| Perform bulk actions | ✓ | ✓ | X | X | X |
| View audit log | ✓ | X | X | X | X |
| Archive and delete process | ✓ | X | X | X | X |
| Duplicate a process | ✓ | X | X | X | X |
| Create reports | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Optional | Optional |
| Share reports | ✓ | Own reports only | ✓ | Own reports only | Own reports only |
| Delete reports | ✓ | Own reports only | Own reports only | Own reports only | — |
| View all reports | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | X | X |
| View Metrics report | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | X | X |
| View process administration table | ✓ | ✓ | X | X | X |
| Modify item | ✓ | ✓ | X | X | X |
| Delete item | ✓ | ✓ | X | X | X |
| Go live (publish a process) | ✓ | X | X | Optional | X |

nodes.label: Managing process members and permissions
nodes.heading: Add a member to a process
nodes.snippet:
You can share a process with any flow member or group in your organization.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

1. Navigate to the process you want to add members to.
2. Click Manage > Share.
3. Click + Add members.
4. Type the name or email address of the user or group under Select users and groups.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

5. Select a role from the Role dropdown.

Data Admin and Report Admin are available on the Enterprise plan only. Reach out to Kissflow support to upgrade your plan and enable these roles.

6. Click Add.

Only account Super Admins can invite an external user who isn't part of your organization directly from the Share settings screen — a Process Admin without Super Admin access won't see this option. If you have access, type the external user's email address, then click the Invite link that appears below it to send the invite.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Share settings screen for adding members to a process
Adding users and groups in the Share settings screen
Invite link shown for an external user's email address

nodes.label: Managing process members and permissions
nodes.heading: Change a member's role
nodes.snippet:
A member loses their current privileges when downgraded to a role with fewer of them. They may still be able to access reports they created or were given access to before the change — you can choose to revoke that access as part of the role change.

1. Navigate to the process, then click Manage > Share.
2. Click the More options button beside the member's name and select Change role.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

3. Select the new role from the list.
4. Read the alert and confirm your action.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

5. Click Change role to apply the new role while keeping the member's existing report access, or Revoke report access & Change to also remove access to reports they created or accessed under their previous role.

This choice only appears when the new role would reduce the member's report access. If it wouldn't, confirming the role change applies it directly.

media.alt: More options icon
Change role option in a member's More options menu
Alert message shown when changing a member's role

nodes.label: Managing process members and permissions
nodes.heading: Grant additional permissions to a member
nodes.snippet:
Use this to give a member a permission outside their role's normal scope — for example, letting a Member create reports, or letting a Developer publish the process.

1. Navigate to the process, then click Manage > Share.
2. Click the More options button beside the member's name.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

3. Under More permissions, select the checkbox for each permission you want to grant. Clear a checkbox to remove a permission you'd previously granted.

media.alt: More options icon
More options menu open for a member, showing the More permissions section

nodes.label: Managing process members and permissions
nodes.heading: Remove a member from a process
nodes.snippet:
A removed member keeps access to reports they created or accessed while part of the process, unless you revoke that access as part of the removal.

1. Navigate to the process, then click Manage > Share.
2. Click the More options button beside the member you want to remove.
3. Click Remove member.
4. Read the alert and confirm your action.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

5. Click Remove member to remove them while keeping their report access, or Revoke report access & Remove to also remove access to reports they created or accessed.

media.alt: More options icon
Alert message shown when removing a member from a process

nodes.label: Process audit log
nodes.heading: Process audit log
nodes.snippet:
Process audit log

The audit log stores the history of all activity related to a process, and lets Process Admins search flow-level and item-level changes made by users in the process.

The audit log is available only to Process Admins. If you don't see it, check with your Process Admin.

nodes.label: Process audit log
nodes.heading: Open the audit log
nodes.snippet:
1. Open the process and click the Manage button.
2. Click Audit log.
3. Go to Flow-level logs to see changes to the process itself (settings, publishing, permissions), or Item-level logs to see changes to individual items within it.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

The sorting, filtering, and export instructions below apply to both views. Each entry shows the following columns:

- Audit ID — a unique ID the system generates automatically for every recorded event.
- Time — when the action happened.
- Acted by — the user who performed the action.
- Event — the kind of activity that took place, for example Process published, Item created, or Item deleted.
- Object — the resource that was changed.
- Event category — a common name for a set of events, useful for filtering down to similar events. Examples include Process Administration and Process Settings.
- Platform — the device or OS the event was recorded from.
- IP — the IP address the event was recorded from.
- Batch ID — a unique ID the system generates automatically for a group of related events. Unlike Audit ID, every event in the same batch shares the same Batch ID.

media.alt: Process manage menu with the Audit log option

nodes.label: Process audit log
nodes.heading: Sort the log
nodes.snippet:
1. Click the down-arrow beside the Time column header.
2. Select Ascending or Descending to sort the log by time.

Sorting is available only on the Time column. The down-arrow on other columns lets you pin or unpin them instead, so key details stay visible while you scroll horizontally.

nodes.label: Process audit log
nodes.heading: Search a filter
nodes.snippet:
Some filters — Audit ID, Batch ID, and IP — search by exact value:

1. Click the down-arrow beside the filter.
2. Enter the exact value in the search area that appears and apply it.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Search area that opens for the Audit ID, Batch ID, and IP filters

nodes.label: Process audit log
nodes.heading: Filter the log
nodes.snippet:
You can filter audit log events on the following fields:

- Audit period — choose Last 7 days, Last 30 days, Last 90 days, or a Custom date range.

Last 30 days may not be available on every account. If you don't see it, contact Kissflow support to check whether extended log retention can be enabled for your account.

- Object — select one or more objects from the suggestions, or enter the exact object name.
- Event category — filtering by event category is one of the recommended ways to narrow down results. If you're not sure which category to pick, search for a flow name like "process" and the field lists every category relevant to it.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

- Acted by — select one or more account users to filter events by who performed them.
- Event — enter or select one action from the suggested list. This filter accepts only one value at a time.
- Audit ID, Batch ID, and IP — enter the exact value to filter events, mainly useful to backtrace a specific event's history.

Each applied filter shows a control to clear it, and you can click Clear all to reset every filter at once.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Event category filter listing categories relevant to a flow
Audit log with filters applied and a clear control on each

nodes.label: Process audit log
nodes.heading: Export the audit log
nodes.snippet:
You can export up to 90 days of the audit log at a time.

1. Open the process and click Manage > Audit log.
2. Go to Flow-level logs or Item-level logs, depending on which log you want to export — see Open the audit log above for what each view covers.
3. Click the Export button and confirm the export.

Export runs as a background job. Once it finishes, you'll receive an email with the download link at your registered email address — this can take a few minutes rather than being instant.

nodes.heading: What's next

nodes.label: Process settings
nodes.heading: Process settings
nodes.snippet:
Process settings

When you create a process, you become its Process Admin. Process settings let you control how the process behaves — from the form title and submission locking to notifications, public forms, sequence numbers, and multilingual support.

To open process settings, click the Manage button on the process, then Settings.

nodes.label: Process settings
nodes.heading: General settings
nodes.snippet:
The General tab covers the form title, submission locking, and the process's quick link.

Configure the form title

The form title is the name given to each item as it moves through the process workflow.

1. On the General tab, click the Add button (+).
2. Choose the system fields or form fields you want, and arrange them in the order you want the title to read.
3. Click Save.

Based on this format, titles are generated automatically and shown at the top of each process form.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Lock submissions

When you're making significant changes to a process, you may want to stop members from starting new items while you work.

1. On the General tab, check the Lock submissions checkbox.
2. Click Save.

Locking submissions restricts members from creating and submitting new items. Items already in progress continue through the workflow as normal.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Share the quick link

Share the process's quick link with members so they can start a new item directly. Logged-in members go straight to the form; anyone not logged in is directed to the login page first.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

nodes.label: Process settings
nodes.heading: Notification settings
nodes.snippet:
On the Notification settings tab, enable or disable notifications, preview them, and customize the templates for the long and short notifications sent to users for the different events in your process.

Learn more about process notifications.

nodes.label: Process settings
nodes.heading: Public form settings
nodes.snippet:
On the Public form settings tab, you can enable or disable the public form for a process.

- To enable it, turn on Public form URL, then copy the URL and share it with anyone outside your organization.
- To disable it, turn off Public form URL. External users will no longer be able to start new items from the public form, though items already in progress continue through the workflow.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Learn more about configuring a public form.

nodes.label: Process settings
nodes.heading: Sequence numbers
nodes.snippet:
The Sequence numbers tab lists every configured sequence number prefix for this process.

- Total sequence number — the count of active prefixes for your sequence number field.
- Prefix — the prefix configured in the form. A dynamic field, such as Department, produces a different sequence for each value it generates.
- Current number — the current value for each prefix.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Each prefix row has two actions, and there are two more that apply to all prefixes at once:

- Reset — enter a number for that prefix. The prefix's current value is set to the number you enter, so the *next* item raised for that prefix gets that number plus one.
- Remove — deletes that prefix entirely. The next item raised for it starts a fresh sequence from 1.
- Reset all — enter a number to apply to every active prefix at once, the same way Reset does for a single prefix. This does not delete any prefixes.
- Remove all — deletes every active prefix for the field. The next item for each one starts a fresh sequence from 1.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Reset and Remove are separate actions. Reset only changes the current value of an existing prefix — use Remove (or Remove all) if you want to delete a prefix so it starts over from 1.

nodes.label: Process settings
nodes.heading: Multilingual settings
nodes.snippet:
Multilingual settings let process and board admins present workflow forms in multiple languages by translating form labels, either manually or with AI translation.

For example, if you've built a form in English but want to support Chinese speakers:

1. Select the language.
2. Download the form labels.
3. Manually translate the labels, or use AI translation.
4. Import the translated labels back into the form.

Once set up, the form automatically switches to Chinese for users who've selected Chinese in their profile settings, while English speakers continue to see the English version.

Multilingual settings are also available for Boards and for apps built on the Kissflow Apps builder, not only standalone Processes. Only languages supported at the platform level can be used within forms.

Learn more about multilingual settings in forms.

nodes.label: Process settings
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Admin actions in a process
- Managing process members and permissions
- Configuring a public form
- Multilingual settings in forms

nodes.label: Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
nodes.heading: Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
nodes.snippet:
Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process

Process Admins and Developers manage a process through its full lifecycle — from publishing it live, to editing it later, to duplicating, archiving, or deleting it when it's no longer needed.

nodes.label: Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
nodes.heading: Publish a process
nodes.snippet:
1. Finish designing the process form and workflow.
2. Run the AI Solution analyzer to check for potential errors in your design. This step is optional but recommended.
3. Click Go live at the bottom right of the process editor.

Going live publishes the process in one step — all of your latest changes to the form, workflow, and permissions go live together.

The previous version of a process can't be restored once you publish. Review your changes carefully before clicking Go live.

Changes you make in the process editor are saved automatically as you work. If you've made unwanted changes before publishing, you can ask another Process Admin to open the process and discard your changes while they take over editing.

Changing a field's type isn't recommended once the field already has data in it. If the field existed before the process's last publish, Kissflow restricts which type changes are allowed to avoid data conflicts when you publish again.

media.alt: Go live button at the bottom right of the process editor

nodes.label: Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
nodes.heading: Edit a process
nodes.snippet:
1. Click Manage at the top right of the process.
2. Click Edit process.

If another Process Admin or Developer is currently editing the process, you'll see a message letting you know.

You can review their changes and choose one of the following:

1. Discard their changes and take over editing — removes any unpublished changes made by the other user, so you start fresh.
2. Save their changes and take over editing — saves the other user's changes first, then hands editing over to you.

If you'd rather not interrupt their work, ask the other user to publish their changes instead.

media.alt: Manage menu open with the Edit process option
Message showing that another user is currently editing the process

nodes.label: Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
nodes.heading: Duplicate a process
nodes.snippet:
Duplicating a process creates a copy with the same fields, sections, tables, workflow, and permissions as the original. Items and data are not carried over.

1. Click the More options button and select Duplicate.
2. The new process is named Copy of <original process name> by default — you can rename it later.
3. Choose whether to duplicate the original process's reports.
4. Click Duplicate.

A newly duplicated process is typically locked for submissions, so no one can initiate items until you've had a chance to check its reports and settings. A Process Admin can unlock it for submissions when it's ready.

Integrations associated with the original process aren't duplicated. Duplicate those integrations separately.

media.alt: More options icon
Duplicate process dialog with a name field and a checkbox to duplicate reports

nodes.label: Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
nodes.heading: Archive a process
nodes.snippet:
Archive a process you don't need active anymore. Its data is retained, but no one can view or act on its items while it's archived.

1. Click the More options button at the top right of the process.
2. Click Archive.

Process Admins can access the audit log for an archived process and unarchive or delete it.

To find an archived process you manage, go to Explore > Managed by me > Processes, then filter the status to Archived. To find archived processes managed by others, use the Others tab instead.

Unarchive a process

1. Go to Explore > Managed by me (or Others) > Processes, and filter the status to Archived.
2. Select the process and click Unarchive.

media.alt: More options icon
Explore panel filtered to archived processes
Archived process list with the Unarchive action

nodes.label: Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
nodes.heading: Delete a process
nodes.snippet:
A process must be archived before it can be deleted. From the archived process, click the More options button and select Delete.

Deleting a process removes it and all of its data permanently — this can't be undone. Before you delete, review who the process is shared with and check for any other processes connected to it.

nodes.label: Publishing, editing, duplicating, archiving, and deleting a process
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Reviewing publish changes
- Running the AI Solution analyzer
- Managing process members and permissions
- Process settings

nodes.label: Reviewing publish changes
nodes.heading: Reviewing publish changes
nodes.snippet:
Reviewing publish changes

The Review changes feature gives Process admins and Developers complete visibility into every update made to a process before it goes live. Instead of manually comparing versions or tracking edits across sessions, you get a clear, consolidated summary of what changed.

media.alt: Review changes in Kissflow Process

nodes.label: Reviewing publish changes
nodes.heading: Open Review changes
nodes.snippet:
Inside the process editor, you can review changes in two ways:

1. While editing — Click Review changes at the top-right corner at any time during your editing session, without needing to publish.
