- Choose who should be assigned when items are sent back to the step. Learn more about send back actions.

- If you click Manage field permission, you’ll be able to see what fields are editable, read-only, and hidden at that step. Learn more about managing field permissions.

> Note:
>
> When you add a new step to an existing process workflow, only items that haven’t reached that step yet will follow the updated workflow.

nodes.label: Making a process workflow
nodes.heading: Adding a parallel branch
nodes.snippet:
Parallel branches serve two use cases. One is when you want two or more steps to happen at the same time. Each branch moves forward independently of what is happening in the other branches. For example, many of the steps in employee onboarding can happen simultaneously, and they aren't dependent on other steps to finish first. However, the workflow will not proceed further until all the branches have finished. Learn more about simultaneous tasks.

The second use case is to create two separate branches that are dependent on data in the form. If certain data is triggered, the item goes down one side. If not, it goes down the other. To configure this, click the branch, and under the When should items move to this branch? drop-down, select Based on condition, and then write the condition for when that branch should happen.

When you add a branch, you first give the entire branch a name. Then you can click the Add button to add steps to that branch. Steps in the branch can be collapsed or expanded.

Learn when to use the all assignees option vs a parallel branch.

nodes.label: Making a process workflow
nodes.heading: Adding a Goto
nodes.snippet:
A Goto allows you to hop between workflow steps when certain conditions are met. You can jump forward or backward within the workflow steps. For example, if someone is filling out a travel request but doesn’t require hotel accommodations, plane tickets, or a travel advance, you can skip other steps and go straight to a final approval.

To configure the Goto, enter the conditions under which it should fire and then enter the name of the step the item should go to.

nodes.label: Making a process workflow
nodes.heading: Running (connecting) a process
nodes.snippet: You can connect multiple processes to automatically initiate related items based on actions taken in a parent/source process. Learn more about connected processes.

nodes.label: Making a process workflow
nodes.heading: AI-suggested workflow and steps
nodes.snippet: You can create an entire process workflow and individual steps with the help of AI. Learn more about creating AI-suggested workflow and steps.

nodes.label: Making a process workflow
nodes.heading: Moving, copying and deleting a step
nodes.snippet:
Click the More options button on a specific process step and,

- Move the step - Click Move and paste it in a desired location.
- Copy and paste the step - Click Copy and paste it in a desired location.
- Delete the step.

nodes.label: Making a process workflow
nodes.heading: Exporting a workflow
nodes.snippet: Click the Export button at the bottom left, and select the format to export.

nodes.label: Send back actions
nodes.heading: Send back actions
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

You can select who should be assigned when an item is sent back to a step in a process workflow.

nodes.label: Send back actions
nodes.heading: Assign to approvers
nodes.snippet: When an item is sent back to a step, it will be assigned only to the user who previously approved it at that step. This ensures that the same person who earlier approved the item is responsible for re-evaluating it.

nodes.label: Send back actions
nodes.heading: Assign to original assignees
nodes.snippet:
When an item is sent back to a step, it will be assigned to all users and groups originally set as assignees for the step.

- Let’s say User A, User B, and Group C are assignees of a step. When an item is sent back to this step, all three will be assigned. This ensures that the task is reintroduced to the broader pool of assignees for reconsideration.

- Let’s say a new user (User D) is added to a group (Group C) that is set as assignee for a step. When an item is sent back to this step, User D will also be assigned along with the other group members in Group C.

- Let’s say User A reassigned an item to User E (who is not an original assignee in the step), and User E approves the item. Later when the item is sent back to this step, User A, User B, and Group C will be assigned (not User E).

Regardless of how many times the item is sent back to the step, only the original assignees (User A, User B, and Group C) will be assigned.

- Let’s say Round Robin or Least Load is selected as an assignment preference and User A gets assigned in the run time. Now, when an item is sent back to this step, only User A will be assigned even though the assignee pool consists of User A, User B, and Group C.

nodes.label: Simulating a workflow
nodes.heading: Simulating a workflow
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Simulating a workflow lets you do a mock initiation of your process workflow and check whether its path works well as expected before publishing it live. You can also reset the simulated data and execute the simulation again until you achieve the desired results.

nodes.label: Simulating a workflow
nodes.heading: Simulating a workflow
nodes.snippet:
1. Click the Simulate button at the bottom of the process editor to preview how your workflow renders in real-time.
1. You can simulate a workflow from the Form, Workflow, or Permissions tab as you wish. The workflow steps are listed on the left side of the screen when you are in the Simulation mode.
2. The Start label denotes the beginning of the workflow and the Completed label denotes the completion of the workflow respectively.
3. Navigate through every step and enter values in the form fields to see how the process performs. You can view the form's web or mobile version by toggling the button at the top.

2. Click the Submit button at the bottom to advance to the next step. As you complete the steps, they will be tagged as Checked in green. The steps you skip will not have any tag associated with it.
3. Review the integrations listed, if any, for every step you navigate under the Triggered integrations section to your right.

> Note:
>
> To view the integrations triggered at each step during the workflow simulation, you'll need to be on our Enterprise plan. If you're not on this plan, feel free to reach out to our support team for assistance with upgrading.

nodes.label: Simulating a workflow
nodes.heading: Impersonating workflow simulation
nodes.snippet:
In our _Enterprise_ plan, you can also change the initiator of the workflow and review the altered workflow path based on the initiator's scope in the process. You are basically simulating the workflow as another member in your process by changing the initiator.

Select a process member from the Simulate workflow as dropdown to change the initiator. The process members will be listed here and as an Admin, you can change the initiator and check if the workflow path renders as planned for the chosen user and their mapped roles and responsibilities. The data entered in the form for testing will be automatically reset when you change the initiator so click Yes, Change initiator to give your consent and proceed.

Think of a Leave approval process to understand how process simulation works. The two key players in this workflow are the Employee and the Manager, hence the two different initiators would also be the Employee and the Manager respectively.

If the initiator is an Employee, the path of the workflow would be as follows:

1. Initiate leave request
2. Manager's approval
3. Receive leave status

If the initiator is a Manager, the path of the workflow would be as follows:

1. Ask for more input from Employee
2. Approve leave
3. Update leave balance
4. Notify leave status

As you can see, the paths these workflows take are different, and hence, the need to simulate and test them becomes very significant.

nodes.label: Simulating a workflow
nodes.heading: Resetting data used for simulation
nodes.snippet: You may have a lot of test data while you build your process, as you would simulate the workflow as many times as you want until you achieve the desired results. Click the Reset data button to delete the data stored in your process form as part of your simulation.

nodes.label: Application roles
nodes.heading: Application roles
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

nodes.label: Application roles
nodes.heading: Creating functional roles for accessing an application
nodes.snippet: Individuals or app-level user groups, be it in-app (within the same app) or external (portal apps), representing business units, departments, or divisions that will interact with the live app, are known as roles. Each role can be assigned access permissions to process, board, and dataform that apply to all its users.

nodes.label: Application roles
nodes.heading: Adding a role to an application
nodes.snippet:
Inside the app builder, click the Roles option on the left navigation bar. You can create an in-app role or link a role from an external app.
In the Roles page, you will find both In-app and Linked roles to the left and the workflows in your app on the right. From this space, you can share workflows with roles and review the permissions that have been granted.
You can manage permissions for Dataforms and their corresponding views and reports , Boards and corresponding views and reports, Processes and their reports, Analytics reports, Decision tables, and External data objects.

Creating an in-app role

In a new app, you will have two in-app roles by default Admin and User. However, you can create any number of new roles based on your needs.

1. Click \+ New role.

2. Provide a role name and description. Set the home page and navigation bar for users with these roles to see when the app gets published.

The home page and navigation are always set as default. You can change this to a page or navigation you've created. To do this, click the Edit icon near the role's name.

3. Click Create to save the role. To add users and groups to the roles, go to Manage > Roles and click Manage members to add members to the role you want.

nodes.label: Application roles
nodes.heading: Linking a role from an external app
nodes.snippet:
You can link a role from another app in your Kissflow account to your current one. This lets you assign specific workflow tasks to users in that role without granting them full access to your app's data.

For example, imagine an app managed by C-suite executives in your organization contains a workflow that needs input from an Engineering Manager.

- The problem: You can't add the manager to the C-Suite App, as it contains confidential data.

- The solution: You know the manager has the "Manager" role in the "Engineering App." You can link that "Manager" role from the Engineering App directly to the specific task in your C-Suite workflow.

This way, the Engineering Manager is assigned only the dataform they need to complete, while the rest of the C-Suite App remains secure.

1. Click \+ New role > Linked role

2. Select the external app and the roles from the dropdown menus.

3. Link the roles.
Now you can view the roles from the Linked tab in the left panel.

nodes.label: Application roles
nodes.heading: Editing roles and permissions
nodes.snippet:
You can manage permissions for both in-app and linked roles from this tab. On the right, you will find all the workflows in your app and their corresponding child flows, such as views and reports. Using radio buttons, you can share main workflows such as dataforms, boards, processes, and the views and reports right from this page.

In-app roles

1. The new roles you create and the existing ones will be shown here to the left. Click a role on the left to select it and you can view the permissions assigned the selected role on the right.

2. Click the radio buttons to manage permissions for working with processes, boards, datasets, and lists.

3. When you click on a permission, it will be auto-saved and the workflow will be shared with the selected role instantly.

4. If you have previously shared a workflow using its dedicated share button, those permissions will reflect here. Any changes you make here will override the previously assigned permission. For example, if you have given Read-only permission for the User role through the dataform's share icon, you can change it from the Roles tab and this will override the previously given Read-only permission.

> Note: Permissions are inherited when a workflow (dataform, board, process) is shared with a role. Learn more about how inheritance works.

External app roles

You cannot modify an external app’s role directly from the source app. Instead, you must edit the roles on the external app's in-app page.

To view and grant permissions, select a linked role from the left panel and select a workflow on the right. The permissions granted for all data shared across various data modules in the app will be listed here.

Limitations in sharing workflows with linked roles

The following are the limitations in sharing workflows with roles linked from external apps:

Dataforms - any kind of permission cannot be granted for accessing a dataform. However, dataform views (all except sheet view) and reports can be shared with linked roles.

Boards and processes - Can be shared with Initiate permission but giving Manage permission is not allowed for board and process items.

External data objects and decision tables - cannot be shared with linked roles.

nodes.label: Application roles
nodes.heading: Deleting roles
nodes.snippet:
You can use the search bar to find a specific role if you have several roles in your app.

For in-app roles, you can click the Delete button , while for external apps, you can use the Delink button .

nodes.label: Role permissions
nodes.heading: Role permissions
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Kissflow Apps allows you to share your process form, board form, dataform, dataset, and list with different roles in your application and to external apps and assign them unique access permission.

> Note: Manage permission cannot be assigned to externally linked roles. However, it is possible to assign the Initiate permission.

Process form

A role with access to any process form will have permission to initiate or manage the process form.

Initiate

Manage

Initiate new items in the process form

Create and manage items in the process form

Work on all of the items they have created and assigned to them

Have default access to all reports as part of the process form

How permission inheritance works

When a process is shared with a role, its permissions are automatically inherited by the associated reports.

This means that when you assign a role to a process, all users in that role will automatically get access to the related reports without needing to share them separately.
You can still adjust or override the default permissions at any time.

The table below shows how permissions are inherited for processes:

Permission level

Access to process reports

Initiate

❌

Manage

✅

Dataform

A role with access to any dataform will have permission to initiate or manage the dataform.

Read-only

Edit

Manage

Allows users only to view the items.

Enables users to view, create, and update the items.

Enables users to view, create, update, and delete the items.

How permission inheritance works

When a dataform is shared with a role, its permissions are automatically inherited by the associated views and reports.

This means that when you assign a role to a dataform, all users in that role will automatically get access to the related views and reports without needing to share them separately.
You can still adjust or override the default permissions at any time.

The table below shows how permissions are inherited for dataforms:

Permission level

Access to dataform views

Access to dataform reports

Read-only

✅ (all except sheet view)

❌

Edit

✅

✅

Manage

✅

✅

For example, if a dataform is shared with Read-only permission, the users won’t have access to its reports by default.

If the dataform is shared with Edit or Manage permission, users will automatically gain access to views and reports.

Board form

A role with access to any Board form will have permission to initiate or manage the Board form.

Read-only

Initiate

Manage

Edit

Can view items in the system views, but cannot edit them.

Can initiate new items and work on all of the items created by them.

Can create new board items, manage items created by them and assigned to them. They will have access to all reports.

Can create, view, and update items.

How permission inheritance works

When a board is shared with a role, its permissions are automatically inherited by the associated views and reports.

This means that when you assign a role to a board, all users in that role will automatically get access to the related views and reports without needing to share them separately.
You can still adjust or override the default permissions at any time.

The table below shows how permissions are inherited for boards:

Permission level

Access to board views

Access to board reports

Read-only

✅

✅

Initiate

✅

✅

Edit

✅

✅

Manage

✅

✅

Board views and reports will be accessible at all permission levels.

> Note: To override the inherited permissions, go to the Roles page and adjust the permissions as per your requirement.

nodes.label: Sharing data across apps using component referencing
nodes.heading: Sharing data across apps using component referencing
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Component referencing across apps in Kissflow enables seamless and dynamic data sharing across applications within your account or external portal apps. This guide provides a comprehensive overview of utilizing component referencing to access views, reports, and workflow steps from different data sources like your application's processes, boards, and dataforms.

nodes.label: Sharing data across apps using component referencing
nodes.heading: Capabilities of component referencing across apps
nodes.snippet:
Component referencing enables several advanced interactions across applications:

- Accessing views and reports: This feature allows the integration and display of data layouts and reports from one application within another, ensuring consistent data utilization across your organization.

- Acting on workflow steps: Facilitates the execution of actions related to workflow steps from one application to another, streamlining processes that span multiple applications.

- Initiating workflow items: Enables users to start processes and initiate items in one application from the interface of another, enhancing workflow flexibility and responsiveness.

nodes.label: Sharing data across apps using component referencing
nodes.heading: Setting up component referencing across apps
nodes.snippet:
Here are the three prerequisites for referring flows of a source app from your current app:

1. Adding a role - Go to the source app from which you want to reference a component, and add the roles from your current app to the External apps section of the source app.

2. Permission assignment - In the source app, navigate to the view, report, or process you wish to access from your current app. Assign the necessary permissions to the app roles added in the previous step.

Note: For external app roles, only Initiate permissions can be granted at the flow level share,
which applies solely to processes and boards.

3. Component consumption -  After assigning roles and sharing components in the source app, you can access those components in the page builder of your current application. However, this is only possible if at least one role from your current app has been authorized to access the components in the source app.

> Note:
> Only individual components can be shared, not the entire flow.

Practical use case example

Consider a scenario where you manage the IT asset procurement through an internal procurement app and a vendor portal app. The internal procurement app, which is meant for the internal members of the organization, facilitates placing asset requests using a Purchase Order (PO) process form. The vendor portal app is dedicated to vendors to verify purchase orders sent to them and acknowledge the fulfillment.

After you raise a purchase order, it’s to be sent to the vendor for approval before processing the receipt.

Challenges in the current process

- Step assignment: Similar to the purchase order process, portal users trigger a status update process via integration. However, in-app members (source app) need help to track this process's status, making tracking and oversight challenging.

- View sharing: Direct view sharing between apps is not supported. The workaround involves using a custom component to fetch data via API, which adds complexity.

Solution using component referencing across apps

Component referencing across apps simplifies sharing data between the procurement (source) app and the vendor (portal) app, enabling efficient management of work items. Vendors can now actively participate in the procurement process and vice versa.

For instance, the vendor must review and accept each Purchase Order (PO) before issuing an invoice.

To facilitate this:

1. Link the vendor’s role to your app’s under External app roles.

2. Next, assign this role to the PO process. You can assign an initiate or manage permission to this role.

3. Assign this role with appropriate permissions to the PO process and add a workflow step, ensuring each order receives vendor approval before proceeding.

Similarly, vendors can create, submit, and fulfill invoices in the vendor portal app upon your approval.

nodes.label: Sharing data across apps using component referencing
nodes.heading: Advantages of component referencing across apps
nodes.snippet:
- Efficient collaboration: Component referencing across apps allows for seamless data exchange and workflow coordination, reducing the need for manual intervention and closing communication gaps.

- Enhanced visibility: Manage end-to-end data effectively while maintaining confidentiality where necessary.

nodes.label: Switching roles at runtime in Kissflow Apps
nodes.heading: Switching roles at runtime in Kissflow Apps
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Kissflow Apps offers the ability for developers to switch between different user roles during the runtime of an app, without interrupting the flow of development. This feature is designed to enhance the testing process by enabling developers to experience the app as different user personas without manually enrolling themselves in other roles.

nodes.label: Switching roles at runtime in Kissflow Apps
nodes.heading: Key benefits of role switching
nodes.snippet:
- As a developer, you can switch between app roles at runtime without needing to exit the app.

- When you switch to a role using the Viewing as dropdown, the system will automatically remove you from the current role and enroll you in the selected one. Due to this, the last role used by a developer is saved in user preferences and applied automatically the next time the developer runs the app.

nodes.label: Switching roles at runtime in Kissflow Apps
nodes.heading: How to switch roles at runtime
nodes.snippet:
Scenario:

You have set up custom navigation for the app with multiple menus that are intended for different user roles. Testing out how the navigation changes for each role by assigning users to roles manually can be a tedious process.

Follow the below steps to switch roles with a single click:

1. Click Run to open the runtime view with the navigation menu.

2. Click the Viewing as dropdown menu. It will list all the available roles in the app.

3. Click the role whose view you want to see.

You will be automatically enrolled in the selected role, overriding the previous role that you were assigned to.

> Note: The ability to switch roles is only available in the development environment. In staging and production, users will be assigned roles based on their initial setup, and role switching is not permitted.

nodes.label: Switching roles at runtime in Kissflow Apps
nodes.heading: How to assign multiple roles to a user
nodes.snippet:
1. While role switching restricts you to one role at a time, you can manually add yourself to multiple roles and view pages assigned to those roles at once. To do this, go to Manage > Roles.
2. Click Manage members against the role you want and add your user name.

3. Now, you will be enrolled into multiple roles, granting you access to all the pages for which these roles have permissions. Learn more about adding users to roles.

> Note: Manually assigned roles will be overridden whenever the Viewing as menu is used to switch to a different role.

nodes.label: Using variables in application development
nodes.heading: Using variables in application development
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

nodes.label: Using variables in application development
nodes.heading: Overview
nodes.snippet:
In application development, variables are used to store dynamic values that can be used by any user anywhere within the application or on a specific page. A variable lets you define constant key-value pairs. These can be used to:

- Call out values within page components of a page.
- Filter data inside your workflows like board or process.

There are two types of variables that you can use: Global variables and local variables.

Global variables: Global variables are declared as a common value that can be used throughout the application any number of times by any user. Once declared, a global variable stays in the app throughout the duration the app is running.

Local variables: Local variables are created within specific pages and can be used only within the page where they are created, limiting their scope. Other than the scope, a local variable can be used any number of times within the page where it is defined.

Global Variable

Local Variable

Global variables are declared as a common value in the app.

Local variables are declared within a specific page in the app.

The variable’s scope is global, i.e., all forms in the app can access it.

The local variable’s scope is limited to the page where it is created. No other page inside the app can use a local variable created within another page.

Any change in the global variable affects wherever it is used inside the app.

Any change in the local variable affects the components where it is used within the page it is defined.

A global variable is active in the application memory for the entire time the app is in use.

A local variable is active whenever the page where it is defined is running.

Advantages of using local variables

Local variables offer a limited scope compared to global variables. However, using local variables can help you manage memory usage, increase processing speed and improve the application's overall performance.

While it is not possible to use the same variable name for the same type of variable, you can create global and local variables by the same name to organize the app better and prevent any naming conflicts.

Local variables can ensure improved scalability by creating as many variables as needed to execute quickly and handle larger workloads.

Variable types

You can create a variable corresponding to one of the six variable types listed below. Each data type serves a specific purpose based on the kind of data the field needs to handle:

- Text – A sequence of characters or words, such as names or descriptions.

- Number – Any numerical value, including integers and decimals.

- Boolean – A true/false value, often used for toggles or checkboxes.

- Object – A structured collection of key-value pairs, useful for storing related data.

- Array of strings – A list of text values, ideal for multiple-choice inputs.

- Array of objects – A list of structured data items, commonly used for complex datasets.

Variable limits

The variable types Object, Array of strings, and Array of objects can be used to created JSON variable. These three variable types come with limits.

Objects

- Root object keys: 100 maximum (top level)

- Nesting depth: 2 levels

- Level 1 nested object keys: 20 maximum

- Level 2 nested object keys: 10 maximum

- Array items: 10 per array

- Array item object keys: 10 per array

JSON schema for objects

__CODE_BLOCK_0

Example JSON for objects

CODE_BLOCK_1

Array of strings

- Root array items: 30 maximum

JSON schema for array of strings

CODE_BLOCK_2

Array of objects

- Root array items: 100 maximum

- Nesting depth: 3 levels

- Level 1 nested object keys: 30 maximum

- Level 2 nested object keys: 20 maximum

- Level 3 nested object keys: 10 maximum

- Array items: 10 per array

- Array item object keys: 10 per array

JSON schema for array of objects

CODE_BLOCK_3

Example JSON for array of objects

CODE_BLOCK_4__

nodes.label: Using variables in application development
nodes.heading: Creating global variables

nodes.label: Using variables in application development
nodes.heading: Creating local variables
nodes.snippet:
1. Open an app in Kissflow.

2. Go to Application builder.
3. On the left navigation bar, click Pages and open the required page.
4. Under Page properties, click Local variable and enter the details of a variable.

- Variable name: The key name for your variable.
- Description: The description for your variable.
- Variable type: You can select the following data types: Number, Text, DateTime, and Boolean.
- Default value: Specify the default value for the key. The default value differs based on the data type.

Click Add to save the variable.

All the local variables created within the current page will be listed here. You can also update or delete a variable from this section.

Alternatively, you can also add new local variables from the JavaScript editor within a page.

Naming conventions for variable names

- Global and local variable names cannot be empty or start with numbers or any other special character, such as an underscore.

- A variable cannot have the same name as another variable of the same type.
- Reserved keywords cannot be used as variable names. Click here for a list of reserved keywords in Kissflow.

How to declare global and local variables in the javascript editor?

Use the following syntax to declare new global and local variables within the JavaScript editor in a form.

Global variable

__CODE_BLOCK_0

CODE_BLOCK_1

In which the syntax, kf.app.setVariable creates and defines a new global variable.

Local variable

CODE_BLOCK_2

CODE_BLOCK_3__

In which the syntax, kf.app.page.setVariable creates and defines a new local variable.

Click here to learn more about how to declare and use variables using javascript using the Kissflow SDK methods.

Note: When you delete a page from the app, all local variables that are defined within the page will also be deleted.

Where are variables used?

When you add a page component like a button or label, you can use the app variables inside the fields as dynamic values or use them inside JavaScript functions. Once the application is deployed, these global variables will render during an app session.

For example, an IT admin might want to keep track of the total number of IT assets available in an organization to manage existing assets effectively. To do this, they can store the count in a global variable called 'assets\_master\_count'. This variable can then be accessed and used anywhere within the application.

To retrieve the asset count for a particular department alone, the admin can create an individual page for the department and use local variables for this purpose.

FAQs

1. Can I create a global and local variable with the same name?
Yes, it is possible to use the same variable name for global and local variables. They operate independently as their scopes within the application differ.

Can a variable name be updated after creation?
No, it is not possible to update the variable name once created. However, you can edit the variable description, data type, and default value at any time.

nodes.label: Variables and parameters in app development
nodes.heading: Variables and parameters in app development
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Understanding variables in app development

Click here to learn about the types of variables available and how to use them in app development.

Input parameters

The input parameters are used to provide data to a field. They can be created within a page and passed from one page to another. Input parameters can be used within a page as well as passed to other pages. For example, you can pass an instance ID and activity instance ID to a process form from another process item using input parameters.

In the app builder > pages, the input parameters are found on the right side under the General properties tab .

To create an input parameter:

1. Click the Add button .
2. Enter the parameter name.
3. Add a description to the parameter if needed.
4. Click Add to add the new parameter. You can also edit or delete a parameter later.

Event parameters

These are parameters passed from an event within a component. An event parameter can be created by selecting a component, going to the Event properties tab, adding an event, and selecting the action and page for redirection.

For example, an instance ID and activity ID can be passed through an on Click event that redirects to another page.

Configuration properties

These are constants that have the same value throughout the app across different environments such as development and production. They can be set with a default value and can be changed during runtime. For example, an API key can be a config property.

Comparing variables and parameters

Type

Scope

Where can it be defined?

Usage

Example

Global variables

Across all environments of the app

Variables section in app builder, inside a page

JavaScript code

User email

Local variables

Page

Inside a page

JavaScript code

Page ID

Input parameters

Page

General properties of the page body

Used across components, JavaScript code

Application ID

Event parameters

Page/app depending upon whether it is passed on

Event properties of a component

JavaScript code, passing values to input parameters of another page

Instance ID

Config property

Across all environments of the app

Inside an app, as a constant

JavaScript code

API key

Usage in JavaScript editor

JavaScript code can be added to an event within a component by opening the code editor under the Event properties of the component and selecting the JavaScript action. Once the code editor is open, you can use global and local variables, popups, event parameters, and input parameters by double-clicking on their names to define them within the code.

In summary, variables, input parameters, event parameters, and configuration properties are all used in app development to store and pass data within the app. Each has its own scope, usage, and examples of when they might be used in a real-world app.

Adding events to dataforms

You can create events for individual dataform component in your app.

1. Select the particular dataform in your apps page

2. Go to the Events tab

3. Choose an event from the drop-down menu

- On submit

- On save

- On discard

- On delete

4. Select the action you want the event to trigger.

- Redirection: You can guide users to another page.

- Pop up: Display a pop-up when an event occurs.

- Custom javascript actions: You can configure custom actions beyond redirection and pop-ups.

nodes.label: Developer Guide
nodes.heading: Developer Guide
nodes.snippet:
Developer Guide

Extend Kissflow with APIs, SDK, and custom code.

nodes.label: API and SDK documentation
nodes.heading: API and SDK documentation
nodes.snippet:
API and SDK documentation

Kissflow's API reference and SDK documentation are maintained on the Kissflow developer portal, separate from this site. Use them to build integrations, automate actions, and extend Kissflow with custom code.

nodes.label: API and SDK documentation
nodes.heading: API reference
nodes.snippet: The API documentation covers the REST APIs available for Kissflow apps, boards, processes, and other entities — including authentication, request and response formats, and available endpoints.

nodes.label: API and SDK documentation
nodes.heading: SDK documentation
nodes.snippet:
The SDK documentation covers the Kissflow SDK, used to build custom components and extend the product's UI and behavior in JavaScript.

The Kissflow JavaScript SDK repository contains the package source and related development resources.

nodes.label: Contextual global search
nodes.heading: Contextual global search
nodes.snippet:
Contextual global search

Global search puts every workflow, item, and person in your account within reach from one place. Type a keyword, or describe what you're looking for in your own words, and Kissflow's AI surfaces the workflows that match — even if you don't remember its exact name. You can also use global search to find people in your account.

media.alt: Global search bar open with search results listed below it

nodes.label: Contextual global search
nodes.heading: Search with AI
nodes.snippet:
Contextual search uses AI to understand what you describe and match it to the right process, board, or dataset — no exact workflow name needed. Type a few words about what you're trying to do, and the most relevant workflows rise to the top.

AI matching only works for processes, boards, and datasets — it's what surfaces a workflow when you describe it in your own words instead of typing its exact name. It doesn't cover apps, portals, or individual items. You can still find those with a plain keyword search and the filters below; they just won't come up from a natural-language description.

media.alt: Typing a natural-language description into search and seeing matching workflows appear

nodes.label: Contextual global search
nodes.heading: Filter your results
nodes.snippet:
The global search bar also supports plain keyword search across everything in your account, including apps and individual items — not just the AI-matched workflows above. When a search returns results across many workflows, narrow them down by type:

1. Run a search from the global search bar.
2. Select a filter above the results — Items, Flows, Apps, or Users.

This is especially useful in large or complex accounts, where a single keyword can otherwise return results spread across dozens of workflows.

nodes.label: Contextual global search
nodes.heading: Pick up where you left off
nodes.snippet: Workflows you've recently opened from search appear by default the next time you open the search bar, before you type anything. This saves you from retyping a query just to get back to something you were working on.

media.alt: Search bar showing a list of recently accessed workflows before any search term is entered

nodes.label: Contextual global search
nodes.heading: Go deeper with item-level search
nodes.snippet:
Select More results in the search panel to open the item-level search window. Every matching item is listed with its key properties, so you can review it without opening the workflow first.

From here, narrow the list further:

- Workflow — restrict results to a specific process, board, or dataset.
- Date range — item-level search shows items from the last 90 days by default. Change it to All, Last 30 days, Last 6 months, or Last year to widen or narrow the window.

media.alt: Item-level search window listing items with filters for workflow and date range

nodes.label: Contextual global search
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Homepage overview
- My items

nodes.label: Homepage overview
nodes.heading: Homepage overview

media.alt: Kissflow homepage with the top navigation bar and default widget dashboard

nodes.label: Homepage overview
nodes.heading: The top bar
nodes.snippet:
Across the top of every page, you have quick access to:

- Global search — search across items, workflows, and apps by name or keyword.
- Help — click the help icon to open the help documentation or reach the Kissflow community.
- Notifications — the bell icon shows updates on items assigned to you and other account activity.
- Your profile — click your profile picture to get to My settings, Themes, or to sign out.

nodes.label: Homepage overview
nodes.heading: Ask: Kissflow's AI agent
nodes.snippet:
Ask lets you query your workflow data using natural language and get reports and insights back instantly, without building a report by hand. Click Ask on your homepage to start a conversation.

Before you can ask a question, add context: click + Add context and select the processes, boards, apps, or datasets you want the AI to draw on. You can select up to 3 workflows at a time.

Only workflows you have sufficient access to are available to add as context. If a workflow you expect to see is missing, check your permissions on it.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Once you've added context, type your question in plain language. Ask interprets your intent, identifies the relevant fields in the selected workflows, queries the data, and returns a response.

The conversation doesn't reset after each question — Ask keeps up to 40 previous messages as context, so you can ask follow-up questions without repeating yourself.

Generating a report

You can also use Ask to create reports with a simple prompt. When Ask generates a report, click Edit and save to send it to the Analytics report builder, where you can refine it further. Saved reports show up under Analytics > Reports.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Checking a response

Every response comes with a plain-language explanation of the result. Click Show details to see exactly how Ask got there:

- Sources — the workflows used and the fields referenced.
- Query — the exact query run against the data.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

You can rate any response with a thumbs up or thumbs down, and optionally add more detail — whether the response was accurate, how fast it was, and any other comments. This feedback helps improve Ask over time.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Use More options to open the help documentation or clear the current conversation. You can also expand the Ask interface for a more focused, full-screen view.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Adding context and asking a natural-language question in the Ask panel
Editing and saving an Ask-generated report into the Analytics report builder
Show details panel with Sources and Query tabs for an Ask response
Feedback options on an Ask response, including thumbs up, thumbs down, and additional comments
More options menu in Ask with links to help documentation and clear conversation

nodes.label: Homepage overview
nodes.heading: Default widgets
nodes.snippet:
By default, your homepage shows five widgets, each using fixed system fields as columns:

- Items in your queue — items assigned to you.
- Items created by you — items you've initiated.
- Recently accessed — items you opened most recently.
- Tagged comments — comments where you've been mentioned.
- My to-do — items you've marked as to-do.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Marking items as to-do

Open any process or board item assigned to you and use Mark as to-do (or Remove from to-do) at the top of the item. You can do this directly from the Items in your queue widget. Items you've marked appear in the My to-do widget for easier tracking.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Default homepage dashboard with the five standard widgets
Marking an item as to-do from its header, and the item appearing under My to-do

nodes.label: Homepage overview
nodes.heading: Customizing your homepage with widgets
nodes.snippet:
Widget customization is an Enterprise feature. On the Basic plan, your homepage always shows the five default widgets.

If your account has customization enabled, you can add, remove, and rearrange widgets to build a dashboard that matches how you work. Click Customize at the top right of the homepage to open the widget picker, drag the widgets you want into your dashboard, arrange them, and click Save.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

There are 13 widgets to choose from — most are standard and work out of the box, while a few are flow-specific and need to be configured before they'll show data.

Standard widgets

| Widget | Description |
|---|---|
| Items in your queue | Shows all items currently assigned to you. |
| Items in your queue – overdue | Shows items in your queue that have passed their due date. |
| Items in your queue – due today | Shows items in your queue that are due today. |
| Tagged comments | Displays comments where you've been tagged. |
| My to-do | Shows items you've marked as to-do from processes or boards assigned to you. |
| Recently accessed | Quick access to the items you opened most recently. |
| Access requests | Lists access requests awaiting your action. |
| Flow creation requests | Displays requests for creating new flows. |

Items created by you is also on the widget picker, but unlike the widgets above it needs a quick one-time configuration (setting a status filter) before it can be saved to your dashboard.

Flow-specific widgets

These widgets need to be configured with a specific flow (and, for reports, a specific report) before they show data:

| Widget | Description |
|---|---|
| Items in your queue – specific flow | Shows items assigned to you from a chosen flow. |
| Items created by me – specific flow | Lists items you created in a specific flow. |
| Chart reports | Visual representation of a report from one of your flows. |
| Tabular reports | Tabular view of a report from one of your flows. |

You can add up to 3 report widgets (chart or tabular), and up to 15 widgets in total, on your homepage.

Use Reset to discard unsaved changes and go back to your last saved dashboard. If you remove every widget and save, your dashboard reverts to the five default widgets rather than staying empty.

media.alt: Customizing the homepage dashboard by adding and arranging widgets

nodes.label: Homepage overview
nodes.heading: Using AI auto suggest
nodes.snippet:
Instead of building your dashboard widget by widget, use AI auto suggest from the widget picker:

- Get suggestions — AI surfaces widgets likely to be useful based on your activity and usage patterns. You just review the suggestions and place the ones you want.
- Ask for widgets — type what you're looking for, for example _items created by me in employee confirmation_, and AI configures the matching flow-specific widget for you to drag into place.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

AI auto suggest only recommends widgets from the standard 13-widget catalog — it doesn't create new widget types. For flow-specific widgets, it can go one step further and hand you a fully configured widget based on your request.

media.alt: Using AI auto suggest to get and configure widget recommendations

nodes.label: Kissflow extension for Gmail
nodes.heading: Kissflow extension for Gmail
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow extension for Gmail

The Kissflow for Gmail extension lets you view and approve items assigned to you in Kissflow directly from your Gmail inbox, without switching tabs.

nodes.label: Kissflow extension for Gmail
nodes.heading: Install the extension
nodes.snippet:
1. Go to the Kissflow for Gmail listing on the Chrome Web Store.
2. Click Add to Chrome.
3. Click Add extension to confirm.

nodes.label: Kissflow extension for Gmail
nodes.heading: Sign in to Kissflow from Gmail
nodes.snippet:
1. Open Gmail. A Kissflow icon appears on the right navigation panel.
2. Click the Kissflow icon.
3. Enter the domain name of your Kissflow account and click Submit.

Already have another Kissflow account signed in? See switch between accounts below.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

nodes.label: Kissflow extension for Gmail
nodes.heading: Approve or act on items
nodes.snippet:
Once signed in, the extension shows two sections:

- My tasks — items you can approve instantly.
- Tasks awaiting your action in Kissflow — items that need your input before they can be approved.

To approve an item instantly:

1. Under My tasks, find the item.
2. Click Approve.

If you want to review the item before approving it, click View task to open it in your Kissflow account.

To act on an item that needs your input:

1. Under Tasks awaiting your action in Kissflow, find the item.
2. Click Submit to open it in your Kissflow account, where you can provide the required input.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

nodes.label: Kissflow extension for Gmail
nodes.heading: Manage connected accounts
nodes.snippet:
You can connect and switch between multiple Kissflow accounts from the extension.

1. Click your account name under My tasks to return to the extension's login page.
2. Enter the domain name of another Kissflow account and click Submit.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Click the Refresh icon to reload tasks with the latest data from your Kissflow account, the Close icon to exit the widget, or the Trash icon to remove an account from the extension.

nodes.label: Managing boards in the PWA
nodes.heading: Managing boards in the PWA
nodes.snippet:
Managing boards in the PWA

Manage your boards from anywhere using the Kissflow Progressive Web App (PWA). The Home dashboard has two widgets for working with board items:

- My tasks — lists items assigned to you.
- My items — lists items you created.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Home dashboard with the My tasks and My items widgets

nodes.label: Managing boards in the PWA
nodes.heading: Create an item
nodes.snippet:
1. On the dashboard, tap the New item button next to a board.
2. In the form that opens, fill in the details — assignee, due date, status, priority, and any other fields your board captures.
3. Tap Submit.

You can also get to a board's new-item form by tapping Explore, opening the board, and tapping the New item button there.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: New item icon
Creating a new item from the dashboard

nodes.label: Managing boards in the PWA
nodes.heading: Browse items in a board
nodes.snippet:
1. Tap Explore.
2. Tap a board to see its items.
3. Switch between tabs to filter the view:
- All — every item in the board. This is the default tab.
- Assigned to me — items assigned to you for action.

If you're an initiator in the board, you also get an Initiated by me tab alongside All, pinned by default.

4. Tap the Sort button to sort the items shown in a tab.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Sort icon
Sorting items in a board tab

nodes.label: Managing boards in the PWA
nodes.heading: Manage an item
nodes.snippet:
1. Open an item from the board.
2. Tap the More options button .
3. Choose an action:
- Copy link — copies a direct URL for the item that you can share with others.
- Watch — watch an item that isn't assigned to you to keep track of its progress.
- Clone — creates a duplicate of the item.
- Delete — removes the item.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: More options icon
More options menu on an item showing Copy link, Watch, Clone, and Delete

nodes.label: Managing boards in the PWA
nodes.heading: Track items from My tasks and My items
nodes.snippet:
1. On the dashboard, tap the My tasks widget to see items assigned to you, or the My items widget to review the progress of items you created.
2. Tap an item to open it. Any changes you make to its fields are auto-saved.

You can also add notes to collaborate with others on the item. Notes support @mentions, emojis, attachments, and other formatting options.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Opening an item from My tasks and adding a note

nodes.label: My items
nodes.heading: My items
nodes.snippet:
My items

My items brings together every item you've created or that's waiting on you for approval, across all your processes, boards, and apps — so you don't have to open each flow individually to check on your work.

Items are grouped under two tabs:

- Assigned to me — items from any process, board, or app that need your attention.
- Created by me — items you've submitted or created, wherever they live.

If you're an Account Admin or a flow approver, you'll also see an Admin actions tab with pending Item reassignment, Flow approvals, Admin assignment, and Join request actions that need your attention.

nodes.label: My items
nodes.heading: Finding items assigned to me
nodes.snippet:
The Assigned to me tab lists items from every process, board, and app you have pending work on. Use the navigation panel to move between flows, or apply the Due by filter to narrow the list.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Click any item to open its process or board form. Closing the item returns you to the Assigned to me list.

Filter the list using the Due by field:

- All — every item assigned to you, regardless of due date.
- Today — items due today.
- Tomorrow — items due the following day.
- This week — items due sometime this week.
- Overdue — items whose due date has passed.

media.alt: My items page showing items assigned to the user

nodes.label: My items
nodes.heading: Finding items created by me
nodes.snippet:
The Created by me tab lists every item you've created, across processes, boards, and apps, in the left navigation panel. Click an item to open its process or board form.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Filter created items by status:

- In progress — items in the In progress status, for both processes and boards.
- Not started — items in the Draft status on processes, and items in the Not started or Reopened status on boards.
- Completed — items in the Completed, Withdrawn, or Rejected status on processes, and items in the Done or Closed status on boards, from the last 90 days.

Only items completed in the last 90 days appear when you filter by Completed.

Depending on the status filter you choose, you can further narrow the list using the Created or Completed date field:

- All — items created or completed at any time.
- Today — items created or completed today.
- Yesterday — items created or completed the previous day.
- Past week — items created or completed in the last 7 days.
- Past 14 days — items created or completed in the last 14 days.
- This month — items created or completed during the current calendar month.
- Past 30 days — items created or completed in the last 30 days.
- Custom date range — items created or completed within a date range you specify.

media.alt: My items page showing items created by the user

nodes.label: My items
nodes.heading: Admin actions
nodes.snippet:
The Admin actions tab appears only when there's something pending that needs an Account Admin's or flow approver's attention. If your account has several Account Admins or flow approvers, they all see the same pending actions — once one admin completes an action, it disappears from everyone else's list.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Under Admin actions, you'll find four sections: Item reassignment, Flow approvals, Admin assignment, and Join requests.

Item reassignment

Items assigned to a user who's since been deactivated or deleted are left with no one to act on them. These items show up under Item reassignment, where an Account Admin or Flow Admin can hand them off to an active user.

To reassign an item:

1. Click My items > Admin actions > Item reassignment.
2. Select an item from the list and click Reassign.
3. In the dialog that opens, review the item's process and current step, select an active user to reassign it to, add a note if needed, and click Reassign.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

The item moves to the new user and drops off the Item reassignment list. Reassignment happens one item at a time.

Flow approvals

By default, anyone in your account can create processes, boards, and datasets. If your account's flow settings require approval before a new flow can be created, requests from users land under Flow approvals for the specified approvers.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Approvers can Approve or Deny each request from here.

Admin assignment

Every process and board needs at least one admin. If a flow's only admin is deactivated or deleted, the flow is left without one — Admin assignment lists these flows so you can assign a replacement.

To assign an admin:

1. Click My items > Admin actions > Admin assignment.
2. Select the process or board that needs an admin and click Assign admin.
3. Select a user and click Assign to make them the admin.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

If a flow needs more than one admin, repeat this action for each additional admin — the dialog assigns one user at a time.

Join requests

If your account allows new signups, people outside your account can request to join using your account's unique join URL. These requests show up under Join requests for Account Admins to Approve or Deny.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Approving a request activates the user in your account.

If your account has no user licenses available, activating the user fails and they stay in a requested state until a license frees up.

media.alt: My items page showing the Admin actions tab
Reassigning an item from the Item reassignment tab
Flow approvals tab showing a pending flow creation request
Assigning an admin to a process from the Admin assignment tab
Join requests tab showing a pending signup request

nodes.snippet:
1. Download the Kissflow app from the App Store or Google Play
2. Enter your account URL or scan the QR code from your desktop
3. Sign in with your credentials

nodes.label: Signing in to Kissflow
nodes.heading: Troubleshooting
nodes.snippet:
| Problem | Fix |
|---|---|
| "Account not found" | Check with your admin that your email was added to the account |
| Password reset | Click Forgot password on the sign-in page |
| SSO redirect loop | Clear browser cookies, try incognito mode, contact admin |
