nodes.label: What is Kissflow
nodes.heading: What is Kissflow
nodes.snippet: Kissflow is a work platform that lets you build apps, automate workflows, and manage work — without writing code.

nodes.label: What is Kissflow
nodes.heading: Key capabilities
nodes.snippet:
- Apps — Build custom business applications with drag-and-drop pages, forms, and data models
- Processes — Design multi-step approval workflows with conditional routing and SLA tracking
- Boards — Track work items across statuses with Kanban, list, timeline, and gallery views
- Integrations — Connect to Google Workspace, Salesforce, Slack, SAP, and 40+ services
- AI Builder — Generate workflows, form fields, formulas, and custom components with AI
- Analytics — Create reports, dashboards, and data views across all your apps

nodes.label: Apps FAQs
nodes.heading: Apps FAQs
nodes.snippet:
What programming languages can I use to create Kissflow Apps?

Kissflow Apps isn't tied to a single programming language. Our Software Development Kit (SDK) is designed for client-side apps and is written in JavaScript. To interact with server-side apps, use the Run script connector in Integration.

Can I get a Kissflow Apps account for testing purposes?

Yes. If you don't already have an account, sign up for a free trial with your work email address at Kissflow. Trial availability and duration depend on your plan, so check the current pricing page for details before you sign up.

Is there a central platform to access different low-code apps?

Yes — the Kissflow App Store. It hosts ready-made apps you can install with little to no coding, and you can also build and share your own apps with other Kissflow users. See Introduction to Kissflow App Store to get started.

How do I provide public access to an app?

Kissflow Apps themselves don't support public links, so there's no direct way to make an entire app publicly accessible. Specific components within an app, such as processes, offer limited public access through public forms. These let external users interact with a process form without accessing the app itself.

How do I share private access to an app?

Since apps can't have public links, share access intentionally instead: invite the users you want to have access and assign them appropriate roles. They need to be licensed users to open the app.

If you need to collect data from external users, use a public link on the specific process that needs it rather than trying to share the whole app. Public process links are built for external interactions like form submissions, while the app itself stays private.

What if I need an entire app to be public?

Use a Kissflow Portal instead. Portals are built for sharing flows and apps with external users who don't have a Kissflow license.

nodes.label: Boards FAQs
nodes.heading: Boards FAQs
nodes.snippet:
Can I handle multiple use cases, such as issue tracking, help desk, and service requests, using Kissflow Board?

Yes. Kissflow Board is built to support a wide range of workflows on a single platform — project management, issue tracking, bug tracking, help desks, service requests, ticketing systems, and more.

There are a few boards I use frequently. How can I access them quickly without going to the Explore page?

Pin them to the left navigation. On the board, click the icon next to the board's name to pin it. Pinned boards then appear in the left navigation for quick access.

I'd like to add custom statuses to my board. Is that possible?

Yes. You can add any number of custom statuses relevant to your workflow from Settings.

What happens when I rename a custom status?

The new name is applied across the board immediately. Items currently assigned to that status show the new name — there's nothing to update on the items themselves.

I no longer need one of the statuses in my workflow. Can I delete it?

Yes. Delete custom statuses you created from inside the Edit workflow menu. If items are still assigned to a status you delete, you're prompted to confirm deleting those items along with the status — there's no option to move them to another status first, so reassign any items you want to keep before deleting the status.

Can I customize the board form? If so, how?

Yes. Customize the form to capture information relevant to your workflow. Drag and drop fields, or add new sections and tables, using the form editor.

I no longer want priority for the items in my board, since this field doesn't apply to my use case. What do I do?

Turn off priority in settings. You can do this even if priority was already used to create items or set up other features such as filters and views.

Can I create custom filters to track certain items frequently?

Yes — use Views inside the board to create customized views of your data. Saved views appear in the Views dropdown, and you can also pin them as tabs for quick access.

I see items can be retrieved using different options such as "Created by me" and "Requested by me." Aren't these the same?

No, they show different data. Created by me lists items you created in the board. Requested by me lists items where you're recorded as the requester, even if someone else created the item on your behalf — for example, if you use a board as a help desk, a support agent might "create" an item that you, the customer, "requested."

I want to find out the time spent on each status for an item. How can I do that?

Open the item, click the Item log tab in the right navigation strip, and select the Status log subtab. It captures every status change for the item along with the time spent in each status. See Collaborating on items for more.

Where can I find a history of actions performed on an item?

Open the item, click the Item log tab in the right navigation strip, and select the Activity log subtab. It lists key actions performed on the item — such as changes to system fields, with the value before and after each change — plus when the item was created, closed, or cloned. See Collaborating on items for the full list of what's tracked.

How do I determine the resolution time of an item?

Resolution time is tracked as a system field for items in the Resolved or Closed status, and is available as a column in views and reports on the board.

nodes.label: Analytics FAQs
nodes.heading: General
nodes.snippet:
Can I bring in data from external sources into Kissflow Analytics?

No. Kissflow Analytics is built to report on data that already exists in your Kissflow account — processes, boards, datasets, and dataforms. It isn't a general-purpose BI tool for importing outside data.

Can I connect BI tools like Power BI or Tableau to Kissflow Analytics?

Not directly. Use the Kissflow connector for BI to connect Kissflow data to external BI tools. See Enabling Kissflow connector for BI to set it up, or contact Kissflow support for help.

How often does my data sync into Analytics?

Kissflow syncs your processes, boards, datasets, and dataforms into Analytics once every hour. If a report looks out of date, open the data source in Data explorer and check its Last synced time.

How does data sync work with third-party middleware like Snowflake?

There's no direct database access to your Kissflow data. Instead, integrations like the Kissflow connector for BI go through middleware such as Snowflake, which reads from the same hourly-synced Analytics data. See Configuring Snowflake for Kissflow connector for BI for setup steps.

Do I need a specific Kissflow plan to access Analytics?

It depends on the capability. Some Analytics features — like creating dataviews with SQL — are only available on higher-tier plans. Check your account's plan details, or contact your Kissflow admin or our support team to confirm what's included for your account.

What are some common use cases for Kissflow Analytics?

See our Analytics documentation for common report examples, including a process item completion time report, a process step performance report, and a user workload report.

What are the different roles for data sources and reports in Analytics?

Data sources have two roles: Report admin, who gets full control of the data source, and Report creator, who gets view access to the data source so they can build dataviews and reports from it. Neither role can share a data source directly, or create, edit, or delete data sources — process tables, board tables, system tables, and so on are generated automatically.

Report roles work differently: Admins have full control over a report, while Members can view and share it by default, and can be granted edit access.

Why can't other users edit the reports I've shared with them?

Report members can only view reports by default. To let them edit, grant them edit access on the report.

How will child tables in a process or board appear in Analytics?

Each child table is created as its own table in Analytics, named after the parent process or board followed by the child table's name. For example, a Purchase Order process with a child table called Item Details appears in Analytics as Purchase Order Item Details.

Can I edit or modify a query once it's published?

Yes. The dataview's owner can edit a published dataview from Data explorer > My dataviews > More options > Edit.

If I add or remove fields in a dataview, will the changes show up in existing reports?

No. Reports created from a dataview don't pick up field changes automatically. If you add or remove fields in the dataview, create a new report to see the updated data.

What are system tables in Kissflow Analytics?

System tables are system-generated tables that hold activity and history data for items in your processes and boards, and you can build any report type on top of them. There are three kinds: Process Instance and Activity Instance for processes, Board Workflow Instance for boards, and User and Group tables for account-wide user and group data.

How can I open a Kissflow process from a Power BI report or dashboard?

Add a dynamic URL to your Power BI report that links to the specific Kissflow web page. See Microsoft's guides on adding a dynamic URL and Power BI hyperlinks in tables for the steps.

What's the table naming convention in Analytics, and how do I relate it to the flow name?

Analytics displays table names with underscores in place of spaces. For example, a process named Purchase Request appears as the Purchase_Request table. You can also match a process to its table using the process name in the process's URL. See the entity relationship diagram for how tables relate to each other.

nodes.label: Analytics FAQs
nodes.heading: Reporting features
nodes.snippet:
Can I create a report based on a child table within a process?

Yes. Find child tables under Data explorer > My tables, then create a report from them the same way you would from any other table.

Can I create a report based on a dataset?

Yes. Datasets appear under Data explorer > My tables.

Can I create a report based on a board?

Yes — from a single board or by combining data across several boards. Board tables are listed under Data explorer for every board in your account.

How can I create reports based on subitems in a board?

Filter the board table by the Parent ID column to isolate subitems, then click Create report. See Board table in Analytics for the filter steps.

Can I join multiple tables to create reports in Analytics?

Yes. Use SQL JOIN statements in a dataview to combine rows from two or more tables on a common field. For example, a procurement spend analysis report joins a Purchase Order table with a Vendors table to show which vendors received the most spend.

How do I create metrics reports in Kissflow Analytics?

Join the process table with the activity instance table, matching _instance_id on the activity instance side with _id on the process side. See Process table in Analytics and System tables in Analytics for the columns and schema involved, and Creating process item completion time report with Analytics for a worked example.

How can I create a summary report of my board data in Analytics?

Build a customizable status summary report using dataviews to pull insights from one or more boards. This gives you more flexibility than a board's built-in Summary report, which only covers that one board.

How can I use Analytics to track my team's performance?

Try a step performance report to see how long items spend in each workflow step, or an item completion time report to track time from creation to completion. Both can be customized — see our Analytics documentation for more report ideas.

What report types does Kissflow Analytics support?

Three: tabular, chart, and pivot. Chart reports offer ten chart types, including area, bar, doughnut, line, pie, and scatter — see Chart reports for the full list.

Can I export a report in Analytics?

Only tabular reports support data export. Click More options on the reports page, select Export, then choose CSV or JSON. You'll get an email with a link to download the file.

Can colleagues in the same department view reports together?

Yes — create a report (in Process or Analytics) and share it with the team so everyone who needs it has access.

How do I filter Step Performance and User Performance reports by division or department?

Create a dataview that joins the ActivityInstance table with the relevant process table, then build a Step Performance or User Performance report from it. See Creating process step performance report with Analytics for the walkthrough. You can also do this in Power BI using the same tables through your BI connector.

nodes.label: Analytics FAQs
nodes.heading: Solutions
nodes.snippet:
How can I join two processes?

Join them on a common field. For example, if a Purchase Request process and a Purchase Order process both have a PR ID field, use that field to join the two in a dataview.

I'm getting "You don't have permission to access this resource" in a SQL query, even as a Super Admin. How do I fix it?

This happens when the querying user doesn't have access to the table or system table the dataview references. Super Admins don't get access to every table by default — add yourself to the relevant table or system table via Share first, then re-run the query. See Process table in Analytics, Board table in Analytics, Dataset table in Analytics, and System tables in Analytics for how sharing works on each.

How can I join a process table and a child table?

Match _id in the process table with _instance_id in the child table. See Process table in Analytics for the columns and schema involved.

I'm getting "purchase_Request does not exist" in my SQL query. How do I fix it?

This usually means the datasource name in your query doesn't match exactly — dataview queries are case-sensitive, so double-check the datasource name's capitalization.

What do I do if I lose my BI connector credentials?

You receive the credentials by email when you provision the Kissflow connector for BI for your account. If you lose access to that email, ask your current Account Owner to transfer ownership to you, or contact Kissflow support to have the credentials resent.

nodes.label: Product FAQs
nodes.heading: General
nodes.snippet:
What is Kissflow Work Platform?

Kissflow is a single platform for managing the full spectrum of work across your organization — apps, processes, and boards — without requiring code. Alongside these building blocks, it includes collaboration, integration, and analytics tools for day-to-day operations.

Who is Kissflow for?

Kissflow is built for the entire organization, not just one department or role. Individual users see exactly what's assigned to them and track it as it moves through a flow. Team leaders create and control codeless workflows and forms. Across the organization, Kissflow acts as an operational system of record for what work is in progress and what's been completed.

How is Kissflow different from other products?

Most tools focus on one slice of work — automated processes, collaboration, or project and case management. Kissflow brings all of these together on one platform, so it can be used across departments rather than for a single use case. It's designed for the common business user and builder, with a layout that doesn't require specialized training in process, project, or case management concepts.

Do you have templates to help me get started?

Yes. Kissflow ships with a library of pre-built templates for processes, projects, cases, and datasets, installable from the Kissflow marketplace. Once installed, you have full control to configure a template to fit your needs.

Example templates include:

- Sales Enquiry
- Purchase Request
- Employee Transfer Request
- Purchase Catalogue
- Software Directory
- Lead Qualification
- Sales Pipeline
- Customer Onboarding
- IT Help Desk
- Bug Tracking
- Incident Management

You can also build your own flows from scratch for work unique to your organization.

nodes.label: Product FAQs
nodes.heading: Pricing
nodes.snippet:
How does pricing work?

See the pricing page for current plan details, or contact support through the in-product chat for specifics.

How do I cancel my account?

Contact support through the in-product chat, or email support@kissflow.com.

Can I extend my trial?

If your trial period isn't enough time to evaluate Kissflow, open the in-product chat and ask support about extending it.

Do you have a partner reseller program?

Yes — see kissflow.com/partners for details.

Do you provide personalized training?

Yes, through Kissflow Academy, our training portal. It offers professional courses built by Kissflow experts. For more information, contact training@kissflow.net.

Do you offer consulting services?

Yes. Kissflow's consulting team can help you build out an entire network of interconnected processes, including custom integrations with third-party apps. Contact consulting@kissflow.net to discuss your requirements.

nodes.label: Product FAQs
nodes.heading: Technical
nodes.snippet:
What web browsers do you support?

Kissflow's web app is best viewed on:

- Google Chrome 120 and above
- Microsoft Edge 120 and above
- Safari 17 and above
- Mozilla Firefox 120 and above

Other browsers may work but aren't guaranteed to give you the best experience — keep your browser updated to the latest version.

Where is my data stored?

Your data is stored on servers located in the USA and the European Union.

What languages does Kissflow support?

Kissflow's interface is available in 20 languages, including English, Spanish, French, German, Japanese, and Simplified Chinese. See Multi-language support in Kissflow for the full list and how to change your interface language.

nodes.label: Product FAQs
nodes.heading: Support
nodes.snippet:
What's the best way to get support?

Kissflow offers several ways to get help: Kissflow Community for posting questions and reporting issues, in-product chat, and email at support@kissflow.com for when you can't log in. Email responses are typically slower than chat or Community.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI and icon location */}

See SLAs and support models for exactly how to reach each channel and the response-time targets that apply.

media.alt: Chat icon used to start a conversation with support

nodes.label: Account governance
nodes.heading: Account governance
nodes.snippet: Account governance under account administration is a centralized hub, allowing super admins and IAM admins to audit and manage their Kissflow accounts. This feature is pivotal for maintaining data security and protecting sensitive account information.

nodes.label: Action
nodes.heading: Action
nodes.snippet: An event a connector performs after it is triggered. Multiple actions can be configured for a single integration flow.

nodes.label: Activity instance table
nodes.heading: Activity instance table
nodes.snippet: In Kissflow Analytics, the activity instance table is a system-generated table that displays the complete step history of each item across all processes in the Kissflow account.

nodes.label: Administration
nodes.heading: Administration
nodes.snippet: The Administration section of a process lets Process admins and Data admins access and modify all items within the process.

nodes.label: Aggregation
nodes.heading: Aggregation
nodes.snippet: Aggregation field pulls together data from various fields to show relationships and perform calculations. You can use it to calculate sums, averages, or other aggregations of data within a single form or across different processes. You can apply filters to restrict the values used in the aggregation, giving you more control over the results.

nodes.label: AI-suggested fields
nodes.heading: AI-suggested fields
nodes.snippet: AI-suggested fields are a list of form fields automatically generated by AI. Unlike standard fields that require manual selection and configuration, these fields are suggested based on the provided form name and come pre-populated with a relevant Field name and Field type.

nodes.label: AI-suggested steps
nodes.heading: AI-suggested steps
nodes.snippet: AI-suggested steps in process use artificial intelligence to automatically generate workflow steps tailored to your process. For individual steps, clicking AI-suggested steps provides intelligent suggestions, which can be refreshed for more options.

nodes.label: AI-suggested workflow
nodes.heading: AI-suggested workflow
nodes.snippet: AI-suggested workflow is a feature that automatically generates an entire process workflow based on the process title or a keyword you provide. When you click Suggest, our AI analyzes your input and creates a complete set of workflow steps, saving you time and effort in process design. This builds upon our AI-suggested fields feature, offering a more comprehensive solution for workflow creation.

nodes.label: Analytics report
nodes.heading: Analytics report
nodes.snippet: Analytics reports allow you to create cross-form reports from various data sources, including process tables, child tables, system tables, datasets, and data views. These reports can be visualized in tabular or graphical formats. There are four types: Tabular reports, charts, card, and pivot reports.

nodes.label: App builder
nodes.heading: App builder
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow App Builder is a workspace within the development environment where you can assemble the pages and components of your app to create a complete, functional application. It is accessible only from the development environment. That is, when you click Edit app on a published or unpublished app, the app builder automatically opens.

The app builder makes it easy to develop multiple features simultaneously, allowing you to work on a page while also modifying a process in a separate tab, accessible from the left panel. You can switch between tabs without losing any progress. It also provides several default components that you can simply drag and drop onto your canvas, along with a variety of layout and styling options to customize the look and feel of your app.

nodes.label: Application
nodes.heading: Application
nodes.snippet: An application is a software solution that combines various elements such as forms, workflows, and integrations to carry out one or more tasks of a complex use case. Users can create enterprise applications by assembling these components visually, often with minimal or no coding. You can build fully functional and complex applications using Kissflow Apps.

nodes.label: Assignee
nodes.heading: Assignee
nodes.snippet: An assignee is the account user to whom a process or board item is assigned for their action.

nodes.label: Audit log
nodes.heading: Audit log
nodes.snippet: Audit logs provide valuable insights, enhance security, and help track potential exposure of sensitive data, allowing for better governance and management of your Kissflow account. At the account level, super admins, IAM admins, and user admins can access logs for account settings, user activities, and flow-level actions. For individual flows, process admins can view detailed logs of all activities related to their processes, including user actions and item-level changes.

nodes.label: Board
nodes.heading: Board
nodes.snippet: A board is a highly adaptable workflow that lets you keep track of information and manage work efficiently. You can create work items in a board and track their progress across different statuses.

nodes.label: Checkbox
nodes.heading: Checkbox
nodes.snippet: A checkbox field allows users to select multiple options from a list, dataset, or dataform in a form. It's a versatile field type that can be used in various scenarios.

nodes.label: Child table
nodes.heading: Child table
nodes.snippet: In the analytics module, child tables are automatically created for process and board. They are named in the ProcessName-ChildTableName or BoardName-ChildTableName format. For example, the Purchase Request process generates a child table named Purchase Request Catalog Items.

nodes.label: Collaborator
nodes.heading: Collaborator
nodes.snippet: Users with whom a connector has been shared. These users can use the connectors in different integrations.

nodes.label: Components
nodes.heading: Components
nodes.snippet: Components are the building blocks used to create applications in the Kissflow app builder. They include elements like containers, forms, tables, and reports which can be visually configured to define the app's functionality and user experience. Components allow users to design tailored solutions that address specific business needs through customization.

nodes.label: Connection
nodes.heading: Connection
nodes.snippet: An Authentication that enables Kissflow to interact with internal or external connectors using Access key, secret token, or OAuth authentication.

nodes.label: Connector
nodes.heading: Connector
nodes.snippet: Connectors can be services or applications that communicate with each other, comprising triggers, actions, and search actions based on their use cases.

nodes.label: Custom components
nodes.heading: Custom components
nodes.snippet: Custom components can be created in the app builder’s pages in addition to the the system components provided by Kissflow out of the box. Custom components allow developers to create high-quality app components and share them with others, shortening application development time.

nodes.label: Data Source
nodes.heading: Data Source
nodes.snippet: In Analytics, a data source refers to any form of data from which you can create dataviews and reports. This can be a flow table, child table, or a system table.

nodes.label: Dataset
nodes.heading: Dataset
nodes.snippet: A dataset is a collection of tabular data that can be used in various flows.

nodes.label: Dataview
nodes.heading: Dataview
nodes.snippet: A dataview is a tabular representation of data created from one or more tables or other dataviews in Kissflow Analytics. It is formed by performing joins or unions via Snowflake SQL queries in the query editor. You can create reports based on the dataview once it's generated.

nodes.label: Deadline
nodes.heading: Deadline
nodes.snippet: The time allocated for a step assignee to act on a process item.

nodes.label: Dimension
nodes.heading: Dimension
nodes.snippet: A dimension is an attribute used in analytics to categorize and provide context to the data shown on a chart. Dimensions are usually represented on the X-axis of a graphical chart. For example, in a sales chart, dimensions could include product categories, regions, or time periods.

nodes.label: Document template
nodes.heading: Document template
nodes.snippet: A document template allows you to automate the generation of process-related PDFs. It's particularly useful for creating standardized documents like offer letters or reports.

nodes.label: Editor
nodes.heading: Editor
nodes.snippet: Editor is a configuration interface where users select and configure trigger connectors, trigger events, action connectors, and action events.

nodes.label: Evaluate
nodes.heading: Evaluate
nodes.snippet: Evaluate is an AI-powered validation tool in the AI Formula builder that allows users to test formulas, whether manually entered or AI-generated, before saving them. It simulates the formula's execution to ensure the logic produces the expected output.

nodes.label: External data object
nodes.heading: External data object
nodes.snippet: An external data object is a flow in Kissflow Apps that allows app developers to securely access the data stored in external platforms, such as MySQL and MSSQL, without storing the data within Kissflow.

nodes.label: Failure analysis
nodes.heading: Failure analysis
nodes.snippet: Report generated by the Troubleshoot feature after analyzing an error. Failure analysis is divided into three sections: Impact, Root cause, and Suggested solutions.

nodes.label: Field ID
nodes.heading: Field ID
nodes.snippet: A field ID is a unique string automatically assigned to each field in the settings panel of the form builder.

nodes.label: Field
nodes.heading: Field
nodes.snippet: Fields are the building blocks of a form, used to capture user input within a workflow. You can access the available fields from the fields panel on the left side of the from builder.

nodes.label: Form
nodes.heading: Form
nodes.snippet: A form is an entity used to collect input from participants in your workflow. It is used in processes, boards, and dataforms in Kissflow. A form consists of three main components: section, field, and table.

nodes.label: Formula builder
nodes.heading: Formula builder
nodes.snippet:
Formula builder is a built-in tool in Kissflow forms that helps you create and manage expressions more efficiently. It offers an intuitive editor with smart auto-suggestions when using the dot operator (.) or calling functions, and a user-friendly drag-and-drop interface for adding fields and functions.

The builder also includes a search function to locate specific fields or functions quickly, and lets you evaluate expressions before saving them. The formula builder simplifies the entire process of writing and editing expressions.

For Enterprise plans, an AI formula builder is available as an add-on.

nodes.label: Geolocation
nodes.heading: Geolocation
nodes.snippet: A geolocation field in a form allows you to record users' location data with their consent. This enhances data collection capabilities, providing valuable location-based information for various use cases.

nodes.label: Goto
nodes.heading: Goto
nodes.snippet: A Goto is a feature in process workflow that allows you to jump between process steps based on specific conditions. It provides flexibility by enabling forward or backward movement within the workflow. For example, in a travel request process, if an employee doesn't need hotel accommodations, plane tickets, or a travel advance, you can use a Goto to skip those steps and move directly to final approval.

nodes.label: Grid
nodes.heading: Grid
nodes.snippet: Grid is a layout widget that groups related fields into a single container to optimize screen space in a form. It allows you to apply visibility conditions to the entire grid, enabling users to show or hide a collection of fields.

nodes.label: Guided autofill
nodes.heading: Guided autofill
nodes.snippet:
While smart attachments autofills process form fields, guided autofill allows you to further instruct how to autofill.
This can be done by adding autofill instructions to form fields. You can instruct AI to follow a format or template or even translate the autofill content to a desired language.

nodes.label: Impact
nodes.heading: Impact
nodes.snippet: Impact is a section within the Troubleshoot feature’s Failure analysis report that provides a non-technical summary or gist of the error that occurred during the integration.

nodes.label: Impersonation
nodes.heading: Impersonation
nodes.snippet: Impersonation allows service accounts to act on behalf of users or items within a Kissflow account. For example, it can make changes to user details, access all processes and integrations, or perform actions within specific flows.

nodes.label: Initiate
nodes.heading: Initiate
nodes.snippet: Initiate is a type of permission that allows users to initiate new items in process and boards and work on the items they create.

nodes.label: Integration
nodes.heading: Integration
nodes.snippet: An integration consists of a series of sequential steps that transfer and transform data between various Kissflow applications or third-party applications.

nodes.label: Item bar
nodes.heading: Item bar
nodes.snippet: An item bar is a colored horizontal bar that represents an item inside the timeline layout of a board.

nodes.label: Item
nodes.heading: Item
nodes.snippet: An item is a single instance/task in a process and board.

nodes.label: Kanban
nodes.heading: Kanban
nodes.snippet: Kanban is a layout type in a board's task management that visually organizes items or workflows into columns, representing different stages of progress, like To do, In progress, and Done.

nodes.label: Key
nodes.heading: Key
nodes.snippet: The first field, found in the first column, is the key. This field is unique and you cannot rename, delete, or change its field type.

nodes.label: Kissflow App Store
nodes.heading: Kissflow App Store
nodes.snippet: Kissflow App Store is a centralized repository that hosts a wide array of applications that cater to different business use cases. Unlike the process and board templates in Kissflow’s template gallery, the Kissflow App Store hosts pre-built applications that are designed and developed by Kissflow specialists with little to no coding effort. After installation, you can quickly deploy these applications for your end users. You can also inquire for new apps that address use cases that are not already available.

nodes.label: Kissflow Apps
nodes.heading: Kissflow Apps
nodes.snippet: Kissflow Apps empowers you to build fully functional applications using a low-code development approach that requires minimal to no coding. With visual tools and optional custom scripting, it enables the creation and deployment of complex, scalable applications with ease.

nodes.label: Kissflow Bot
nodes.heading: Kissflow Bot
nodes.snippet: Kissflow Bot is a chat-based assistant that connects your Kissflow workflows to chat apps like Slack, Google Chat, and Microsoft Teams. It helps you receive notifications and take quick actions like approving or viewing tasks directly from your chat app.

nodes.label: Least loaded assignment
nodes.heading: Least loaded assignment
nodes.snippet: Least loaded is an assignment method that you can choose under assignment preference for distributing tasks in a process. It assigns the task to the user with the fewest current assignments. This method aims to balance the workload among team members in real-time, preventing any one person from becoming overwhelmed.

nodes.label: Legend
nodes.heading: Legend
nodes.snippet: In the chart interface of Kissflow reports, the Legend appears as a key within the chart area. It provides labels that explain the data displayed in the chart. Users can hover over the chart area to view the data corresponding to each label in the Legend.

nodes.label: List
nodes.heading: List
nodes.snippet: List is a layout type in a board's task management that presents items in a simple, linear format, typically organized in rows, where each item is listed one after another.

nodes.label: Lookup layout
nodes.heading: Lookup layout
nodes.snippet: Lookup layout is a configuration setting that determines how lookup results are visually presented to the user within a lookup field in Forms. It controls the display format both during the selection and after an item has been chosen.

nodes.label: Manage
nodes.heading: Manage
nodes.snippet: Manage is a type of permission that allows users to initiate new items and manage existing ones. They will have access to all reports by default.

nodes.label: Matrix
nodes.heading: Matrix
nodes.snippet: Matrix is a layout type in a board's task management that organizes items in a grid, based on multiple criteria helping teams prioritize and categorize items.

nodes.label: Measure
nodes.heading: Measure
nodes.snippet: In Analytics, a measure is an attribute used to compare data on a chart. It is displayed on the Y-axis of a graphical chart. You can choose to display multiple measure values in a chart/pivot report. Measures are aggregated (Sum, Average, Count, Min., Max., Std Dev) to describe the data.

nodes.label: Model viewer
nodes.heading: Model viewer
nodes.snippet: Model viewer in Apps is similar to an entity-relationship (ER) model widely used in DBMS (Database Management System). Model viewer can help you create a real-time visualisation of all the process, board, data forms that are present in the app and the relationship between them.

nodes.label: Multi-select dropdown
nodes.heading: Multi-select dropdown
nodes.snippet: A multi-select dropdown field is a form element that allows users to choose multiple options from a list, dataset, or dataform of values. It's similar to a regular dropdown, but with the added functionality of selecting more than one item. This field type is particularly useful when you need to gather multiple selections from a predefined set of choices.

nodes.label: Navigation
nodes.heading: Navigation
nodes.snippet: The navigation bar and its associated menu options allow you to define your application's structure for different user role types. You can design your own navigation and assign it to different user roles.

nodes.label: Page
nodes.heading: Page
nodes.snippet: Pages help you create interactive interfaces within the app builder that allow you to collect, display, and manage data visually. You can create multiple pages, tailor them to specific user roles, and control access through permissions. Pages enable you to aggregate and present data in an organized, aesthetic format, offering a comprehensive overview on a single screen.

nodes.label: Parallel branch
nodes.heading: Parallel branch
nodes.snippet: Parallel branches in workflows enable concurrent tasks or conditional routing based on form data, ensuring flexibility and efficiency in process design.The workflow won't move forward until all parallel branches are completed. This feature is particularly useful for processes where multiple teams need to act simultaneously without depending on each other's tasks.

nodes.label: Portal Admin
nodes.heading: Portal Admin
nodes.snippet: A Portal Admin has access to the Portal settings option under Portal administration. Portal admins can control the portal's default settings that impact how data is formatted and displayed. They will also have access to the user management screen, audit log, and deployment history.

nodes.label: Process instance table
nodes.heading: Process instance table
nodes.snippet: The process instance table shows the current status of each item across all processes. This table provides insights into the progress and performance of processes within the account in Analytics.

nodes.label: Process
nodes.heading: Process
nodes.snippet: A process is a structured workflow for getting tasks done in a specific order. It involves initiating items within the process and moving them forward to subsequent steps in the workflow involving approve, reject, withdraw, and reassign functionalities.

nodes.label: Remote lookup
nodes.heading: Remote lookup
nodes.snippet: The remote lookup is a field in our form builder that allows you to retrieve data from external systems and integrate it into your forms.

nodes.label: Rerun
nodes.heading: Rerun
nodes.snippet: Option to rerun a previously completed or failed integration with the same configuration and data.

nodes.label: Root cause
nodes.heading: Root cause
nodes.snippet: A section within the Failure analysis report that identifies the specific technical reason for an integration's failure. It explains the actual origin of the error and why it occurred.

nodes.label: Round robin assignment
nodes.heading: Round robin assignment
nodes.snippet: Round robin is an assignment method that you can choose under assignment preference for distributing tasks in a process. It assigns tasks to users in a rotating sequence. For example, if you have three users, the first task goes to User A, the second to User B, the third to User C, then back to User A, and so on. This ensures an even distribution of work.

nodes.label: Run
nodes.heading: Run
nodes.snippet: The Run option allows you to preview the app before deploying it to the test or production environment. To successfully run the app in preview mode, the app must contain at least one page and a navigation.

nodes.label: Run history
nodes.heading: Run history
nodes.snippet: Record of all data pieces that have passed through your workflow, showing automated tasks and their execution status.

nodes.label: Run
nodes.heading: Run
nodes.snippet: Each piece of data that passes through the workflow system. Multiple runs can occur when an integration processes multiple items.

nodes.label: Scanner
nodes.heading: Scanner
nodes.snippet: Scanner is an advanced form field that allows users to capture data by scanning barcodes and Quick Response (QR) codes directly within a form. It is compatible with process forms, board forms, and dataforms.

nodes.label: Search action
nodes.heading: Search action
nodes.snippet: Functionality that finds and fetches specific data from applications based on defined requirements.

nodes.label: Section
nodes.heading: Section
nodes.snippet: A section is a group of related fields. Flow admins can create sections to organize fields that are logically connected when grouped together.

nodes.label: Smart attachment
nodes.heading: Smart attachment
nodes.snippet: A smart attachment is an AI-powered field type in our forms that automatically extracts data from uploaded documents like receipts or invoices. It uses intelligent document processing to map the extracted information to corresponding fields in the form, saving users time on manual data entry.

nodes.label: Solution analyzer
nodes.heading: Solution analyzer
nodes.snippet: The Analyze button in the process builder is an AI feature to assess whether the workflow aligns with the intended solution. It identifies critical issues and recommends improvements to enhance overall workflow quality.

nodes.label: Solution overview
nodes.heading: Solution overview
nodes.snippet: A solution overview gives you a complete, AI-generated breakdown of your process in a readable, structured format that highlights how different components come together to solve a business problem.

nodes.label: Solution
nodes.heading: Solution
nodes.snippet:
A solution in Kissflow refers to any business use case or an operational need solved using the platform’s no-code tools, such as a Process, Board, App, or Portal.

Solutions can function as standalone entities (for example, a single process or board) or as integrated systems, where multiple solutions and even external tools work together to deliver a unified outcome.

nodes.label: Step
nodes.heading: Step
nodes.snippet: A single configuration unit within an integration that consists of either a trigger or an action. Steps are executed sequentially to complete the workflow.

nodes.label: Style
nodes.heading: Style
nodes.snippet: Style is a configuration that defines the visual appearance of a form and its various building blocks. This includes color schemes for sections, tables, and fields, as well as layout preferences for help text.

nodes.label: Subitem
nodes.heading: Subitem
nodes.snippet: Subitems in Kissflow Boards are smaller, related tasks that are used to break down a parent item into manageable parts.

nodes.label: System tables
nodes.heading: System tables
nodes.snippet: In Analytics, system tables are automatically generated by the system to monitor status and activities across datasources like processes, boards, and datasets. Examples include Activity Instance, Process Instance, Board Workflow, User and Group tables.

nodes.label: Table
nodes.heading: Table
nodes.snippet: A table acts as a separate section, allowing flow admins to collect and manage data in a structured, tabular format.

nodes.label: Timeline
nodes.heading: Timeline
nodes.snippet: Timeline is a layout type in task management that visually represents items along a horizontal time axis, showing when each item begins and ends in a board.

nodes.label: Trigger
nodes.heading: Trigger
nodes.snippet: A trigger is the primary event that starts other actions in your workflow. It initiates the automated sequence of steps.

nodes.label: Troubleshoot
nodes.heading: Troubleshoot
nodes.snippet: Troubleshoot is an AI-powered feature that analyzes failed integrations to identify the error. It generates a comprehensive report including the impact, root cause, and prioritized solutions to help users resolve issues quickly.

nodes.label: Validation
nodes.heading: Validation
nodes.snippet: Validation is a field setting available on the settings panel of the form builder. It allows flow admins to add specific conditions to a field so that the form can be submitted only if those conditions are met.

nodes.label: View
nodes.heading: View
nodes.snippet: A view is a filtered subset of your dataset. You can create views to control access to certain parts of a dataset.

nodes.label: Reference
nodes.heading: Reference
nodes.snippet:
Reference

Glossary, FAQs, and quick lookups.

nodes.label: Ensuring smooth access to Kissflow
nodes.heading: Ensuring smooth access to Kissflow
nodes.snippet:
Ensuring smooth access to Kissflow

If you can't reach Kissflow, or parts of the product fail to load, the most common causes are a corporate firewall blocking required domains, or an ISP blocking the underlying Amazon or Google infrastructure Kissflow runs on.

nodes.label: Ensuring smooth access to Kissflow
nodes.heading: Symptom: Kissflow won't load, or loads partially
nodes.snippet:
Pages fail to load, styling or icons are missing, or features like chat support, file picker, or maps don't work — while other websites work fine.

Cause: Your organization's firewall is blocking one or more domains that Kissflow depends on.

Fix: Ask your IT team to whitelist the following domains.

Core application

- https://*.kissflow.com — the asterisk (*) whitelists any subdomain of kissflow.com, which covers your account's own subdomain (for example, https://yourcompany.kissflow.com). If your firewall doesn't support wildcard entries, whitelist your exact account subdomain instead.
- https://static-cdn1.kissflow.com — static assets.
- https://www.gstatic.com — subresources for Google-hosted embeds like reCAPTCHA and Google Maps.

Some accounts are served additional static-asset domains depending on your data region (for example, a second CDN host for US accounts, or a kissflow.eu variant for EU accounts). If assets still fail to load after whitelisting the domains above, check with your Kissflow contact for the exact CDN domain for your account's region.

Supporting services

| Domain | Used for |
| --- | --- |
| https://o300529.ingest.sentry.io | Error reporting |
| https://widget.intercom.io | In-app chat support |
| https://js.intercomcdn.com, https://api-iam.intercom.io, wss://nexus-websocket-a.intercom.io | In-app chat support (Intercom-hosted resources) |
| wss://*.firebaseio.com | Real-time updates |
| https://fonts.gstatic.com, https://fonts.googleapis.com | Fonts |
| https://maps.googleapis.com | Map fields |
| https://www.googleapis.com | Google Drive file picker |
| https://unpkg.com | Custom low-code components |
| https://www.recaptcha.net | Bot protection on forms |
| https://*.storage.googleapis.com | File and image storage |
| https://us.i.posthog.com | Product analytics |

If you previously whitelisted https://app.posthog.com for product analytics, update it to https://us.i.posthog.com — Kissflow now sends this traffic through PostHog's regional ingestion host.

If your account uses the Kissflow App Store, also check with your IT team whether appstore.us.kissflow.store and resource.us.kissflow.store need to be whitelisted separately, since these can be served from a different environment than your main account domain.

nodes.label: Ensuring smooth access to Kissflow
nodes.heading: Symptom: Kissflow is unreachable even with domains whitelisted
nodes.snippet:
Cause: Your ISP may be blocking the underlying Amazon or Google cloud infrastructure that Kissflow's servers run on, rather than blocking Kissflow's domains directly.

Fix: Try accessing Kissflow over a VPN. If Kissflow loads normally through the VPN, ask your ISP to unblock Amazon and Google server ranges, or continue using a VPN as a workaround.

nodes.label: Troubleshooting guide - common issues and solutions
nodes.heading: Troubleshooting guide - common issues and solutions
nodes.snippet:
Troubleshooting guide - common issues and solutions

This guide covers the issues Kissflow users run into most often, grouped by area, with the likely cause and how to fix each one. If a fix here doesn't resolve your issue, contact support via chat.

nodes.label: Troubleshooting guide - common issues and solutions
nodes.heading: General
nodes.snippet:
"Something went wrong. Please contact customer support."

This generic error can appear for several reasons — most often a recently added formula or a workflow misconfiguration. Start with the basics:

1. Clear your browser cache and reload the page.
2. Try the same action in an incognito/private window to rule out a stale cache or extension conflict.
3. If a specific form or workflow triggers it consistently, check any formulas or automations you recently added there.

If the error persists, contact our support team via chat with the steps that reproduce it.

Account approval is pending after sign-up

New sign-ups are added to the account as pending users, not active ones. Every Kissflow account has a set of user licenses, and an Account Admin controls who gets activated with one. If your account is stuck in the pending stage, you can't sign in until an Account Admin manually activates it.

Contact one of your Account Admins and ask them to approve your access request.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Validation error when submitting a workflow or project form

If a form won't submit and shows a validation error, check:

- Field values — make sure required fields are filled in and match the expected format.
- On-submit validation — a custom validation rule on the form may be rejecting the input.
- Sequence fields — misconfigured sequence number settings can also block submission.

If the form still won't submit after checking these, contact support.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Kissflow Homepage loads as an empty screen

This usually clears up with a hard refresh:

1. Clear your browser cache.
2. Reload the page.

If the homepage is still blank after that, contact support — it may be a browser extension or network issue on your end that's worth ruling out together.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

"Verification Link Expired" when signing in from the verification email

Account verification links are valid for 24 hours only. If you open the link after that window, you'll see the Verification Link Expired error instead of being signed in.

Ask your Account Admin to send you a new verification email.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Form changes don't show up when previewing a workflow

If you edit a workflow form and the preview still shows the old version, the preview is likely holding on to simulated data from an earlier run.

Click Reset data in the bottom-left corner of the preview form, then preview again.

Resetting clears only the preview's simulation data — it doesn't affect any live requests already submitted on the form.

media.alt: Pending user approval notice shown to a new sign-up
Validation error shown on a process form during submission
Empty Kissflow Homepage screen
Verification Link Expired error message

nodes.label: Troubleshooting guide - common issues and solutions
nodes.heading: Workflows
nodes.snippet:
Process

Send back and Reject buttons are missing from an approval step

If you're an approver and don't see the Send back or Reject buttons on a process form, the process administrator has hidden them for that step. Only the process administrator can restore them — contact them if you need those actions available.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

"There is no assignee for the next step" when initiating a request

This error means the next step in the process workflow doesn't resolve to a valid assignee. Check:

- The step's assignee configuration — is an assignee actually set for this step?
- Any formula used to determine the assignee.
- If the assignee comes from a lookup field, the lookup configuration itself — an empty or broken lookup result will leave the step without an assignee.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Changing a process form's title

As a process administrator, you can rename a process form's title:

1. Open the process and click Manage at the top right.
2. Go to Settings > General Settings.
3. Use Configure form title to set the new title.

Integration

An integration with multiple steps fails with an unknown error

Every run of an integration is logged, including error details for failed runs. Check the run history for the specific step and error that failed:

1. Click Explore > Managed by me > Integrations > Run history.
2. Select the integration you want to check.
3. Open the failed run to see which step failed and why.

New workflow fields don't appear in an integration step's configuration

Fields you add to a workflow after an integration step was already configured don't sync into that step automatically. Refresh the step to pick them up:

1. Open the integration editor for the integration you want to update.
2. Edit the trigger or action step that needs the new fields.
3. In the configuration form, click Refresh fields in the bottom-left corner.
4. Confirm the new fields now appear, then test the trigger or action again.

media.alt: Approval step in a process form without the Send back and Reject buttons
No assignee error shown when initiating a process request

nodes.label: Using board layouts
nodes.heading: Using board layouts
nodes.snippet:
Using board layouts

A board's layout makes it easy to locate and navigate between items. Three default tabs let you quickly switch between the most commonly used groups of items:

- All — the default tab that displays all the items in the board.
- Assigned to me — all the items assigned to you for action.
- Created by me — all the items you have created.

More system views — such as Critical, Due this week, Overdue, Unassigned, and Archived items — are available from the Views menu, and you can save your own with Save as a new view.

How your items are displayed depends on the layout you choose. There are four:

| Layout | Best for |
|---|---|
| List | Spreadsheet-style review and bulk updates |
| Kanban | Visualizing progress across statuses |
| Matrix | Slicing data across two dimensions |
| Timeline | Planning items against dates |

nodes.label: Using board layouts
nodes.heading: List
nodes.snippet:
The list layout shows your items in a tabular format: rows are items, and columns are fields from the board form. Click a row to open the item's form. This layout is ideal if you're used to managing tasks in spreadsheets — you can review a lot of information in one go.

Click the Show fields button to select the fields displayed as columns, and drag and drop columns to arrange them. The list layout also supports bulk actions: select multiple items to delete them or update most custom field values.

In the list layout you can:

- Search for items using keywords.
- Sort items by a field value in ascending or descending order.
- Export items as a CSV file.
- Perform bulk updates to items.
- Configure the fields displayed as columns.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: List layout of a board showing items as rows with fields as columns

nodes.label: Using board layouts
nodes.heading: Kanban
nodes.snippet:
The kanban layout is a visual system divided into columns that represent the statuses in your workflow. Each item appears as a card, placed in the column matching its status. Drag and drop cards across columns to change status; click a card to make any other change in the form.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Kanbans show an item's progress as cards move through columns to completion, and they make bottlenecks visible — for example, items piling up in a particular status.

Unlike the list layout, columns can't be configured because they represent statuses. However, you can choose the fields displayed on a card with the Show fields button. The ID, Title, Assignee, Status, Start date, Due date, and Priority fields are always shown and cannot be removed.

The Priority field is hidden if priority has been turned off in the board's settings.

In the kanban layout you can:

- Search for items using keywords.
- Sort items by a field value in ascending or descending order.
- Drag and drop cards to change status.
- Configure the fields displayed on a card.
- Perform bulk updates to items.
- Collapse columns you're not actively using.

media.alt: Kanban layout of a board with cards arranged in status columns

nodes.label: Using board layouts
nodes.heading: Matrix

media.alt: Matrix layout of a board with items plotted on a grid of rows and columns

nodes.label: Using board layouts
nodes.heading: Timeline
nodes.snippet:
The timeline layout gives a panoramic view of your board items against time, so you can see what's coming up next, plan items and subitems ahead, and meet deadlines comfortably.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

The left panel displays the ID, title, assignee, status, priority, start date, and due date of items and subitems by default — click the Show fields button to change what's shown. On the right, vertical columns represent time, and each item appears as a horizontal bar positioned between its start and due dates. Overdue items, completed items, and items without dates are shown in distinct colors.

You can change an item's dates from its form, drag a bar to a different time range, or grab the ends of a bar to adjust its start and due dates. Hover over a bar to see the item's details.

Timeline intervals and actions

Set the timeline's interval from the dropdown near the layout selector: Hour, Day, Week, Month, Quarter, or Year.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

- Day — the most granular day-to-day view, best for tracking daily progress.
- Week — optimal for planning the week ahead.
- Month — ideal for looking ahead to the month and identifying long-term goals.
- Quarter — for boards that track strategic initiatives or long-term goals.
- Year — for tracking items that change on a yearly basis.

Click Zoom in or Zoom out to scale the layout, and Today to snap to the current date. You can also:

- Search for items and subitems using keywords.
- Sort items in ascending or descending order.
- Export items as a CSV file.
- Save the current setup with Save as a new view.

Timeline layout in practice

Consider a board for managing blog production. Creating a new blog post involves a sequence of tasks: the content writer drafts the post, the copyeditor edits it, the manager approves it, and the social media manager promotes it. As board items on a timeline, the plan might look like this:

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Week 1

1. Research and outline of the blog post (Assignee: Content writer).
2. Blog writing (Assignee: Content writer).

Week 2

1. Blog editing (Assignee: Copyeditor).
2. Blog review and approval (Assignee: Manager).
3. Blog promotion (Assignee: Marketing team).

Looking at the timeline, the team can spot and avoid two common bottlenecks:

- Dependencies and overlaps — each step depends on the preceding one. If the writer can't finish the draft by the end of week 1, the team sees immediately that the downstream tasks need rescheduling.
- Resource allocation — tasks overcrowded within a timeframe indicate that a teammate may be overallocated. The team can reallocate tasks or extend the timelines of low-priority work to prevent burnout.

The same approach works for event planning, product development, construction, manufacturing, and agile project management — anywhere increased visibility, advance planning, and accountability matter.

media.alt: Timeline layout of a board showing items as horizontal bars against a calendar
Interval dropdown in the timeline layout showing the hour to year options
Timeline layout showing blog production tasks scheduled across two weeks with different assignees

nodes.label: Using board layouts
nodes.heading: Switching between layouts
nodes.snippet:
You can switch between layouts at any time without losing data. A few things to note:

- Sorting does not carry over. If you sort items in the list layout, that order won't apply to cards in the kanban layout.
- Field configuration does not carry over. The columns in the list layout and the fields on kanban cards are configured independently.
- Filters apply everywhere. A filter applied in one layout filters the same items in every other layout, displayed with whatever sorting that layout currently uses.

- Exporting items from a board

nodes.label: Notes, status logs, and activity logs in boards
nodes.heading: Notes, status logs, and activity logs in boards
nodes.snippet:
Notes, status logs, and activity logs in boards

This content now lives in Collaborating on items, which covers notes, the status and activity logs, and watching items in one place. Head there for the full walkthrough — a quick summary follows below.

nodes.label: Notes, status logs, and activity logs in boards
nodes.heading: Notes
nodes.snippet:
Notes offer a way to collaborate with stakeholders on an item. Add comments, log updates or action items, and react to notes added by others — visible to anyone collaborating on the item.

To add or update a note, open an item and click the Notes tab in the right navigation strip. Type / in the text area to open the formatting menu — attach a link, add a bulleted or numbered list, or apply Bold, Italic, Underline, Strikethrough, Code snippet, and Blockquote formatting.

You can also add emojis, attach files, images, and audio (played directly in Kissflow without downloading), annotate uploaded images, and tag people with @mentions — the tagged user gets a notification. Click the thumbs-up icon to acknowledge someone else's note, or copy a note's link to share it with others.

Once an item has at least 10 notes, a Summarize button appears at the top of the Notes tab to generate a quick AI summary of the recent notes.

nodes.label: Notes, status logs, and activity logs in boards
nodes.heading: Status log
nodes.snippet:
The status log captures every status change made to an item, along with the time spent in each status. Open an item, click the Item log tab in the right navigation strip, and select the Status log subtab.

Use the time spent in each status to spot items that are stuck — especially critical ones — so they get resolved without delays.

nodes.label: Notes, status logs, and activity logs in boards
nodes.heading: Activity log
nodes.snippet:
The Activity log subtab of the Item log tab lists key actions performed on an item — changes to system fields such as Priority, Assignee, Start date, and Due date (with the value before and after each change), plus when the item was created, closed, or cloned. Changes to custom fields are not shown here.

Each entry includes:

- Timestamp — the exact time the action was performed.
- Performed by — the member who performed the action.
- Activity — the type of action, with details of any changes it caused.

nodes.label: Notes, status logs, and activity logs in boards
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Collaborating on items
- Creating and managing items
- Watching an item in a board

nodes.label: Use cases for timeline layout
nodes.heading: Use cases for timeline layout
nodes.snippet:
Use cases for timeline layout

The timeline layout lays your board items out against dates, giving you a bird's-eye view of what's coming up and helping you plan ahead and meet deadlines. This article walks through one example — a content production workflow — to show the layout in action.

For the full reference on timeline intervals, actions, and how this layout compares to list, kanban, and matrix, see Using board layouts.

nodes.label: Use cases for timeline layout
nodes.heading: Content production and marketing campaigns
nodes.snippet:
The timeline layout works well for content and marketing teams tracking a piece of content through multiple handoffs. Consider a board for managing blog production, where a post moves through four steps: the content writer drafts it, the copyeditor edits it, the manager approves it, and the marketing team promotes it on social platforms.

Converting these tasks to board items and laying them out on a timeline produces a view like this:

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Week 1

1. Research and outline the blog post (Assignee: Content writer)
2. Write the blog post (Assignee: Content writer)

Week 2

1. Edit the blog post (Assignee: Copyeditor)
2. Review and approve the blog post (Assignee: Manager)
3. Promote the blog post (Assignee: Marketing team)

Statuses such as Yet to start, In progress, On hold, and Completed are just an example here — your board's statuses are fully configurable in its workflow. See Configuring statuses.

media.alt: Timeline layout showing blog production tasks scheduled across two weeks with different assignees

nodes.label: Use cases for timeline layout
nodes.heading: What the timeline view helps you spot
nodes.snippet:
Because each item bar is positioned between its start and due dates, laying tasks out on a timeline makes a couple of things easier to catch early:

- Sequencing risk — in this example, each step depends on the one before it. Seeing the bars laid end-to-end makes it obvious when a delay in one task — for example, the writer missing the end-of-week-1 deadline — pushes back everything downstream, so the team can adjust the schedule before it becomes a problem.

The timeline layout doesn't track or enforce dependencies between items — it places bars purely by start and due date. Any sequencing insight comes from reading the layout, not from a dependency feature.

- Crowded stretches of time — if several items land on the same assignee in overlapping windows, their bars visibly bunch up, which can be a cue to rebalance work or push out lower-priority items.

For a dedicated breakdown of who's carrying the most work, use the Workload distribution widget on a board dashboard rather than the timeline layout.

Once the content is approved, the marketing team takes over promotion — shown on the timeline as the final bar in the sequence.

nodes.label: Use cases for timeline layout
nodes.heading: Other use cases
nodes.snippet:
The same approach — converting tasks to items and laying them out against dates — works for any workflow with sequential handoffs and deadlines, such as:

- Event planning
- Product development
- Construction
- Manufacturing
- Agile project management

nodes.label: Using Kissflow
nodes.heading: Using Kissflow
nodes.snippet:
Using Kissflow

Work with apps, forms, boards, and processes.

nodes.label: Collaborating on items
nodes.heading: Collaborating on items
nodes.snippet:
Collaborating on items

Every item in a board carries its own conversation and history. Use notes to discuss work with stakeholders, the status and activity logs to trace what changed and when, and watching to stay updated on items that matter to you.

nodes.label: Collaborating on items
nodes.heading: Notes
nodes.snippet:
Notes offer an easy way to collaborate with different stakeholders on an item. Use them to add comments, log updates or action items, and react to notes added by others.

To add a note, open an item on your board, click the Notes tab in the right navigation strip, and type your comment in the text area.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Type / in a note to open the formatting menu:

- Attach — add a link, image, or video clip.
- List — add a bulleted or numbered list.
- Formatting — Bold, Italic, Underline, Strikethrough, Code snippet, and Blockquote.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

In addition to formatting, you can add emojis, attach files or images, and annotate uploaded images.

Tag someone by typing @ and their name — the tagged user gets a notification about the note. You can acknowledge others' notes by clicking the thumbs-up icon, and copy a note's link to share with people so they land right on that note.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

AI summary of notes

To get a quick summary of the recently added notes, click the Summarize button at the top of the Notes tab.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Notes tab of an item showing the conversation thread and the text area for adding a note
Slash menu in a note showing the attach, list, and formatting options
A note with an @mention and the option to copy the note's link
Animation of clicking the Summarize button to generate an AI summary of recent notes

nodes.label: Collaborating on items
nodes.heading: Status log

media.alt: Status log of an item showing the history of status changes with timestamps

nodes.label: Collaborating on items
nodes.heading: Activity log
nodes.snippet:
The Activity log subtab of the Item log tab lists the key actions performed on an item: changes to system fields such as Priority, Assignee, Start date, and Due date, along with when the item was created, closed, or cloned. For each field change, the value before and after the change is shown. Changes to custom fields are not displayed here.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Each entry shows:

- Timestamp — the exact time the action was performed.
- Performed by — the member who performed the action.
- Activity — the type of action, with details of any changes it caused.

media.alt: Activity log of an item listing field changes with before and after values

nodes.label: Collaborating on items
nodes.heading: Watching an item
nodes.snippet:
Watching an item keeps you updated on its progress as assignees and other collaborators modify it. Whether you can watch an item, and add others as watchers, depends on your permissions for that board or view — not simply whether you're an admin or a member.

Watch an item

1. On the board, open the item you want to watch.
2. Click the Watchers button on the top panel of the form.
3. Click Watch to receive updates about the item. Click Stop watching when you no longer want to follow it.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

In the kanban or matrix layout, you can also click the Start watching / Stop watching button directly on an item card.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Add others as watchers

1. Open the item you want to add watchers to.
2. Click the Watchers button on the top panel of the form.
3. Click Add watchers and select members from the list. They are added as watchers as soon as you select them.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

An item can have only one assignee, but up to 49 watchers.

Becoming a watcher is a great way for multiple stakeholders to stay up to date on an item's progress, even if it isn't assigned to them.

Remove watchers

Removing someone from the watchers list stops them from receiving updates about the item.

1. Open the item.
2. Click the Watchers button on the top panel of the form.
3. Under Watchers, hover over a user's name and click the Remove button.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

View your watchlist

On your board, click the Watchlist button to see the items you are currently watching across statuses. Click it again to return to the original view.

A board admin can enable or disable the Watchers setting from the board's optional settings. When enabled, admins and members can watch items, and watchers receive notifications on item updates.

media.alt: Animation of opening an item, clicking the Watchers button, and starting to watch it
Item card in the kanban layout with the watch button visible
Add watchers panel with a list of board members to select from
Watchers list with the remove button shown on hover next to a user's name

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: Creating and managing items
nodes.snippet:
Creating and managing items

Once a board and its form are set up for you, you can start creating items to track your work. Every item moves through the statuses configured in the board's workflow, starting in the first status of the Not started category.

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: Add a new item
nodes.snippet:
1. Click the New item button at the top right of the board.

The button name changes based on the item type configured for the board — for example, New ticket on a ticketing board.

2. In the form that opens, fill in the details:
- Assignee — the member responsible for the item.
- Priority — choose Low, Medium, High, or Critical.
- Start date — when work on the item should begin.
- Due date — when the item should be closed.
- Requester — the user who requested the item.
- Any other fields your board's form captures.
3. Click Submit.

The Priority field is hidden if priority has been turned off in the board's settings.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

New items appear in your board's current layout. Click the All tab to see every item in the board, or switch between layouts to view items in different ways.

media.alt: Board with the New item button at the top right and the list of items below

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: Create multiple items by pasting
nodes.snippet:
You can paste a list of text to create multiple items at once. Copy a list (for example, from a notepad), go to any board layout, and paste it (Ctrl/Cmd + V). In the Paste and create items dialog, select a field to map the title and click Create. The items are created in the first status of the Not started category.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

You can paste up to 20 items at a time.

For larger batches, import items from a CSV file.

media.alt: Animation of pasting a copied list into a board and mapping the title field to create multiple items

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: Update an item

media.alt: Item card with the check mark used to mark the item as completed
Board layout showing completed items struck through after enabling the strikethrough option
Item details showing the Priority dropdown with Critical, High, Medium, and Low options

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: Update items in bulk
nodes.snippet:
You can update several items at once from the list, kanban, or matrix layout. Select the items, then use the action bar to update:

- Assignee
- Status
- Priority
- Start and due dates — sets the item’s start and due dates.

You can also archive, unarchive, or delete the selected items from the same action bar, and update other form fields through the More options button.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: Animation of selecting multiple items in the list layout and updating them with bulk actions

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: Archive an item
nodes.snippet:
Archiving hides items from your board view without permanently deleting them. It's useful when an item is no longer needed for current work but may need to be restored later.

To archive an item, click the More options button on the item and select Archive. An archived item becomes read-only — to edit it again, Unarchive it first.

When you archive a connected item, its connections with its source and dependent items are removed.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

Whether you can archive or unarchive an item depends on your board role:

| Board role | Archive and unarchive items |
| ----------------- | --------------------------- |
| Admin | All items |
| Member | Only items they created |
| Initiator | Only items they created |
| Viewer | Not available |
| Restricted member | Not available |

To see archived items, go to Views at the top of the board and select Archived items.

The Archived items view is available with every board role, but the items shown in it follow your role's item visibility — for example, restricted members see only items assigned to them.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: More options menu on an item showing the Archive action
Views menu open with the Archived items view selected

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: Delete an item
nodes.snippet: Select an item in the board and click Delete. To delete several items at once, select them and click Delete. You can also open an item and click the More options button > Delete.

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: Clone an item
nodes.snippet:
Cloning quickly creates a duplicate that contains all the information captured in the original. The clone is an independent item and can be tracked and progressed separately.

1. Open an item on your board.
2. Click the More options button > Clone.

{/* TODO: verify screenshot matches current product UI */}

media.alt: More options menu in the item form showing the Clone action

nodes.label: Creating and managing items
nodes.heading: What's next
nodes.snippet:
- Adding advanced filters using AI (Enterprise)
